game_explanation:
This is a 29-round repeated public-goods game with four players. Each round each player effectively chooses a contribution from 0–20 (the amount left in the shared pot), which is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can send costly rewards: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the target 2 coins. Rewards are identifiable (players know who rewarded them), but summary information only shows each player's own net payoff and reward activity.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Cooperation is individually costly (you forgo private coins) but benefits the group. Multiplication (1.8) divided by 4 gives a marginal per-player return of 0.45 per coin contributed — less than 1 — so purely self-interested play favors withdrawing the full 20 each round. Social preferences, reciprocity, and reputation concerns (even with anonymous simultaneous play, the record across rounds matters) can sustain positive contributions.
- Rewarding is costly relative to its benefit (3 cost for a 2 benefit), so rewards will be used sparingly and typically only to reinforce high contributors or reciprocate conspicuous cooperation. Because rewards are identifiable, they're useful for targeted reciprocity and for sustaining cooperation among like-minded players. Expect most rewards to be single-unit, infrequent, and focused on the consistently cooperative players.
- Over time, absent strong, consistent rewarding or coordination, contributions tend to drift downward (end-game decline is especially likely as the 29th round approaches). Players with higher patience or stronger pro-social priors (here CHICKEN and DUCK) are more likely to contribute at higher levels and to reward, while more opportunistic players (inferred from the profiles) will vary and sometimes free-ride.
- The observed pattern likely: CHICKEN and DUCK start relatively cooperative and reward occasionally; SLOTH is moderately cooperative and at times rewards for reciprocity; GORILLA fluctuates more, occasionally free-riding, then trying to repair cooperation. Rewards are infrequent, typically 1 unit, and targeted at CHICKEN or DUCK when they contribute noticeably more than others. Contributions slowly decline over the session with some short-lived recoveries when a player increases their contribution.

- DUCK is patient and moderately pro-social; likely to contribute and sometimes punish a clear defection to maintain norms.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) and 23 rounds. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (cooperate: contribute 20) or withdraws them for private use (defect: contribute 0). The group pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so cooperators forgo the private 20 but receive a share of the multiplied pot; defectors keep 20 and also get the share. Cooperators are therefore vulnerable to exploitation by defectors. Players can punish others after payoffs are revealed: each punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and reduces the target’s payoff by 2 coins. Punishment is anonymous (players see how much was spent and how much was deducted overall, but not who targeted whom).

Given the player personas:
- GORILLA is very patient and norm-oriented (high patience, high mental accounting), likely to cooperate consistently and to punish defectors as an enforcement strategy.
- SLOTH is moderately patient and norm-conscious; they are likely to cooperate most rounds and will sometimes punish free-riders.
- CHICKEN is moderately inclined to cooperate but conditional; will punish sometimes to enforce norms.
- DUCK is gain-seeking and low on mental accounting; they are the likeliest to free-ride (defect) repeatedly unless punished enough to change behavior.

Likely dynamics over 23 rounds:
- Early rounds: a cooperative norm is attempted (SLOTH, GORILLA, CHICKEN cooperate; DUCK defects to test). Cooperators will use small, targeted punishments to discourage DUCK.
- Middle rounds: cycles of DUCK occasionally defecting and being punished; CHICKEN sometimes tests by defecting as well and gets corrected. Punishment is used sparingly because it is costly but is applied enough that DUCK alternates between exploiting and cooperating.
- Late rounds: with repeated punishment and some rounds of all cooperation, the group largely returns to cooperation. In the final rounds some players (especially DUCK) may defect again to grab immediate gains; GORILLA (norm-enforcer) may still punish even in the final round.

I simulate this trajectory, keeping chat sparse and punishment decisions limited but targeted.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 29 rounds, four players, and an option to reward others after seeing contributions. The private incentive is to withhold contribution (keep coins privately), while the group incentive is to contribute (since contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and shared). Because payoff from the public pot is 0.45 per coin contributed (1.8/4), each coin contributes more to total group payoff than it returns to the contributor individually — but the contributor bears the full private cost (losing the 1 private coin) and only recoups 0.45, so the dominant private incentive is to under-contribute.

Uncertainties: players don't see others' choices before deciding, so expectations about peers drive behavior. Over repeated rounds, conditional cooperators tend to match the group's trend; reward allows reputational/reciprocal signaling — but rewarding is costly (3 to giver, 2 to recipient), so it is used sparingly. Given the personas:
- GORILLA is patient and risk-tolerant and likely to be a steady high contributor and occasional rewarder to sustain cooperation.
- DUCK is also cooperative and consistent; likely to contribute high and reciprocate rewards selectively.
- SLOTH is moderately cooperative but more variable and less inclined to give rewards.
- CHICKEN is more loss-sensitive and tends to contribute less; could sometimes free-ride, causing others to adjust.

I predict moderate-to-high cooperation early (but below the social optimum), some decline if low contributors appear, and occasional small rewards by GORILLA and DUCK aimed at sustaining cooperation. Rewards will be sparse and usually single-unit gifts.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 23 rounds, four players, and binary contribution choices (either contribute the whole 20 coins or withdraw all 20). With a multiplier of 2.5 and equal split among 4 players, each contributed 20 produces 12.5 coins of benefit to every player. That means a single player's private incentive is to withdraw (get 20 privately) while everyone else cooperates — defection gives a higher immediate payoff. However, punishment (cost 1 to sender, -2 to target) can deter free-riding. Because players are anonymous for punishment receipts and the horizon is finite (23 rounds), I expect:

- Early rounds: testing behavior. Some players (CHICKEN given the persona) are more likely to defect early to exploit cooperators; others (SLOTH, GORILLA) are more inclined to cooperate and occasionally punish defectors to enforce norms.
- Middle rounds: a norm of cooperation develops after a few punishments; most rounds become full cooperation with occasional single defections that draw targeted, small punishments.
- Late rounds (end-game): as the horizon approaches, punishment effectiveness declines. Defections become more common in the final rounds and punishments taper off.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful: calls to cooperate, warnings, short reactions to punishments.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript following those tendencies. Payoff math used each round:
- Let k = number of contributors (players who left 20 in the pot). Each player receives 12.5*k from the public pot.
- A cooperator's pre-punishment payoff = 12.5*k; a defector's pre-punishment payoff = 20 + 12.5*k.
- Final net payoff = pre-punishment payoff - (units_sent) - 2*(units_received).
For readability, each round's summary lists (net payoff, punishment sent, total deduction from punishments received).

game_explanation:
This PGG creates a tension between private extraction (withdraw) and collective investment (contribution). With 20 coins initially in the shared pot each round, a player who withdraws more keeps immediate money but lowers the group benefit because the pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split evenly among the four players. That makes conditional cooperation likely: cooperative types will contribute a lot hoping others reciprocate; selfish types will free-ride. Because the multiplier (2.1) yields a per-player return of 0.525 coins per coin contributed, each coin contributed benefits the group more than the individual, so group-optimal behavior is high contribution, but individually optimal behavior is to withdraw everything.

Punishments and rewards add social enforcement possibilities. Punishment is costly to the punisher (1 coin) but hurts the target more (−3), making punishment an effective, though expensive, deterrent. Reward is cheap for the recipient (sender pays 1, recipient gains 1), and so is often used sparsely to reinforce high contributors. Real games tend to have sparse punishment and reward: players avoid punishing every deviation because of the cost, but will punish clear, repeated free-riding; rewards are used to reinforce cooperation occasionally.

Using the personas:
- SLOTH: patient and tolerant of risk, likely a steady high contributor and willing to reward/punish modestly to sustain cooperation.
- GORILLA: mixed tendencies — moderate contributions, somewhat responsive to losses; may punish or reward sparingly.
- DUCK: low patience and strong mental accounting suggest a tendency to free-ride early; likely to contribute low amounts but may increase if punished/rewarded.
- CHICKEN: patient and pro-cooperation, likely to contribute high amounts and to use occasional rewards; may punish clear free-riders.

Uncertainties: exact contribution magnitudes and who will bear punishment cost are noisy. Punishment is used sparingly and typically only after repeated free-riding. Toward the end (round 10) expect some decline in contributions (endgame effect) because future retaliation/reward is less relevant.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and six rounds. Each round every player effectively chooses either to leave the full 20 coins in the public pot (contribute 20) or to withdraw them for private use (contribute 0). The total public contribution is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so an individual who defects (withdraws 20) gains an immediate private advantage versus a cooperator, but if many cooperate everyone gets a bigger shared return. Because punishment and reward are available, early defectors can be disciplined (punishment is costly to both sender and receiver) and cooperators can be encouraged (reward benefits recipients). Observed tendencies suggest SLOTH and DUCK are relatively more likely to cooperate; GORILLA and CHICKEN are more likely to test or defect, especially near the last round. Expect some early exploitation, a few targeted punishments and a few sparse rewards to stabilize cooperation mid-game, and some unraveling (more defection) in round 6 because the game ends then. Chat will be sparse and purposeful; punishment/reward will be used sparingly to signal norms rather than as continuous tit-for-tat.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game gives each player an initial 20-coin pot and asks them how much to withdraw (private) vs leave as a contribution. Contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so individual incentives favor withdrawing (private gain) while group payoff is maximized by high contributions. With punishment (costly to the sender, larger cost to the target) and rewards (cost 1 to sender, benefit 1 to receiver), players can respond to others' contributions. Typical dynamics:
- Early rounds often show conditional cooperation: many players contribute moderately-high amounts but punish or withhold rewards from obvious low contributors.
- Punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher; small, credible punishments can correct free-riding and raise contributions afterward.
- Rewards are rarer but can reinforce cooperative behavior.
- As the end (round 10) approaches, contributions often drift downward (end-game effect) because future retaliation is less relevant.
Uncertainties: exact magnitudes depend on each persona's tendency to cooperate, patience, and mental accounting. Expect SLOTH and CHICKEN to be steady cooperators, GORILLA to be somewhat strategic (occasional experiments), and DUCK to be the most variable (occasional low contributions followed by corrections when punished or rewarded).

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods dilemma with four players and 29 rounds. Each coin a player keeps yields a direct private payoff of 1 (by withdrawing it); each coin contributed yields a total group return of 1.8 (so 0.45 to each player). That means the individual incentive is to withdraw everything (private return > individual share of public return), while the group-level optimum is to contribute as much as possible. Because contributions are private and players know the horizon (29 rounds), expect: an initial phase where cooperative contributions are plausible (norm-seeking, testing), sparse positive rewards aimed at sustaining cooperation (rewards are costly: giver pays 3 per unit to give 2 to someone else), occasional free-riding (especially from risk-loving/impulsive profiles), and some endgame decline in contributions. Players' visible priors matter: SLOTH is patient and inclined to sustain cooperation; GORILLA is middling and responsive to social cues; DUCK is risk-seeking and tends to defect or experiment; CHICKEN is opportunistic/variable and may switch strategies. Rewards will be infrequent, small (1 unit typical), targeted at high contributors or used as gestures, and mainly sent by the more cooperative players (SLOTH and GORILLA), with occasional gestures by CHICKEN. DUCK rarely rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a 23-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the public pot (contribution = 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribution = 0). The public pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so a contributor's round payoff is (2.5 * total contributions)/4, while a withdrawer's payoff is 20 plus that same share. The social optimum is everyone contributing (each gets 50), but individual incentive is to withdraw (you keep 20 plus still get the shared return), so free-riding is tempting.

Given the personas:
- GORILLA (high patience/risk-taking) tends to sustain cooperation early and push norms.
- DUCK and CHICKEN (high patience and mental-accounting) are inclined to cooperate and enforce norms, though CHICKEN may punish defectors.
- SLOTH (medium tendencies) is less stable and more likely to test or defect intermittently.

I predict the game will begin with cooperation, see occasional single-player defections (mostly by SLOTH), some targeted punishment or reciprocation, and intermittent recoveries back to cooperation. Towards the end, as the horizon closes, defections increase and the final round collapses to full withdrawal (everyone defects). Chat will be sparse and functional: short promises, calls to cooperate, occasional accusations or apologies when payoffs reveal defectors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) playing 29 rounds. Each round players decide how many of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) — the remainder is private. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so there is a social incentive to contribute (everyone benefits from others' contributions) but an individual incentive to free-ride (keep private coins). Because contributions are revealed before sanctions, players can target punishment or reward at specific behaviors. Punishments are costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) and very costly to the target (-4 per unit); rewards are costly to the giver (3 per unit) and moderately beneficial to the receiver (+2 per unit). Over many rounds, expect:
- Early rounds: exploration and moderate cooperation driven by prosocial players (DUCK, CHICKEN) and conditional contributors (SLOTH). GORILLA's lower cooperative tendency produces occasional low contributions.
- Middle rounds: a mixture of rewards to sustain cooperation and sparse punishments aimed at persistent low contributors; punishment is used sparingly because it is costly.
- Late rounds: gradual erosion of contributions as the end approaches, with occasional last-minute sanctions or rewards but less punishment overall as retaliation and final-round incentives loom.
Uncertainty remains about how often players will punish or reward; real behavior typically shows relatively few, modest sanctions/rewards. The transcript below simulates one plausible unfolding consistent with these tendencies.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives (take private coins) against a group benefit (contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split). The socially optimal move is to contribute more, because each coin contributed yields 2.1/4 = 0.525 coins back to each player — less than 1 to the contributor individually, but beneficial to the group overall. Players cannot communicate, so they rely on observed past contributions and the scarce punishment/reward signals to infer motives. Punishment (cheap to the punisher, costly to the target: cost 1 to sender, -3 to target) and rewards (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target) are available but typically used sparingly; punishments can deter clear free-riding, rewards can sustain cooperation.

Given the four personas:
- GORILLA and CHICKEN lean toward higher, more consistent contributions.
- SLOTH is moderate and responsive to social feedback.
- DUCK is somewhat risk-seeking and more likely to under-contribute early but may adjust after receiving sanctions.

I predict: moderate-to-high contributions early, a few targeted punishments or small rewards to correct obvious free-riding, brief increases in cooperation after sanctions/rewards, and a gradual overall decline toward the end as end-game incentives to free-ride grow. Punishment and reward events will be sparse and targeted rather than widespread.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round each player either leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (cooperate = contribute 20) or withdraws them for private use (defect = contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally. Players can punish others after seeing contributions: each punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the target. Punishments are visible as amounts sent and amounts deducted, but players cannot see who punished whom.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- The one-shot incentive favors defection (taking 20 privately while still getting a share of others' contributions). A single defector among cooperators typically earns noticeably more that round.
- Because punishment is costly, players tend to punish sparingly, mostly directed at clear defectors to discourage free-riding. Punishment levels are often too small to fully eliminate the defector's advantage in a single round, but repeated or coordinated punishment can reduce the attractiveness of defection.
- Players with high mental accounting and patience (SLOTH, DUCK) are more inclined to cooperate initially and enforce norms. GORILLA is somewhat similar but more variable. CHICKEN is more prone to risk/loss-driven behavior and is more likely to defect intermittently — provoking punishment.
- Messages are typically sparse and used to signal intentions (threaten punishment or promise cooperation). Because the game is finite (23 rounds), cooperation tends to be maintained for many middle rounds but collapses near the end (final-round defection), especially once the future punishment threat weakens.
- Uncertainties include whether punitive actions will be coordinated enough to change CHICKEN's strategy, and whether some players will try opportunistic defections mid-game (e.g., GORILLA or DUCK) once they observe punishment fatigue.

Given these priors, I predict a pattern where most rounds early and mid-game have majority cooperation with CHICKEN defecting occasionally and being punished; a few opportunistic defections by others occur; cooperation holds for a long middle block; toward the final rounds most players switch to defection.

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game has a simple but strong strategic tension. Each round a player either keeps the full 20 (defect) or leaves the full 20 in the public pot (cooperate). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so the private incentive is to withdraw (defect) every round while group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes. Because punishment and reward are available after contributions, cooperation can be sustained if players are willing to pay to punish free-riders or reward cooperators — but both actions are costly to the sender and anonymous to recipients, so they are used sparingly in practice.

Given the provided personas, I expect DUCK and CHICKEN to be comparatively more willing to contribute early (DUCK especially patient; CHICKEN strongly norm-focused), while SLOTH and GORILLA are more likely to take advantage and withdraw early. CHICKEN is the most likely to punish free riders initially; DUCK may use limited rewards to encourage cooperators. Punishment will be rare and modest because it is costly to send and, in this game, often fails to fully eliminate the short-term advantage of defecting. Over rounds we should see partial cooperation early, some targeted punishment, a mid-game drift toward defection as punishers get exhausted or realize they are paying too much, a brief attempt to rebuild cooperation, and then the final-round unraveling (many players defecting when future enforcement is impossible).

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game pits private incentives (keep 20 coins) against collective benefit (contribute 20 coins to the pot). A contributed 20 adds 1.6*20 = 32 to the total pot, but because the pot is split equally among 4 players, each player gains 8 from one person's contribution. So contributing costs you 20 privately while returning only 8 to you personally — private incentive favors withdrawing (defecting), while the group benefits if many contribute. The reward stage can shift incentives: each reward unit costs the sender 3 and gives the recipient 4, so senders can transfer resources to reward cooperators (or reward other motives) at a net loss to themselves.

Uncertainties and dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' intentions. Some players will try cooperation and others will test by free-riding. Chat and early rewards can seed norms.
- Rewarding is anonymous to targets, but players learn how much each avatar spent on rewards and how much they received. Observing peers pay to support cooperators tends to strengthen cooperation over rounds.
- Players with higher mental-accounting and strong risk/gain preferences may be more likely to prioritize private payoff early (free-ride). Patient or pro-social-leaning players are more likely to contribute early.
- End-game (round 6) often sees more defection because future retaliation or reciprocation is impossible; some players who cooperated earlier may defect in the last round.

I use the provided personas as priors: CHICKEN and DUCK lean cooperative/strategic and will often contribute and use selective rewards; GORILLA is cautious but responsive to incentives and may switch to cooperate after seeing reciprocation; SLOTH tends toward private-payoff focus and is slower to join cooperative norms.

This is a 23-round repeated public goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player either withdraws all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leaves all 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The group pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally — so if k players contribute, everyone receives 12.5·k from the pot; contributors get 12.5·k, defectors get 20 + 12.5·k. Individually, defecting strictly dominates contributing within a single round, but repeated interaction and visible round-by-round payoffs allow conditional cooperation, promises, and punishments to shape behavior.

Likely dynamics given the player personas:
- GORILLA (high patience) and CHICKEN (high mental accounting, patient) serve as cooperation anchors: they are relatively likely to propose and sustain contributions early on.
- SLOTH (medium patience, mixed tendencies) is conditional — will often cooperate if others do but will punish persistent free-riding.
- DUCK (very high loss-lottery choice, low mental-accounting) is the most likely to free-ride repeatedly, though may occasionally contribute to reset trust.

Because all players see each other's round payoffs, they can infer who defected (payoffs differ by exactly 20 between defectors and contributors given k). Expect sparse chat: short proposals, confirmations, complaints, and occasional apologies. Cooperation will appear intermittently for early/mid rounds, with occasional full-group cooperation spurts; toward the final rounds, incentives to defect dominate and cooperation will unravel (likely mutual defection by the final rounds).

game_explanation:
This three-player public goods game has the classic tension between private incentives and collective benefit. Each coin a player contributes yields 1.9 coins to the public pot which are then split equally among the three players, so each coin contributed returns 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 to the contributor. That means every coin a player gives up reduces their private return by about 0.367 on net, so the dominant (selfish) incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes more. Because the game lasts a known finite number of rounds (6), we expect some of the usual patterns: early rounds may show conditional cooperation (players match perceived norms/pledges), experimentation and punishment can occur if someone defects, and contributions often decline toward the final round (end-game effect).

Messaging can help coordinate cooperation (promises or conditional strategies), but messages are cheap and non-binding, so their credibility depends on players’ types and history. Using the provided personas: DUCK is patient and strongly mentally-accounting (likely to favor higher contributions and conditional cooperation); SLOTH is impatient and strongly mental-accounting but tends toward conservative/low contributions; GORILLA is intermediate, somewhat risk-tolerant and flexible — likely to experiment and respond to social cues. So I predict: DUCK will start high and try to sustain cooperation; SLOTH will contribute low amounts; GORILLA will sit in the middle initially and may adjust (testing or increasing if others cooperate, or reducing if others defect). Overall contributions will show some early cooperation driven by DUCK, occasional strategic shifts by GORILLA, modest increases if coordination messages succeed, and a typical drop near the final round.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (each coin kept gives 1 to the player) against a group return (each contributed coin yields 2.1 to the pot, or 0.525 to each of 4 players). The individual incentive is to withdraw (free-ride) because each coin a player keeps gives them 1 versus only 0.525 returned from contributing; collectively everyone would be better off if players contributed more. Because the game lasts 10 rounds and players can punish (cost 1, -3 to target) or reward (cost 1, +1 to target) after seeing contributions, the main enforcement mechanisms are social: light punishments of persistent free-riders and occasional rewards for high contributors. Punishment is costly to the sender and often used sparingly; rewards are also used sparingly. Near the end of the 10 rounds players tend to reduce enforcement (the “endgame” effect), so cooperation often drifts down.

Using the provided personas as priors: SLOTH and GORILLA are relatively patient and likely to contribute at above-average levels early on; DUCK and CHICKEN have stronger "mental accounting" tendencies and therefore are more likely to treat the private withdrawal as sticky (contribute less), though CHICKEN shows a stronger tendency to gamble/loss-seeking and may vary. Expect early cooperation from SLOTH and GORILLA, mixed contributions from DUCK, and lower contributions from CHICKEN. The group will show occasional punishments aimed at low contributors; these punishments are infrequent and small (one-unit punishments). Rewards are rarer but may be used once to signal appreciation for a high contributor. Over rounds cooperation drifts toward moderate contributions with occasional short retaliatory punishments; in the final round contributions drop further and enforcement largely subsides.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and a 23-round horizon. Each round each player decides whether to contribute the entire 20-coin endowment to the group pot (contribute = 20) or withdraw it for private use (contribute = 0). The total contributed is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally among all four players. The marginal per-coin private return (if you withdraw) exceeds the per-coin group return (0.625 per coin to each player), so there's a strong individual incentive to withdraw (free-ride) even though everyone would be better off if many or all contributed.

Key dynamics and uncertainties likely to appear:
- Early rounds: some players (SLOTH, GORILLA) have profiles that make them more inclined to cooperate or try to coordinate. Others (DUCK, CHICKEN) are less tied to mental accounting and are more likely to withdraw. Because contributions are observable only after decisions are made (and only round payoffs are shown), trust and cheap talk (messages) will shape behaviour. Expect occasional pledges to contribute; these may or may not be honored.
- Conditional cooperation: cooperative players will likely reduce contributions after seeing persistent defections. Some players will attempt to re-start cooperation with promises; cheap talk may occasionally succeed but is fragile.
- Over time: given the finite horizon and visible short-term payoffs favoring withdrawers, cooperation tends to decline, with intermittent attempts to revive it. There may be a few isolated successful coordinated contributions (including possibly a full-group success if enough players commit), but most rounds will skew toward low or zero contributions.
- Messaging: sparse but strategic — a few pre-round pledges and occasional reactive messages after payoffs. Expect GORILLA (high patience, pro-cooperation) to advocate and SLOTH to start cooperative but become conditional; DUCK and CHICKEN will often prioritize private payoffs.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 23. Each round begins with an explanation of the likely immediate incentives and expectations, followed by any chat, the contribution vector (in avatar order), and the round summary showing each player's net payoff for the round.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives against group welfare. Each player starts with 20 coins in a shared pot and chooses how many coins to withdraw for private use; the remainder becomes their contribution. Because contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and then split equally among 4 players, each coin contributed returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 coins to the contributor directly. That means privately keeping a coin yields 1.00 to the individual while contributing yields 0.45 back to that same individual — so the individually optimal move is to withdraw everything (0 contribution), while the group optimum is to contribute everything. This classic tension creates scope for free-riding.

Given the ability to punish and reward after seeing contributions, players can try to sustain cooperation. Punishment is costly: each punishment unit costs 3 coins to the sender and deducts 4 coins from the target. Rewarding also costs 3 per unit and gives the recipient +2 per unit. Because players can identify who punished or rewarded them, sanctions can be targeted and thus credible. However, sanctions are costly and, in real experiments, are used sparingly.

Based on the provided personas, I expect:
- SLOTH (patient, strong mental accounting) will lean toward steady, relatively high contributions and occasional targeted punishment of clear free-riders — but will punish infrequently.
- GORILLA (impatient, low mental accounting) will be more likely to under-contribute at times (free-ride), especially early or when punishments look weak.
- DUCK (mixed patience, risk-seeking) will vary; sometimes contributing well, sometimes opportunistically.
- CHICKEN (impatient but norm-sensitive, high mental accounting) will contribute at a moderately high level and act as the main enforcer with occasional punishments and occasional rewards to stabilize cooperation.

I predict an initial round of moderate cooperation, occasional defections by GORILLA, and a pattern where CHICKEN (and sometimes SLOTH) use sparse punishments to restrain persistent free-riding. Rewards will be rare but used to reinforce steady contributors. Over 29 rounds this should yield a mix of contributions with no full collapse to zero contributions, but also no perfect efficiency — a stable middle ground maintained by occasional sanctions and acknowledgements.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 3 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group sum is doubled and split equally among the three players, so the private-best response in a one-shot setting is to contribute 0 (free ride), but if others contribute, contributing increases everyone’s return. Over repeated rounds players often start with some cooperation (testing or trusting), observe payoffs, and then adjust: cooperators tend to reduce contributions if they see exploitation; conditional cooperators may reciprocate others' past generosity; persistent free-riders often exploit early cooperation and maintain low contributions. Endgame effects usually push contributions down toward 0 in the final rounds.

Using the provided personas as priors:
- SLOTH: patient and with strong mental accounting — likely to start moderately cooperative and reduce contributions slowly when exploited.
- GORILLA: very risk-taking and aggressive in lottery-like choices — likely to favor freeriding early and may contribute only small amounts; may occasionally experiment but tends to seek the highest immediate payoff.
- DUCK: very patient but risk-averse in gain-like settings — inclined toward cooperation but sensitive to being exploited; may try to sustain cooperation early but will cut back if exploitation persists.

Given those tendencies, I predict initial moderate cooperation from SLOTH and DUCK, early low contributions from GORILLA (exploitation), then gradual decline in total contributions as SLOTH and DUCK react. By the final rounds, contributions are likely to approach 0 (endgame free-riding).

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game pits private incentives (each coin kept yields 1 to the player) against group incentives (each contributed coin returns 1.8 total, or 0.45 to each individual). Individually, the dominant action is to withdraw everything (contribute 0), so cooperation must be sustained by norms, reciprocity, or costly sanctions/rewards. Punishment is costly (3 per unit) and deters by removing 4 per unit; rewards are also costly (3 per unit) and transfering 2 per unit to the recipient. Because sanctions and rewards are expensive, expect them to be used sparingly and strategically: players who care about group outcomes (CHICKEN, SLOTH to a degree) will try to support cooperators and deter serial free-riders (GORILLA) early on, but costly enforcement will not be constant. DUCK is variable and will sometimes match cooperators or abstain. Over 29 rounds we expect some initial cooperation from CHICKEN and moderate cooperation from SLOTH and DUCK, GORILLA to defect often early, occasional targeted punishments of obvious free-riders, intermittent rewards for consistent cooperators, and a gradual decline in contributions as the horizon advances (endgame effect), with punishments becoming rarer as costs outweigh future benefits.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 rounds, 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player withdraws some of the 20 coins (equivalently: chooses a contribution from 0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so contributions provide a social return but leave an incentive to free-ride (you get only 2.1/4 = 0.525 per coin contributed, less than 1 private coin kept). Because players see only round summaries (including who rewarded/punished them) and the game has a known finite horizon, dynamics will combine a few tendencies:

- Early rounds: many players (especially those with higher patience and pro-social priors such as GORILLA and DUCK) tend to contribute relatively high amounts; players with lower mental-accounting or weaker pro-social cues (CHICKEN) are more likely to contribute less or experiment with free-riding.
- Mid-game: if free-riding appears, conditional cooperators will either reduce contributions or use the costly sanctioning instruments (punish or reward) to try to steer behavior. Punishment is costly and typically used sparingly — small, targeted punishments are more common than frequent large ones. Rewards are also used but less frequently than contributions themselves.
- Late rounds: because the interaction is finite and common knowledge, contributions often decline toward the last rounds (backward-induction pull), unless social norms have already been stabilized through sanctions. Players who value reciprocity or long-term norms (GORILLA, DUCK) may sustain higher contributions longer; CHICKEN is more likely to defect as end approaches. SLOTH tends to follow mental-accounting preferences and respond to local incentives rather than strong principled cooperation.

Uncertainties: exact levels of punishment/reward are hard to predict; players may punish only once in reaction to repeated low contributions, or they may prefer to reward good contributors. We therefore predict mostly cooperative contributions from GORILLA and DUCK, moderate and responsive contributions from SLOTH, and variable lower contributions from CHICKEN, with sparse punishments and occasional small rewards to stabilize cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public-goods game with 4 players. Each round every player effectively decides to either leave 20 coins in the public pot (contribute = 20) or withdraw them for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split evenly among all players, so each contributor increases everyone’s share by 8 coins (because a 20-coin contribution becomes 32, split four ways = +8 each). That makes contributing socially valuable but individually costly (you give up 20 privately and receive only k*8 from the pot where k is the number of contributors).

Because contributions are binary and decisions are private each round, early rounds often show some uncertainty—some players test cooperation while others try to free-ride. Punishment (costly: 3 to punish, -7 to target per unit) and reward (costly: 3 to reward, +4 to target per unit) allow cooperators to sanction defectors or reinforce cooperation, but both are costly to the actor so they’re used sparingly. Messages can signal intentions, encourage contributions, or threaten sanctions.

Given the provided player priors (generally patient players, mixed mental accounting), I expect initial mixed behavior with modest use of punishment to deter free-riding, then a few rounds of cooperation once norms form, and some erosion of cooperation toward the end (final-round temptation to defect). Punishment will be targeted but limited (single-unit sanctions typical), and rewards will be used occasionally to encourage turning points.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players, a 1.8 multiplication on the group pot, and the option to punish (costly to source, costly to target) and reward (costly to source, beneficial to target) after seeing contributions. Key incentives:
- Contributing raises group payoffs but is individually costly: each coin contributed returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 to the contributor via the shared return, so the private return per contributed coin (0.45) is smaller than the private benefit of keeping it (1). The one-shot Nash incentive is to withdraw everything (contribute 0).
- Repeated interaction, uncertainty about others, and the ability to punish/reward create scope for cooperation: modest contributions can be sustained if players value future rounds or if sanctions/rewards are used to discipline free-riders.
- Punishment is effective but costly: each punishment unit costs the sender 3 and subtracts 4 from the target. Rewards cost 3 and give the target +2. Players tend to use sanctions sparingly because of the high sender cost; rewards are also used sparingly.
- Expect initial moderate cooperation (testing and reciprocity), occasional defections as players test or exploit, a few targeted punishments early-mid to enforce norms, some small rewards to reinforce high contributors, and a gradual endgame decline toward lower contributions as round 29 approaches. Personas bias predictions: CHICKEN and DUCK are likely to make higher, consistent contributions; GORILLA shows moderate behavior with occasional tests; SLOTH is cautious but responsive to sanctions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), 23 rounds known in advance. Each round every player either leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribute 0). The total pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally; defecting (contributing 0) gives a private 20 plus the equal share, so defection is individually tempting while full contribution is collectively best. After redistribution players may pay to punish others (1 coin cost to the punisher removes 2 coins from the target). Punishment is costly for the sender and anonymous for targets (they see aggregate deductions but not who punished them), but summary information does show how much each player spent on punishment and how much was deducted from them.

Given the player personas and typical experimental dynamics, I expect:
- Early rounds: most players try cooperation, but at least one risk/greed-leaning player (GORILLA) will test defection early to exploit the group. Others respond with modest punishment to deter free-riding.
- Middle rounds: cooperation becomes the norm after punishment enforces it; occasional one-shot defections (experimentation or mistakes) provoke targeted, small punishments from a few cooperators.
- Late rounds (endgame): cooperation frays as the horizon approaches; defections increase near the final rounds. Some players may still punish in the last rounds out of fairness preferences, but punishments are generally limited because they cost the punisher and provide no future deterrent.

I will simulate a plausible sequence across all 23 rounds with sparse, purposeful chat messages and modest punishment levels.

game_explanation:
This is a three-player repeated public goods game with a per-round endowment of 20 coins and a multiplier of 2 on the group pot that is split equally among all 3 players. That means each coin contributed returns 2/3 coin to the contributor (social return < private cost), so the one-shot selfish incentive is to contribute 0. Still, some players will contribute out of pro-social preference, patience, or mental accounting. Because contributions are private at decision time and punishment happens after contributions are revealed, the main mechanism that can sustain above-zero contributions is targeted punishment: players can spend 1 coin to reduce another player's round payoff by 2 coins.

Likely unfolding here: DUCK and SLOTH are the more patient / mental-accounting–oriented players and tend to contribute at moderate levels. GORILLA is more variable and will sometimes free-ride or respond to losses/risk cues by lowering contribution. Early rounds tend to show modest cooperation with occasional small punishments directed at clear under-contributors; punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher and because punishers are anonymous to targets. Over rounds, punishment can push an opportunistic player to increase contributions somewhat, but in late rounds contributions usually drift down (end‑game effect). Uncertainties: how often GORILLA retaliates with punishment, and whether SLOTH and DUCK coordinate on a stable contribution level. I predict small, infrequent punishment actions mostly aimed at GORILLA when they under-contribute; no large-scale punishment campaigns.

game_explanation:
With three players and 6 rounds, players face the classic tension: contributing to the public pot helps the group but costs private coins; free-riders benefit without paying. The 1.9 multiplier creates a social return less than the private return (0.95 per coin contributed to oneself), so purely selfish incentives push toward 0 contribution. Repetition and punishment can sustain higher contributions: those who punish low contributors pay a cost now to reduce a target's payoff and signal that low contributions are costly. Because punishment is expensive (cost 4 per unit, 12 deducted from the target), we expect punishment to be used sparingly — typically early or mid-game to enforce norms, then decline in final rounds (endgame). Chat messages will be few and strategic (requests, calls for fairness, apologies). Based on the personas:
- SLOTH (high mental accounting, patient) is cautious: likely moderate contributions and reluctant to punish except to enforce fairness.
- GORILLA (patient, moderate risk) tends to cooperate moderately and will use punishment once to discipline obvious free-riding.
- DUCK (risk-seeking, low mental accounting) is the most variable and more likely to under-contribute early or in the last round; after being punished, DUCK may increase contributions.

Below is a round-by-round transcript that follows these patterns.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-round public goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round players choose an integer contribution (0–20) out of the 20-coin pot; withheld coins are private. The group contribution is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so there is a social benefit to contributing but a private incentive to free-ride. Because players see contributions each round and can reward others (cost 1 coin to give 1 coin), we should expect conditional cooperation: players who see higher contributions from others tend to maintain or increase their own contributions and may give small rewards to signal approval; those who see free-riding tend to reduce contributions. Rewards will be sparse (1–2 units) and targeted at relatively higher contributors. Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation as players test each other; mid-game may stabilize if positive reciprocity and rewards are present; as the end approaches (final rounds) expected contributions usually decline because future reciprocity is limited, and rewards become rarer. Individual tendencies from personas: GORILLA and SLOTH are more patient and thus somewhat more likely to contribute moderately; DUCK is middling; CHICKEN is the most likely to free-ride. The transcript below simulates plausible, conservative choices and sparse rewarding consistent with these incentives and uncertainties.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with three players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group sum is doubled and split equally, so each player gets (2 * total_contribution)/3 plus their private remainder (20 - own contribution). Incentives: contributing helps the group return but is individually costly; the dominant one-shot incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride). Repetition and feedback create scope for conditional cooperation: cooperative players may contribute more early and adjust based on peers' behavior; strategic players may exploit cooperators or punish defectors by reducing contributions. Uncertainties include whether cooperators will stick to high contributions when exploited and whether low contributors will respond to others by increasing contributions. With visible peers' outcomes each round, expect: DUCK (high cooperative tendency) to start high and decline if exploited; GORILLA (mixed, conditional) to give moderate contributions and adjust gradually; SLOTH (low patience, asset-protective) to contribute little and often free-ride. Toward the final rounds, anticipate an endgame decline in contributions.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game is a repeated, 6-round interaction with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player either leaves the whole 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them privately (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so contributions mostly benefit everyone; the one-shot incentive is to withdraw (free-ride), but repeated play and punishment/reward allow cooperators to enforce contributions.

Likely dynamics:
- DUCK and CHICKEN have high patience and mental accounting, so they lean toward cooperating (contributing 20) and occasionally using rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- GORILLA is less patient and more likely to test/defect; when punished enough, GORILLA may switch to contributing.
- SLOTH mixes: risk-loving tendencies make defection tempting, but SLOTH may respond to punishment and to peer rewards by cooperating in some rounds.
- Punishment and reward will be used sparingly. Cooperators will punish visible defectors intermittently to deter free-riding; occasional rewards will reinforce those who switch to cooperating.
- Final round incentives to free-ride produce some unraveling; players may test defection in round 6.

Below is a full simulated transcript from round 1 through round 6, with brief reasoning at each round start, contributions, sparse chat, the (sparse) punishment/reward actions (only non-zero edges shown), and the summary information that players would see after each round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 3-player public goods game played for 6 rounds. Each round every player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. The social dilemma: contributing benefits the group (each contributed coin yields 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 to each player) but costs the contributor a full coin immediately. Short-run incentives favor free-riding (contribute 0) because a contributor gets less than 1 coin back personally, while group welfare is maximized when all contribute.

Uncertainties include whether declarations in chat are truthful and whether cooperators will punish free-riders by lowering future contributions. Given the personas: GORILLA (high mental accounting, moderately patient) is predisposed to try moderate cooperation and to track norms; DUCK (moderate patience, high loss sensitivity) may prefer stable moderate contributions or punish large losses from being exploited; SLOTH (low patience, low mental accounting) tends to exploit opportunities to free-ride. Expect initial attempts to coordinate on positive contributions (some chat pledges), then a decline in contributions if SLOTH free-rides and cooperators respond by lowering their contributions over rounds. Chat is likely sparse and used for simple pledges, mild reproaches, or last attempts to encourage cooperation.

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game mixes private withdrawal (equivalently, contribution) decisions with a small-cost positive-reward stage. Key incentives and likely dynamics:
- The public multiplier (2.1) makes group contributions socially beneficial: each coin contributed yields 2.1/4 = 0.525 to every player, so the group gains from contributions even though the private immediate return on each contributed coin (0.525) is below keeping it (1). Therefore purely selfish incentives favor withdrawing (free-riding), while social preferences or reciprocity favor contributing.
- Because identity and past actions are observable only via round summaries (including who rewarded whom), players can signal cooperativeness and reward others for cooperating. Rewards cost the sender 1 and give the receiver 1 (pure transfer) and are typically used sparingly to reinforce cooperators.
- Personas bias expectations: DUCK and CHICKEN are the most cooperative/ patient and likely to contribute highly across rounds; GORILLA leans toward lower contributions / free-riding; SLOTH tends to be moderate and responsive to others (may start mid and drift down slowly). Rewards will be selective and small: cooperators will occasionally reward each other; they will rarely reward a known low contributor like GORILLA.
- Over repeated rounds we therefore expect above-zero cooperation maintained by DUCK and CHICKEN, SLOTH hovering in the middle, and GORILLA contributing little. Rewarding will be sparse, targeted at higher contributors, and used intermittently to signal approval. Near the final round a small decline in contributions is possible, but social types may still contribute somewhat.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round repeated public-goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 (cooperate) or withdraws the full 20 (defect). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so the group benefit is highest when everyone contributes; however, an individual can gain by free-riding within a round. Because the game is finitely repeated and players cannot identify who punished or rewarded them, we expect:

- Early rounds: high cooperation as players follow conditional-cooperation norms and have incentives to build reciprocity.
- Middle rounds: sporadic defections may appear. When someone defects, others tend to punish (sparsely) to enforce cooperation; rewards are used occasionally to reinforce good behavior.
- Late rounds (especially the final one): cooperation tends to fall as forward-looking deterrence disappears; punishment becomes less effective as a deterrent, so players may defect more often.
- Chats will be few and targeted (requests for cooperation, short reproaches, brief thanks). Punishment and reward will be used sparingly — mostly 0 or 1 units per action — and typically directed at obvious defectors.

Now the round-by-round transcript.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private payoff temptation (withdraw the whole 20) against group benefit (leave 20 in the common pot so everyone gets a share). Contributing is socially efficient: each contributed 20 produces 1.6*20 = 32 total, i.e. +8 to every player per contributor. But an individual who defects keeps 20 privately while still receiving their equal share of the public good, so short-run incentives favor free-riding. The reward stage lets players spend costly signals (3 coins per reward unit) to add value (4 coins per unit) to others; this can partially compensate cooperators and encourage turn-taking, but rewards are costly and anonymous to targets (targets see amounts received but not who sent them), which limits direct personal reciprocity.

Given four players and six rounds, I expect:
- Early rounds: some testing. A mix of contributions and defections as players assess others.
- Middle rounds: a tendency to stabilize around cooperation if a cooperating majority forms and uses sparse positive rewards to shore up contributors.
- Final round: the classic "end-game" incentive to defect becomes strong; some players may switch to take the 20 privately because future retaliation/rewards are impossible.

Uncertainties: whether defectors will be converted by social messages and rewards, and how persistent cooperators are in the face of repeated exploitation. Rewards should be used sparingly (few units per round) and mostly to reinforce contributors or thank new cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a six-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Because each coin contributed yields 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 to oneself, the selfish incentive is to contribute zero while the group is better off when people contribute. Punishment is available after contributions are revealed: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and removes 12 coins from the target. Messages are allowed at any time and can influence expectations but do not identify who punished whom.

Likely dynamics across six rounds:
- Early rounds: modest cooperation from most players (they value the group return and have patient, mental-accounting tendencies), with occasional opportunistic low contributions. Players who value cooperation (Duck and Sloth) are inclined to punish clear free-riding—sparingly—because punishment is costly but can enforce norms.
- Midgame: tit-for-tat style responses — a punished low contributor often increases contributions in subsequent rounds; retaliatory punishment can occur but is typically mild (1 unit) because punishment is costly.
- Endgame (round 6): with no future rounds to enforce, some players will reduce contributions to free-ride; punishment tends to drop off in the final round because it cannot change future behavior and is expensive.

Overall I predict relatively stable cooperation around the middle of the endowment, occasional single-unit punishments targeted at low contributions, a few brief messages calling out low contributions or acknowledging behavior, and some decline in contributions in the final round.

game_explanation:
This 10-round public goods game pits four players with different priors about patience, risk, and accounting against a standard social dilemma. Each round every player decides how much of the 20-coin pot to withdraw privately (so contribution = 20 − withdraw). Contributions are aggregated, multiplied by 2.1, and split equally; after payoffs are shown, players may punish (cost 1 to source, −3 to target) or reward (cost 1 to source, +1 to target) peers. Players see round summaries (who punished/rewarded them and amounts), so behavior can respond to social enforcement.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: conditional cooperation — more patient, “pro-social” players (SLOTH, CHICKEN) are likely to contribute relatively high amounts; impatient/high-risk GORILLA is likely to under-contribute initially. DUCK will be moderate.
- Punishment is typically sparse: players will rarely punish, but will do so to deter obvious free-riding. Rewards are also used but less frequently than simple withholding behavior would suggest.
- Over rounds, feedback (punishment/reward) tends to push the group toward more moderate-to-high contributions: free-riders either increase contributions to avoid punishment or are occasionally tolerated but not rewarded.
- Uncertainties: exact magnitudes of contributions and the timing of sanctions are noisy; players may experiment with small punishments/rewards to shape others' behavior. The personas bias predictions but do not deterministically fix choices.

game_explanation:
This 4-player public-goods game creates a clear tension between private extraction (withdrawing the whole 20) and contributing to the group pot (leaving 20 in the pot). Because the pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split evenly across all players, a single player's contribution benefits everyone but does not pay off fully for the contributor. With 4 players, each 20 contributed yields a per-player return of 8 (20 * 1.6 / 4). A contributor therefore sacrifices 20 private coins to give each player +8, so the private incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). Over repeated rounds players face strategic uncertainty: contributing can sustain higher group returns if enough others also contribute, but early contributors are exploited if others free-ride.

The additional reward stage lets players transfer small amounts to reward cooperators (cost 3 per unit to giver, +4 per unit to recipient). Rewarding can encourage cooperation, but because the summaries are anonymous about who gave which reward, reputational signaling is imperfect: players can see who spent on rewards and who received totals, but they cannot trace an incoming reward to a giver in the summaries. That reduces the power of direct reciprocity and makes reward more of a costly group signal than a targeted promise of return.

Given the personas: SLOTH is patient and will tend to cooperate early even when exploitation risk is present; CHICKEN is somewhat cooperative and responsive to precedent; DUCK and GORILLA are more inclined toward self-protection (high loss-avoidance cues), so they are likelier to free-ride, though DUCK may occasionally switch to cooperate when prompted. Expect early rounds to show a mix (some cooperators), visible payoff advantage for free-riders, and a gradual decline in contributions as cooperative players either get discouraged or try selective rewarding. Rewarding will be sparse and mostly used to encourage fellow cooperators rather than to punish free-riders.

Rewarding lets players transfer (at personal cost) positive payoffs to others, so it can sustain cooperation but is itself costly and therefore used sparingly.

- DUCK (impatient, low risk tolerance) tends to free-ride; expect low contributions throughout, with occasional short-lived increases if rewarded.
- CHICKEN (conditional cooperator, lower mental accounting) will respond to social signals and occasionally reward or reciprocate but not consistently.

Near the end, standard finite-horizon incentives pull contributions down; rewards become rarer as players conserve coins. Overall, expect declining contributions with sparse, targeted 1-unit rewards across the 29 rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round each player either leaves their 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so each individual’s single contribution yields 12.5 to each player but costs the contributor 20 — individually costly, collectively beneficial. Punishment is available after contributions: 1 coin spent by the punisher removes 2 coins from the punished player's payoff. Players cannot see who directed punishment to them.

Likely dynamics:
- The dominant one-shot incentive is to withdraw (contribute 0). Without punishment, we expect widespread defection.
- With punishment and repeated interaction (23 rounds), a cooperative norm can be sustained early on if some players (here, GORILLA by persona) consistently contribute and enforce cooperation with selective punishment.
- Because punishment is costly to enforceers, enforcement tends to be sparse and targeted: punishments occur when defectors are surprising or repeated.
- Over time, finite-horizon effects and accrued costs of punishment make sustained enforcement harder; cooperation typically erodes toward the end of the game. game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated (6-round) public goods game with three players. Each round each player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins (an integer 0–20) to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split evenly among the three players. Because 1.9 < 3, each coin you contribute returns less than one coin to you personally (per-coin private return ≈ 0.633), so the dominant individual incentive is to contribute 0. However, if everyone contributes, the group earns more overall and each player's round payoff can be higher. Cheap talk (messages) can encourage cooperation early on but tends to be fragile: people who are exploited often reduce contributions in later rounds, and toward the final rounds contributions typically fall (end-game effect).

Using the provided personas as priors:
- SLOTH (high mental-accounting, moderately patient) will try to be fair early — prefers clear, consistent choices — but will reduce contributions if others free-ride.
- GORILLA (moderate patience, fairly loss-averse) is opportunistic and likely to lower contributions to exploit cooperators once it benefits them.
- DUCK (high patience, high risk-taking, low mental-accounting) is more willing to keep contributing for longer (trusting or pro-social), though may give up when exploitation becomes obvious.

I therefore predict initial modest-to-moderate cooperation supported by a brief coordination message; then gradual erosion of contributions across rounds as free-riding appears, with near-zero contributions by round 6.

This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each round every player effectively decides how much to contribute to a shared pot (0–20 integer), with the remainder taken privately. The shared pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so contributions benefit the group but delivering private incentives to under-contribute (free-ride). After contributions are revealed and payoffs computed, players can spend costly reward units (cost 3 to the sender, +2 to the recipient) to reward others; rewards are the only social transfer available, and they are typically used sparingly because they are costly.

Given the player priors, I expect Gorilla and Chicken (high patience, risk-tolerance, and strong mental accounting) to start relatively generous and to use occasional rewards to reinforce cooperation. Sloth (medium scores) will be moderately cooperative but less disciplined about rewarding. Duck has mixed cues and may start moderately but is more likely than Gorilla/Chicken to reduce contributions if they perceive exploitation or expect others to start free-riding. Common uncertainties: players see only their own round payoff and reward flows, not others' contribution histories directly, so social inference is noisy — this tends to make rewards occasional signaling acts rather than continuous enforcement. Over many rounds, typical dynamics: early moderate-to-high cooperation maintained by mutual rewards, then gradual decline as some players reduce contributions (especially near the end) and rewards become rarer; a few intermittent rewards sustain cooperation sporadically.

This 29-round public goods game is set up so that cooperation (high contributions) benefits the group but individuals have an incentive to withdraw coins for private use. Given the player priors: SLOTH and CHICKEN have strong mental-accounting tendencies and are relatively patient, so they start by contributing a lot. GORILLA is more opportunistic/risk-taking and will contribute moderately early but is prone to reduce contributions if others free-ride. DUCK shows weak mental accounting and high loss-sensitivity—more likely to free-ride early and remain low. Rewards are available but costly (3 coins to giver, 2 to receiver), so players reward sparingly: mostly to reinforce high contributors early on. Over rounds we expect an initial cooperative phase sustained by a few reciprocal rewards between the committed contributors, followed by gradual decline as free-riding by DUCK (and then by GORILLA) reduces the incentive to contribute; rewards become rarer and eventually stop, and contributions drift toward low levels by the end of the 29 rounds.

Everyone reduces further; rewards are seen as too costly to justify given low group payoffs.
GORILLA (occasionally responsive to past generosity) gives a one-off 1-unit reward to SLOTH, perhaps acknowledging SLOTH's earlier high contributions. Otherwise the decline continues.
No significant change: most players accept low contribution equilibrium; rewards absent.
Contributions continue to drift down slowly; players conserve coins and do not reward.
Late-middle game: GORILLA reduces to near-zero; CHICKEN and SLOTH still contribute a little but are winding down.

Players further lower contributions; any hope of sustained cooperation has largely evaporated.
Very low contributions across the board; reward is rarely worthwhile at these payoff levels.

Endgame logic strengthens private incentives; players contribute the minimum they find acceptable (mostly zero or very small).

Most players choose zero or one coin; with only two players contributing a single coin, rewards are pointless.
Near the final rounds, the dominant strategy emerges: withdraw privately. Everyone contributes zero — no rewards.
Final round: backward induction and low past reciprocity lead players to contribute nothing. No one rewards.

game_explanation:
This 3-player public goods game sets up a collective-efficiency vs. private-incentive tension. Each coin you contribute returns only 0.633 to you (1.9/3), so private best response is to contribute 0, but the group is better off when everyone contributes. Punishment (costly to send, larger harm to the recipient) gives cooperators a way to discipline low contributors and stabilize higher contributions. Because punishers are anonymous, punishment tends to be targeted at visible low contributions but does not provoke clear retaliation against a named individual — it can cause quieter norm enforcement.

Given the player priors: SLOTH is the most likely to under-contribute (low patience, strong mental accounting), while GORILLA and DUCK are more patient and inclined to contribute and to enforce norms. I therefore expect early rounds where GORILLA and DUCK contribute substantially and punish SLOTH for low contributions; SLOTH responds by increasing contributions over rounds but sometimes lapses and is punished again. Punishment is used sparingly (usually 1 punishment unit) because it is costly. As the 6-round end approaches, policing remains but is slightly attenuated.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game is a multi-round, binary-contribution setting with a small group (4 players) and an explicit reward stage. Each player effectively chooses to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or withdraw it all (defect). The total contribution is scaled by 1.6 and split equally, so a lone defector gains relative to cooperating others in the same round. Because the game lasts six rounds, forward-looking incentives weaken cooperation as the end approaches (final-round defection is common). The reward stage lets players transfer costly help (+4 to recipient for cost 3), which can sustain cooperation by rewarding contributors — but rewards are anonymous in players’ summaries, so targeted reciprocity is imperfect. Uncertainties that will shape behavior: who will test cooperation early, whether cooperators will punish by withholding rewards, and whether any players try to rebuild cooperation via small positive rewards. Given the provided personas, I expect initial partial cooperation with one tester (free-rider) and sparse positive rewards among cooperators. Over rounds cooperation drifts down, with occasional attempts to re-cooperate, and final-round defection is likely.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round each player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally; every coin contributed yields 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 coins back to each player, so contributing benefits the group but reduces an individual's private holding. Key dynamics likely to appear here:

- Conditional cooperation: some players (especially those with moderate-to-high pro-social priors) will contribute initially to test others and then match observed behavior in later rounds.
- Free-riding and punishment: players who keep low contributions can gain in the short run; cooperators may reduce contributions in response (a form of punishment or reciprocity).
- Communication can shape expectations (promises or calls to cooperate), but messages are cheap and can be unreliable.
- Endgame effect: because the game lasts 6 rounds, cooperation often unravels toward the last round(s) as the incentive to build future reputation declines. Expect more cooperation early/mid game and partial unwinding in the final round.

Given the provided personas:
- GORILLA: relatively cooperative and forward-looking; likely to propose and sustain moderate contributions early.
- DUCK: moderately cooperative and likely to reciprocate.
- SLOTH: less tied to mental-accounting cues and more opportunistic; likely to test low contributions and exploit cooperation where possible.

I therefore predict some initial cooperation by GORILLA and DUCK, SLOTH contributing low, eliciting a drop in contributions, then partial recovery as GORILLA tries to coordinate, and some unraveling by round 6.

game_explanation:
This is a 3-player repeated public goods game (6 rounds). Each round each player privately gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution 0–20. The group sum is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the three players, so each coin you contribute returns 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 coins to you personally but 1.9 coins total to the group. That creates the classic tension: group-optimum is to contribute more (everyone would be better off if all contributed high amounts), while individually the dominant short-run incentive is to contribute nothing and free-ride.

Given the provided personas: GORILLA has a strong mental-accounting tendency and is likely to try to coordinate and sustain cooperation (higher, somewhat consistent contributions, and to use chat to encourage cooperation). SLOTH is middling and less bound by accounting — somewhat willing to contribute but responsive to others' behavior. DUCK shows risk/loss-seeking tendencies and may be more opportunistic; likely to exploit cooperative others early or oscillate between low contributions and occasional token contributions.

Uncertainties:
- Whether early cooperation can be sustained depends on whether cooperative signals are believed and whether free-riding is punished.
- Endgame (round 6) typically triggers a drop in contributions because future punishment is impossible.
- Chat can partly mitigate free-riding, but promises can be cheap and may be tested.

Predicted overall unfolding: GORILLA will try to lead with explicit cooperative proposals; SLOTH will respond moderately. DUCK will initially free-ride or give minimal contributions, benefiting from others. This will push GORILLA and SLOTH to lower contributions in middle rounds, but occasional attempts to restore cooperation will produce fluctuations. By the final round, contributions fall (everyone tends to defect).

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game pits private incentives (take the 20 privately) against group payoffs (leave the 20 in the pot so the multiplied sum is shared). With 1.6 multiplication and equal split, each contributed 20 yields 0.4*20 = 8 coins returned to each player per contributor, so a single contributor lowers their private return relative to keeping the 20 but raises group income. Because the horizon is finite (6 rounds), pure backward induction predicts unraveling toward defection late in the game; in practice, many participants cooperate early and use sparse punishment and occasional rewards to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly (3 to sender, -7 to target) and anonymous (targets cannot identify who punished them), so punishment is used sparingly to deter free-riding. Rewards are also costly (3 to sender, +4 to target) and typically used to reinforce cooperation.

Given the four personas:
- CHICKEN is the most pro-social/cooperative and likely to contribute reliably and to both punish and reward sparingly to sustain cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players and 29 rounds. Each round players choose how many of the 20 coins to withdraw privately; the rest become the public contribution. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so the group payoff from a single contributed coin is 1.8 coins divided among 4 players = 0.45 to each player. Individually, withdrawing a coin gives the full 1 coin to the chooser, so there is a classic social dilemma: contributing is better for the group but withdrawing is better for private payoff unless others contribute sufficiently. The reward stage allows costly prosocial reinforcement (each reward unit costs 3 to the sender and gives 2 to the receiver), which can sustain cooperation but is itself costly.

Given the four personas:
- CHICKEN is the most likely to contribute a lot (patient, strongly loss-averse, high mental accounting) and to reward cooperators.
- GORILLA has relatively high mental accounting and tends toward some cooperation and occasional rewarding.
- SLOTH is moderate/mixed and will often contribute at intermediate levels; may reward selectively.
- DUCK is more likely to free-ride (lower patience, higher risk-seeking), contributing little and rarely rewarding.

I therefore predict:
- Early rounds: CHICKEN leads by contributing heavily; GORILLA and SLOTH contribute moderately; DUCK contributes little. Without strong punishment, defectors (DUCK) often obtain higher round payoffs, which tends to erode cooperation over time.
- Rewards will be used sparingly and mostly to reinforce cooperators (CHICKEN, sometimes GORILLA/SLOTH); senders will usually send single units when they do act.
- Over many rounds contributions will trend down (especially approaching the final rounds) as the incentive to free-ride grows and the marginal effect of rewards is small relative to the cost. A few intermittent reward exchanges slow the decline but do not fully prevent it.
- Players see only their own round summary (net payoff, amount they spent on rewards, and amount they received), and they can identify who rewarded them — so targeted rewarding can occur, but it remains sparse.
