game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players per round. Each player effectively chooses either to contribute their whole 20-token endowment to the group (cooperate) or to withdraw it for private use (defect). The group pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so a single cooperator helps everyone but is personally exposed: defecting gives a private 20 plus the shared return, while cooperating gives only the shared return. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding and do not know how many rounds remain, early rounds tend to test norms (some cooperation), and later rounds often show erosion of cooperation unless punishment/reward stabilizes it.

Punishment and reward are costly to the sender (2 coins per unit) and have asymmetric effects on the recipient (punishment removes 3 coins per unit; reward adds 2 coins per unit). Because punishments are effective but costly, expect few, targeted punishments aimed at visible defectors; rewards are used sparingly to encourage cooperators. Players who saw cooperating payoffs drop (compared to defectors) may switch to defection; those who value norms (high mental-accounting) will try to enforce cooperation by punishing, at the cost of their round payoff. There is uncertainty about how persistent defectors will be: defectors earn more immediately, so punishers face a difficult trade-off between bearing costs to sustain cooperation and freeriding by others.

Given the provided personas: some players (SLOTH, DUCK, PARROT) lean toward cooperation; others with low mental-accounting or high risk/gain-seeking (GORILLA, some of DOG/MOOSE) are more likely to defect. CHICKEN's high norm adherence suggests frequent targeted punishment of defectors. Overall we expect initial mix of cooperation with a handful of defectors, limited punishments targeted at defectors, occasional rewards to cooperators, and a gradual decline in cooperation as defectors earn consistently more — unless punishers successfully deter them.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players and 6 rounds. Each round everyone has 20 coins and must either contribute all 20 to the public pot or keep all 20. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and shared equally among the 10 players, so each coin contributed yields 12.4 coins distributed to each player overall (i.e., each contributor increases every player's round payoff by 12.4). Individually, contributing is risky: if few others contribute, contributors earn less than those who free-ride that round; if many contribute, the group can generate large returns.

Given the personas and the known 6-round horizon, I expect:
- An initial cluster of contributors (those with stronger mental-accounting and pro-social priors) will try contributing in early rounds to signal cooperation.
- Free-riders (or those with weaker social cues / low mental-accounting) will exploit early contributions because defecting when others cooperate yields higher individual payoffs.
- Seeing that contributors earn less early on will lead to a decline in contributions over rounds (conditional cooperation), with a substantial drop toward the final rounds. Chat is sparse but used by cooperators to encourage reciprocation and by a few players to note frustration or signal intentions.
- In the final round nearly everyone defects (endgame), though a small number of stubborn cooperators may keep contributing.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players, each choosing a binary action each round: withdraw 20 coins privately (defect) or leave 20 in the shared pot (cooperate). Cooperators increase the pot's return for everyone (pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally), but defectors keep the 20 immediately. After payoffs are distributed, players can punish (cost 2 to sender, -3 to target) or reward (cost 2 to sender, +2 to target). Players see who punished/rewarded them and see round summaries, but they do not know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds are uncertain: some players may test cooperation; others may defect to exploit cooperators because withdrawing 20 gives a strong private payoff unless many others cooperate.
- Conditional cooperators (those who gained when others cooperated) will tend to match observed behavior in subsequent rounds. Punishments are costly and therefore used sparingly, mostly to deter persistent free-riding rather than to fully equalize payoffs.
- Rewards are also costly but can be used to reinforce cooperation when it appears.
- Over repeated rounds without a known end, we can expect fluctuation: clusters of cooperation when enough players coordinate, and clusters of defection when defecting pays off. Punishment occasionally reduces repeat defection but rarely eliminates it entirely.
- Chat is sparse; players use it to signal intentions or to react to punishment/reward.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, private endowments (20 coins per round), and voluntary rewarding after redistribution. Each round the group benefit equals 3.8 times the sum of contributions, split equally across all six players. Because the marginal per-capita return from one coin contributed is 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633, contributing is socially efficient (it raises total group payoff) but individually costly: a contributor sacrifices 1 coin and gets only about 0.63 back from the public pot. Rewarding is doubly costly to the giver (3 coins per reward unit) but benefits the recipient (2 coins), so rewards are used sparingly and usually to reinforce prosocial contributors rather than to punish.

Likely dynamics here:
- Parrot and Chicken show the strongest priors for cooperation (high patience and pro-social tendencies) and therefore tend to contribute more consistently.
- Duck is the habitual low contributor / free-rider in the early rounds and may shift only slowly if others reward or message.
- Sloth and Dog sit in the middle — sometimes cooperative, sometimes opportunistic.
- Gorilla’s pattern is mixed: moderate contributions that can decline if others free-ride; but Gorilla may also reward selectively to sustain reciprocity.

Given these tendencies, we expect:
- Moderately high initial contributions from Chicken and Parrot, medium from Sloth and Gorilla, low from Duck, and variable from Dog.
- A slow decline in average contributions if free-riding persists, with occasional rebounds when players reward or encourage others.
- Sparse rewards (mostly single-unit awards) targeted at consistent contributors (Chicken, Parrot), sometimes to encourage small contributors (Duck) when they try to join.
- Messages will be rare and tactical: short praise, encouragement, or mild complaints.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 26. Each round starts with a short explanation of expectations for that round, then the observed chat, contributions, any rewards (non-zero only), and the summary shown to players (contribution, rewards sent and received by name, and each player’s net payoff that round). Use the avatar order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT for every contribution list.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, indefinite-length public goods game with costly pro-social rewards. Players start with private endowments each round and choose contributions; the group return is multiplied and shared equally, so each player's best immediate reply to others' contributions is to contribute zero, but group welfare is maximized when everyone contributes. Because players can reward others by spending private coins to give benefits to peers, rewards can support cooperation by reinforcing contributors. Typical dynamics: some players begin with moderate-to-high contributions (conditional cooperators), others start low or test the group (free-riders). Early rewards tend to be targeted at high contributors and help sustain cooperation; if free-riding increases, contributions drift down. Chat is usually sparse and used to thank or call out low contributors. Because rewards are costly (3 per unit) and give only 2 to the target, rewards are used sparingly—often 1 unit at a time—and mainly to signal appreciation for consistent contributors.

Given the player priors:
- SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK are relatively patient and likely to start as conditional cooperators (moderate-high contributions).
- CHICKEN may be cautious but sometimes contributes when others do.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds, and a binary choice each round: keep 20 coins or contribute all 20 to a group pot. Contributing is costly to the individual (it reduces one’s own round payoff by 7.6 coins compared to keeping the 20), but benefits the group: everyone gets a share of the multiplied pot. That creates the classic tension — free-riding dominates individually, while full contribution maximizes collective payoff.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds may show some cooperation from players with strong mental-accounting or conditional-cooperator tendencies (OWL, MOOSE, DOG, RABBIT, GORILLA). They signal a cooperative norm.
- Players who are more gain- or risk-focused (DUCK, CHICK, CHICKEN) are likelier to defect early and consistently.
- Observing that free-riders earn more in a given round will tend to reduce others’ willingness to contribute in subsequent rounds (conditional cooperation and retaliatory/imitative defection).
- Messaging can partially sustain cooperation (short, norm-establishing notes), but as the final round approaches, many players revert to selfish play (backward induction), so contributions typically fall in the last round.
- Expect overall: an initial majority of contributors, a mid-game decline as some switch to free-riding, a possible short rebound if some push for coordination, and a drop in the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-player, repeated binary public goods game over 6 rounds. Each round each player decides privately whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (contribute = 20) or keep it (contribute = 0). All contributions are pooled, multiplied by 6.2, and the total is split equally among the 10 players. Because each contributed coin returns 6.2/10 = 0.62 coins to the contributor but costs them 1 coin, the individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0), while the group optimum would arise if many contribute.

Given the personas:
- A few players (e.g., PARROT, SLOTH, DUCK, DOG) are more patient or more likely to act cooperatively and may contribute early to signal cooperation.
- Several players show high sensitivity to losses or low patience (e.g., OWL, MOOSE, CHICK) or low gain-taking (GORILLA) and are likely to defect or punish cooperators by free-riding.
- Observing the payoff feedback (each player sees their own net payoff and peers' round payoffs) will quickly reveal that defectors earn more when cooperators are present. That typically undermines cooperation over rounds.
- Expect some initial cooperation from a minority, a few messages encouraging reciprocity, then declining contributions as players respond to the payoff evidence and the approaching end of the game (end-game effect).

Uncertainties: who will try to sustain cooperation (and for how long) is uncertain — PARROT in particular is likely to keep cooperating even if alone. Some conditional cooperators (SLOTH, DOG) may switch between contributing and not depending on others' observed behavior.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players who each receive 20 coins per round and privately choose an integer contribution (0–20). Contributions go into a shared pot multiplied by 3.8 and divided equally. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players can spend coins to punish (cost 3 per unit; each unit deducts 12 from the target) or reward (cost 3 per unit; each unit gives target +2). Players may send sparse messages. They do not know when the game will end, so behavior tends to show conditional cooperation: many players start by contributing moderate amounts, then adjust in response to others (punishing low contributors and occasionally rewarding cooperators). Punishments are costly and therefore used sparingly; rewards are also used but even more rarely because reward cost-to-target-benefit is inefficient (3 spent to give 2). Over many rounds I expect overall contributions to be fairly steady with occasional defections (small drops to test others), and occasional targeted punishments/rewards to enforce norms. Chat will be rare and purposeful (requests to contribute, short admonitions, thanks).

game_explanation:
This is a classic linear public-goods game with binary choices each round (contribute the whole 20 or withdraw it privately). Individually, withdrawing (free-riding) is the dominant short-run action because any single player's private return from contributing (2.6*20/7 ≈ 7.43) is much less than the 20 they give up. Group-level payoffs are highest when everyone contributes.

Because players can punish (costly to source, damaging to target) and reward (costly to source, beneficial to target), social enforcement is possible: cooperators can be encouraged with rewards or defectors discouraged with punishments. Punishment is more damaging per unit (target loses 3, source pays 2), so a few punishers can deter free-riding, but punishers pay a cost themselves, which can lead to retaliation and cycles of punishment. Players who value fairness and mental accounting (many personas here) will be inclined to contribute and to sanction free-riders sparsely; those with lower social-concern priors may test or sustain free-riding.

Typical unfolding in this group: initial cooperation by most, a few test free-rides, targeted punishments follow to deter repeat defections, occasional rewards to reinforce cooperators, some retaliation from punished defectors in later rounds, and fluctuations in cooperation before a rebound. Chats are sparse and used for short signals (requests, thanks, complaints).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round every player either leaves the whole 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws it (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so defecting (withdrawing 20) gives a private gain plus the shared return, which typically makes free-riding individually attractive in a single round. Because players do not know how many rounds there will be, reputational concerns and conditional cooperation (rewarding contributors, withholding reward from defectors) can sustain cooperation early on. Rewards are the only post-choice sanction/incentive: 1 reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins (so rewards are cost-neutral in transfer magnitude but costly to the sender). Uncertainty about others' choices and the unknown horizon will encourage a mixture of testing behavior (some will try defecting early), conditional responders (those who reward contributors), and occasional switches as players respond to payoffs and chat. Given the player personas, expect a small stable cooperating core (SLOTH, DUCK, PARROT, MOOSE are relatively more likely to contribute) and persistent defectors (GORILLA, DOG) with CHICKEN oscillating. Rewards will be used sparingly and mainly to reinforce contributors or welcome returners.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual temptation to free-ride against group incentives to contribute. Each round, contributing 20 builds a pot that is multiplied and shared; defecting keeps your 20 but still gives you a share of others' contributions, so short-term payoffs often favor free-riding. Because the game allows costly punishment (revealed and targetable), contributors can impose costs on defectors at personal expense to discourage free-riding. Expect early rounds to feature some experimentation: a mix of contributions and defection. Contributors will sometimes punish visible defectors to signal norms; punished defectors may switch to contributing (or persist if the punishment is too weak relative to the private gain). Cooperation often rises mid-game if punishment is credible; in the final round, many players may defect again because there's no future retaliation (end-game effect). Chat messages will be sparse and typically used to warn, complain, or promise punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public‑goods game with 7 players. Each round each player either leaves 20 coins in the shared pot (cooperates) or withdraws all 20 for private use (defects). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so when many people contribute the per‑player return from the pot can be substantial (and larger than the private 20 for defectors only if enough contribute). However, because defectors still share the multiplied pot, there is a short‑term incentive to free‑ride. The group can respond via costly punishment (2 coins to sender, −3 coins to target per unit) or costly reward (2 coins to sender, +2 coins to target per unit). Punishment is used sparingly in real games but can deter repeated free‑riding; reward is also used but less often. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, cooperation can be sustained by social enforcement (punishment/reward), but occasional testing/experiments (someone defecting to probe) and subsequent light sanctions are common.

Given the provided personas:
- GORILLA has a tendency to secure private payoff and may test defection early but can learn from sanctions.
- DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT are generally cooperative and occasionally reward or punish to maintain norms.
- MOOSE tends to enforce norms (punish) when they see free‑riding.
- SLOTH is mixed and may occasionally test by defecting.

Expect mostly cooperative rounds with occasional single defections and targeted, sparse punishments; occasional single rewards for cooperative gestures. Players will send a few purposeful chat messages (calling out defectors, thanking rewarders).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with costly peer-to-peer rewards. Each round players simultaneously decide how much of 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot (multiplied by 3.8 and split equally). Individually, contributing is costly (you only get ~0.63 back per coin), so there's a tension: group benefit favors contributions but private incentives favor free-riding. The reward stage (cost 3 per reward unit to giver, +2 to target) allows players to selectively reward cooperators — which can build cooperation by creating social returns or deflecting free-riding — but rewards are costly to the giver and so are used sparingly. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so early rounds involve testing and signaling; midgame tends to stabilize into norms (either higher cooperation sustained by mutual rewards or lower cooperation if rewards are withheld). Given the six personas, I expect DOG, GORILLA, and CHICKEN to be relatively reliable contributors (DOG and GORILLA especially), PARROT and SLOTH to be moderate contributors who adjust to group norms, and DUCK to start lower and respond slowly to incentives. Rewards will be sparse and targeted (small units) — mostly to praise high contributors or withhold praise for free-riders — with occasional messages encouraging cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with seven players. Each round players decide whether to leave all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraw them privately (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so individual incentives favor withdrawing (defection) while group welfare is higher when everyone contributes. Because players can punish (costly to source, costly to target) and reward (costly to source, beneficial to target) after seeing contributions, we expect the following broad dynamics:

- Early rounds: some players with strong norms or “mental accounting” (DUCK, CHICKEN, MOOSE, and perhaps SLOTH) are likely to contribute. Others with weaker such priors or stronger temptation may defect initially (e.g., GORILLA, DOG, PARROT). Defectors earn more from that round if not heavily sanctioned.
- Punishment and selective rewarding are used sparingly but strategically: cooperators will punish repeat free-riders to change incentives; rewards are used occasionally to encourage cooperation. Because punishments are costly to both sides, punishing tends to be limited to a few units directed at clear free-riders.
- Over rounds: sanctioning tends to increase cooperation — punished players often shift toward contributing in subsequent rounds. Retaliation is possible but uncommon in this set of personas; more likely is convergence to high cooperation after a few sanctioning rounds.
- Uncertainty: since players don't know how many rounds remain, behavior may not fully unravel at the end; small deviations (a one-off defection) can still occur as players occasionally test incentives.

Below is a simulated transcript from round 1 through round 8 showing predicted contributions, sparse chat, and the minimal, targeted punishment/reward behavior that fits these tendencies.

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a tension between the group interest (everyone contributing 20 raises total payoff a lot) and the individual incentive to free-ride (keeping 20 while still receiving a share). Because contributions are binary (0 or 20), players who typically endorse social norms and have higher patience/mental-accounting (e.g., GORILLA, PARROT, RABBIT, DUCK, DOG) are relatively likely to contribute early. Players with low mental-accounting or low patience (SLOTH, CHICK) are more likely to test the group by free-riding, especially early or in the final round. Punishment is costly (2 coins per punishment unit to the punisher) but reduces targets' earnings; so punishment tends to be used sparingly to enforce cooperation after defecting appears, and is less frequent in the last round because it has no future deterrence value. Expect initial cooperation by many, a few early free-rides, a few targeted punishments to establish norms, recovery to broad cooperation in mid-game, and some opportunistic defection in the final round with minimal retaliation.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits a small group against a classic social dilemma: each round players choose whether to keep their private 20 coins or contribute the full 20 to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among the 10 players, so a single 20-coin contribution raises everyone’s share by 12.4 but costs the contributor 20 — individually costly but collectively beneficial. Because players move simultaneously, contributors risk being exploited by free riders. Punishment is available but costly (2 coins per punishment unit to the sender, and 2 coins removed from the target per unit), so punishers trade their own money to reduce others’ payoffs. Over repeated rounds (6 total), we expect some initial cooperation from those inclined to cooperate (high patience / mental accounting), attempts to punish obvious free riders, and then a gradual decline in contributions as cooperating players learn that sparse punishment cannot fully offset the private incentive to defect. Punishment will be used sparingly and mostly by cooperators to signal disapproval; it will modestly reduce defectors’ gains but not always reverse the individual advantage of free-riding. Chat will be infrequent and used to coordinate or call out behavior.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This repeated public-goods game pits individual incentives (take the 20 privately) against group benefit (put 20 into the common pot). The common pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split among all seven players (MPCR = 2.6/7 ≈ 0.37), so contributing 20 lowers an individual's immediate payoff but raises everyone else's payoff; free-riding pays off short-term. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, end-game unwinding is uncertain. game_explanation:
This PGG is a small group (7 players) with a binary contribution choice each round: either keep the whole 20 (contribute 0) or leave it all in the public pot (contribute 20). The public pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so a lone cooperator reduces their private payoff relative to defectors unless many others also cooperate. Players cannot see choices before deciding, do not know the number of rounds, and can only influence others positively via costly rewards (each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Rewards thereby serve only to reinforce cooperation (no direct punishment).

Given the player personas, some are dispositionally more patient and rule-oriented (SLOTH, MOOSE, GORILLA, PARROT) and may try cooperation early. Risk-seeking or low-mental-accounting players (CHICKEN, DOG, DUCK) are more likely to withdraw the pot (defect). Early rounds will likely see a mix: a minority of cooperators and several defectors. Because defecting yields a higher immediate payoff when cooperators are few, cooperators' payoffs will often be lower and cooperation will tend to decline over time unless sustained reciprocity via rewards emerges. Rewards are costly, so expect them to be sparse and mainly directed among clear cooperators to signal appreciation. Chat will be used rarely: short signals like promises or brief complaints are most likely. Over eight rounds I expect initial cooperation by several patient/mental-accounting players, exploitation by defectors, small pockets of mutual rewarding among cooperators, and then a general drift toward defection — with occasional attempts to revive cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with punishment and reward available each round. Each player gets 20 coins and decides how much to contribute to the public pot (0–20). The pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can pay to punish (cost 3, -12 to the target per unit) or reward (cost 3, +2 to the target per unit). Players do not know how many rounds there will be.

Likely dynamics:
- Some players (CHICKEN, PARROT) have strong cooperative priors and will contribute high amounts consistently. Others (GORILLA) tend to contribute little. SLOTH, DOG, and DUCK are midrange and will respond to social feedback.
- Early rounds typically show relatively high cooperation from the committed cooperators and moderate contributions from conditional cooperators. Free-riding by one player (GORILLA) will draw sparse but targeted punishment early on; punishments will be used sparingly rather than every round.
- Rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce high contributors (CHICKEN, PARROT, sometimes SLOTH/DOG). Punishments target persistent low contributors; if they respond by increasing contributions, punishment will taper off.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful: short requests, call-outs, or gratitude. Players can identify punishers, so punishment is targeted rather than anonymous mobbing.
- Over 26 rounds, expect some natural fluctuation but not wholesale collapse: cooperators mostly stay high, conditional cooperators adjust slowly, and the chronic low contributor (GORILLA) remains low but sometimes raises small amounts after punishment.

This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round everyone gets 20 coins and privately chooses how many coins to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to the contributor via the group share — less than 1 — making free-riding individually attractive in a one-shot. However, players can reward others after seeing contributions: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Because rewards can compensate cooperators, they serve as a signaling and enforcement tool for cooperation across rounds.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically show variation: some players (CHICKEN, DUCK, DOG) lean toward higher contributions; others (SLOTH, PARROT) tend to contribute less; GORILLA remains moderate and responsive.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce high contributors rather than to “punish” defectors (negative transfers are not allowed). Expect 0–3 positive reward actions per round, usually directed toward reliably high contributors (CHICKEN, DUCK, DOG), sometimes toward a rising PARROT to encourage contribution.
- Because players do not know when the game ends, reciprocal reward patterns can sustain cooperation. We therefore predict contributions trending upward or stabilizing at moderate-to-high levels for cooperative players, while persistent free-riders (PARROT, SLOTH) continue low contributions but still benefit from the public pot.
- Chat will be sparse and strategic (short praise, requests, or comments), mostly right after contributions or summaries.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game pits short-term private incentives (withdrawing the full 20) against group payoffs from cooperation (contributing 20 to the public pot). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), the private gain from defecting is immediate and large, while cooperative benefits are diluted across the 7 players (the public sum is multiplied by 2.6 then split). Players do not know how many rounds remain, so reputation and reciprocity can sustain cooperation early on, but the high immediate advantage of free-riding usually causes cooperation to decline over time unless costly reward behavior effectively supports cooperators.

Given the player personas:
- GORILLA, CHICKEN, and PARROT are relatively patient and likely to initiate/maintain cooperation and to reward others modestly.
- MOOSE and DUCK are mixed; they may cooperate sometimes and respond to social cues.
- SLOTH and DOG are the least patient and are most likely to defect early and persist.
Rewards are costly (2 coins per unit to the sender and +2 to the recipient). In early rounds we expect a few targeted positive rewards to support cooperators; punishments (negative rewards) are not possible, so defectors are mainly deterred by withholding rewards and occasional targeted rewarding of cooperators. Over rounds, because defectors often keep a higher immediate payoff, cooperation tends to erode, with a slow drift toward universal defection. Chat messages should be sparse and used to encourage cooperation or signal intentions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six identifiable players but an unknown number of rounds. Each round players choose how much of 20 coins to put in a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After payoffs are revealed, players can spend coins to punish or reward others (punishment costs 3 to sender and removes 12 from the target per unit; reward costs 3 to sender and gives 2 to target per unit). Because punishment is costly to both sides (especially costly to the punished), we expect relatively sparse use of these instruments — mostly targeted at clear free-riders. Players with higher “mental accounting” and patience (SLOTH, GORILLA, PARROT) will tend toward steady, moderate-to-high contributions; DUCK and DOG have high pro-social tendencies in these priors and are likely to contribute high amounts but may occasionally test the group. CHICKEN, with lower patience and gain-seeking patterns, is the likeliest to try low contributions early on; others will respond with light punishments or selective rewards to enforce cooperation.

Across rounds we expect:
- Early rounds: some uncertainty and a small number of low contributions (temptation to free-ride). Quick, light punishments in response reduce future free-riding.
- Middle rounds: contributions converge toward moderately high levels (12–19), with occasional lapses punished sparsely.
- Rewards will be rarer than punishment but used selectively to reinforce cooperation (small positive units).
- Chat will be sparse and used mainly to signal intent or protest after perceived unfairness.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round repeated public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately chooses to keep 20 coins or contribute 20 to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players; each coin contributed yields 0.62 coins back to each player, so a contributor loses more privately (20 paid, only 12.4 returned to them from their own 20) but the group as a whole benefits if many contribute. The dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (keep your 20 and still share the public pot), while the group (and total payoff) is maximized if many or all contribute.

In repeated play, typical dynamics: some players try to build cooperation early (pledges, contributions), but visible payoffs and selfish incentives usually lead to some free-riding and a decline of contributions over rounds. Cheap talk (messages) can sustain cooperation briefly if people trust each other, but promises are inexpensive and can be broken. Near the final round, endgame incentives push more players to defect (free-ride). Uncertainty about who will stick with contributions and about reciprocity drives much of the variation.

Given the supplied personas, I predict a mix: a core of steady contributors (those with higher patience or stronger prosocial tendencies) and several free-riders who prefer to keep the 20 when others can be relied on to contribute. Chat will be sparse and mostly pledge/response or short complaints.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, binary contributions each round (0 or 20), a large multiplier (6.2) and costly one-way punishment (each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and reduces the target by 2). Typical dynamics: initial rounds often show substantial cooperation because contributors benefit from mutual returns; defectors (0 contributors) earn more in the short run and so are tempting. Punishment can sustain cooperation by deterring future defection, but punishment is costly and typically used sparingly — enough to shift incentives but not so much as to wipe out cooperators. There is also strategic uncertainty: players don’t observe others’ choices before deciding each round, and last-round incentives favor free-riding (no future retaliation). We should therefore expect:

- Early-round mix: many will contribute, a few may free-ride; those who are commonly cooperators may target visible defectors with modest punishment.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is effective, previously punished defectors often switch to contributing; cooperation tends to rebound.
- Final round: cooperation typically falls as the incentives to punish or to maintain reputation vanish; some players may still punish in the last round out of retribution, but less strategically.

Given the personas, I expect a majority of players will start by contributing, a couple of defectors early on (someone like GORILLA with low patience may free-ride), cooperation rebounds after targeted punishment, occasional single-player lapses occur mid-game, and a measurable drop in cooperation in round 6. Chat is sparse and mostly focused on calling out defection or thanking cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a classic public goods dilemma. Each round every player decides privately whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to a shared pot (20) or keep it (0). The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among the 10 players, so each 20 contributed increases everyone’s share by 20 * 6.2 / 10 = 12.4. Contributing therefore reduces the contributor’s private holding by 20 but returns only 12.4 to themselves — a personal loss of 7.6 compared to keeping the 20. The group optimum is if everyone contributes (each gets 124), but individually the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (keep the 20).

Likely dynamics: some patient or pro-social players start by contributing, hoping to build reciprocity. Free-riding is tempting because defectors earn more in any mixed group; rational players who see others free-riding tend to stop contributing. We therefore expect moderate initial cooperation followed by a gradual decline across rounds. Messaging can sustain cooperation briefly if it signals intent, but messages are sparse and often insufficient to prevent the downward trend. There is uncertainty about how many will reciprocate in each round, so some players (especially patient/high-trust types) may continue contributing for a few rounds before giving up.

Free-riding is tempting because defectors earn more in any mixed group; rational players who see others free-riding tend to stop contributing. We therefore expect moderate initial cooperation followed by a gradual decline across rounds. Messaging can sustain cooperation briefly if it signals intent, but messages are sparse and often insufficient to prevent the downward trend. There is uncertainty about how many will reciprocate in each round, so some players (especially patient/high-trust types) may continue contributing for a few rounds before giving up.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players and binary contribution choices (either contribute the full 20 or withdraw it all). The public pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so individual incentives favor withdrawing (free-riding) while group welfare favors universal contribution. Because players do not know the total number of rounds and can reward others at cost, we expect mixed behavior:

- Early rounds: many players begin cooperatively (contribute) to test reciprocity; a minority may free-ride immediately.
- Rewarding is the only enforcement mechanism. Expect sparse, targeted positive rewards (small units) to reinforce contributors and withhold rewards from defectors. Rewards are costly, so they’ll be used sparingly and often reciprocally among cooperators.
- As rounds progress, cooperation will likely erode if many free-ride persist (cooperators get low payoffs and stop contributing). If a small subset keeps cooperating and rewards each other, we may see stable cooperation among that subgroup while others free-ride.
- Chat will be sparse: short coordination, occasional calls-out of defectors, occasional apologies or offers to coordinate.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with simultaneous contributions and a costly reward stage. Key incentives:
- Contributing raises the shared pot: every coin contributed returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.6333 to each player — so a coin costs the contributor 1 but gives ≈0.633 to every player (including themself), creating a private cost and social benefit. The marginal private return is below 1, so pure self-interest favors contributing 0; social preferences push toward positive contributions.
- Rewards let players transfer help with leverage: each reward unit costs 3 to the sender and gives 2 to the receiver. Rewards thus can encourage cooperation but are themselves costly.
- Players don’t know how many rounds will be played, so conditional cooperation and reciprocity are plausible: people often start by testing others, then increase contributions if peers cooperate; reward is used sparingly to reinforce positive behavior.

With the provided personas:
- DUCK and PARROT are the most likely steady high contributors (patient, pro-social tendencies).
- CHICKEN, DOG and sometimes CHICKEN/DOG will be conditional cooperators—moderate to high contributions but responsive to others.
- SLOTH and GORILLA have more variability; they start lower and may increase if rewarded or see returns.
- Rewards will be sparse and targeted: small positive reinforcement to visible cooperators (1 unit) rather than mass rewarding. Punishment via reward withholding (not sending rewards) is more common than explicit negative transfers because only positive rewards are allowed.

Expect an early testing phase with a mix of high and low contributions, a modest pattern of 0–1 reward units to highlight contributors, and slow drift toward stable cooperation among the more pro-social players, with sporadic defections by SLOTH or GORILLA and targeted rewards to restore cooperation.

Chat will be sparse and used to signal intentions or call out patterns (thanks, requests, short appeals). Below I simulate a plausible 26-round history consistent with these tendencies.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds, and a binary contribution decision (0 or 20 each round). The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all players, so a single contribution of 20 yields 20*6.2/10 = 12.4 to each player (12.4 return on others plus a 0 return to the contributor's private holding). Because the game is finite (6 rounds), incentives to free-ride grow toward the end, but early rounds often see higher cooperation if players signal intentions or punish free-riders. Punishment is costly (2 coins per unit sent by the punisher) but reduces the target’s earnings by 2 per unit; it can sustain cooperation by imposing immediate costs on defectors. Players who were punished in earlier rounds are likely to shift toward contributing in subsequent rounds, especially if they care about avoiding future penalties. Chat messages are rare but can coordinate cooperation or signal intent to punish. Overall I expect initial cooperation from many players, a few sporadic defections that are met with targeted, sparse punishment, increasing cooperation mid-game, and a modest uptick in defection in the final round as forward-looking deterrence falls away.

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game creates a tension between private incentives (keep your 20) and group benefit (contribute 20 so the pot grows). Because contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among 10 players, each 20 contributed returns 12.4 to every player (20*6.2/10 = 12.4). That means a single contributor loses 7.6 relative to keeping the 20 if others' behavior is fixed; however, mutual cooperation raises everyone’s payoffs. Punishment lets players impose costs on defectors (each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and deducts 2 coins from the target). Punishment is costly, so we expect it to be used sparingly and mainly to enforce cooperation: early defectors may be punished to deter further free-riding, which tends to increase cooperation in subsequent rounds. Near the final round, the incentive to punish falls (no future rounds to enforce), so cooperation often drops again.

Given the player priors:
- Some players (OWL, CHICK, DUCK, MOOSE, RABBIT, CHICKEN) are inclined to contribute; others (GORILLA, PARROT, SLOTH, DOG) are more likely to defect or be inconsistent.
- Punishment will be used selectively by cooperators to target repeat or blatant free-riders, with modest intensities (1–2 units typical).
- Chats will be sparse and purposeful (brief warnings, announcements of punishment, or short normative remarks).

game_explanation:
This group faces the standard tension of a public-goods game: each coin you keep gives you 1 privately; each coin contributed returns 3.8 to the group and is split equally across six players, so each contributed coin yields ≈0.63 to each player — less than the private 1, so the one-shot incentive is to free-ride. Because players don't know the number of rounds, short-sighted free-riding can gradually erode cooperation. However, some players (GORILLA, PARROT) have priors that make them relatively more likely to contribute early; others (SLOTH, DOG) are more mixed or conservative. Rewards are costly to give (3 coins per reward unit) but deliver 2 coins to the target; they can be used sparingly to reward cooperative behavior or to try to sustain cooperation, but because rewards are net costly to the group they tend to be used strategically and infrequently.

I expect an initial phase where a few high-contributing players (GORILLA, PARROT, maybe DUCK) set a cooperative tone, with sparse messages encouraging cooperation. Some players will reciprocate partially, but contributions will slowly decline as players test whether others sustain high contributions. Rewards will appear rarely and mainly to reinforce consistently high contributors (especially GORILLA and PARROT). Over many rounds cooperation will drift downwards, with occasional late attempts (a small contribution or a targeted reward) to revive cooperation; in the final rounds, without knowledge of the end, many players will mostly free-ride and rewards will be rare.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 coins per round, contribution choices 0–20, a group multiplier of 3.8 on the sum of contributions, and a six-way equal split of the public pot. After contributions are revealed, costly punishment and costly reward are allowed: each punishment unit costs the sender 3 coins and removes 12 coins from the target; each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the target +2 coins. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so they tend to try to establish cooperation early (conditional cooperation) while also balancing the cost of sanctions. Because punishment is expensive to the sender but very damaging to the target, we expect punishment to be used sparingly and targeted at clear free-riders. Rewards are also costly and used sparingly to reinforce desirable behavior.

Using the provided player priors: SLOTH, CHICKEN, PARROT, and DUCK are biased toward relatively steady cooperation (higher patience and mental-accounting in some), while DOG looks most likely to under-contribute at times (low mental accounting) and GORILLA is mixed/variable. Expect early moderate contributions from most, occasional low contributions from DOG (and intermittent lapses from GORILLA), and a small number of directed punishments (mainly against DOG and the occasional GORILLA lapse) plus occasional one-unit rewards to reinforce good contributors. Chats are rare and usually short reminders or calls for cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods setting with small groups (7 players) and a stark binary choice each round: either withdraw the full 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so individual incentives favor withdrawing (free-riding) while group incentives favor contributing. Players cannot see others' choices before deciding, but can send messages and can reward others after contributions are revealed. Rewards are costly to send (2 coins per reward unit) and benefit the target by the same amount (2 coins per unit), so rewards are strictly transfers used to encourage or thank cooperators (they cannot directly punish).

Given the player personas, expect a mixture of stable cooperators (high patience and mental-accounting, e.g., GORILLA, PARROT, DOG) and some conditional or opportunistic players who may test cooperation early (SLOTH, MOOSE, DUCK). CHICKEN appears more likely to favor private gain (lower loss-avoidance), so may free-ride more often. Because players do not know the number of rounds, signalling early (promises in chat) and small targeted rewards to cooperators are plausible strategies to sustain cooperation. Rewards will be used sparingly, typically 1 unit to thank or reinforce cooperative behavior, and chat will be brief and purposeful (requests for cooperation, a short thank-you, or calling out repeated free-riding).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 20 coins each round, and a potent group multiplier (8.1) that is split equally across all players. Individually, every coin you keep gives you 1 coin; every coin you contribute returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62 to yourself — so the private incentive is to free-ride (keep coins), while the collective optimum is for players to contribute more. Players decide simultaneously and privately each round, but at the end of the round each player observes their own net payoff; because they also know their own contribution, that private payoff lets them infer the total group contribution for the round (they cannot see who contributed what). That inference is enough to update beliefs about the group's average contribution even though individual identities remain hidden.

Given the player personas, I expect a reasonably cooperative opening (several players have high patience/risk-taking or strong mental-accounting cues that correlate with initial contributions), plus a few low contributors from the start (those with low patience or consistently loss-averse / low gain tendencies). Once the first few rounds reveal a lower-than-socially-optimal total (via private payoffs), many conditional cooperators will reduce their contributions in response, producing a steady decline across rounds. A few players (notably highly patient or strongly pro-social personas) will remain above the group midpoint for longer, creating a long tail of small but nonzero contributions through the middle rounds; by the end most players will converge near zero contributions.

Uncertainties: because players only see their own payoff, they may interpret the same total in different ways (as stable cooperation or luck), so reactions can vary; also the exact magnitudes of adjustments depend on individual updating rules. Below I simulate one plausible path consistent with these priors: moderate opening contributions, visible decline over rounds as free-riding dominates, with a small persistent minority of contributors (especially PARROT and a couple others) holding on longer.

Conditional cooperators trim slightly; the most self-interested (DOG, COW) remain near zero. PARROT, MOOSE and other patient types stay relatively high but may reduce a touch to test others.

Totals continue falling as free-riding becomes the norm (players now expect low average contributions). A few high-patience/gain-seeking players (PARROT, MOOSE) keep contributing to preserve some public good, but even they trim a bit.

Most players now see the trend and move toward low contributions; a small cluster (PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT) are the only ones contributing amounts that keep the pot nonzero. Some players drop to 0 as they prefer the private return.

The inferred total continues to shrink (players see their own payoffs reflect a smaller pot). Many settle on minimal contributions; PARROT and a couple others maintain modest positive contributions out of patience or pro-social preference.

By now the pot is appreciably smaller; players who were conditional cooperators shrink their offers further or hold steady at low amounts. A few players give 1–3 coins to keep some public good alive; many contribute 0.

Mid-to-late game: reputational concerns don’t apply (no communication, no identities revealed across rounds), so incentives to free-ride dominate. Several players go to zero; PARROT and a couple of others still contribute small amounts out of intrinsic preference.

Further drift toward zero. Some players who tried small tests earlier now stay at 0 after seeing small returns. A small minority (PARROT, MOOSE) still contribute small positive amounts; a few others give 1–2 coins occasionally.

Approaching the final third of the game, most players converge near zero: the public pot is small and private incentives dominate. PARROT and a couple others maintain minimal contributions out of intrinsic or patient tendencies.

With very small inferred totals, almost everyone gives nothing; a few patient/pro-social players still place 1–4 coins to sustain any group payoff. The pattern is stable: low pot, low incentive to contribute.
Near the endgame, nearly all players give zero; a couple of remaining contributors lower their amounts as the overall benefit shrinks. Expect almost all zeros with small exceptions.
Final two rounds: contributions are minimal. Some patient players might keep contributing a token amount out of fairness or habit, but most exploit the private return.

Final round. No future consequences and the public return per coin is below the private return, so most players choose 0. A small minority (here PARROT) may still give a token amount out of intrinsic preference; others stick to zero.

game_explanation:
This is a classic repeated public goods game with 10 players, large multiplier (6.6) but the sharing rule (split across 10 people) makes individual marginal return per coin 0.66 < 1. That creates a social dilemma: if everyone contributed all 20, group payoffs would be very large, but each coin individually costs more to the contributor than it returns to them. Players therefore face tension between short-run selfish incentives to free-ride and longer-run motives (conditional cooperation, reciprocity, intrinsic pro-social preferences). Because players do not see others' contributions and only see their own round payoff, learning about the group is noisy; messages are the main channel for coordinating expectations. Players do not know how many rounds remain, increasing the weight of long-run motives for more patient types and making abrupt final-round defections less certain.

Given the player personas:
- Some (MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, PARROT) are relatively patient and/or have high mental accounting; they’ll tend to start fairly cooperative and reduce slowly.
- Some (GORILLA, DUCK, DOG) are mixed risk/gain types and may experiment or reduce contributions earlier to exploit.
- Others (SLOTH, CHICKEN, CHICK) are cautious, adapting gradually.
- Expect an initial phase of moderate-to-high contributions driven by optimism and conditional cooperators, then a gradual decline as the private feedback (own payoff) and a few low contributions encourage freeriding. Sparse but purposeful chat may propose cooperation early, complain mid-game, and justify reductions later. Because payoffs are private, coordination to sustain cooperation is harder, so contributions decline but not collapse instantly.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round, players choose an integer contribution 0–20 from a private endowment of 20; the group pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split evenly. After seeing contributions, players can spend coins to punish specific peers; punishments are costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and costly to the punished (5 coins per unit), and players learn who punished them. Players do not know the total number of rounds while playing, which tends to encourage conditional cooperation and experimentation: many players will initially contribute moderately as a signal of goodwill, then adjust based on others' behavior. Punishment is typically used sparingly to discipline persistent low contributors or to respond to unexpected defections; it can sustain higher cooperation but also risks escalation if misused.

Given the provided personas, I predict a mixed but gradually cooperative dynamic. Some avatars (e.g., CHICKEN, CHICK, DUCK) are predisposed to higher contributions or risk-taking and will often contribute generously as a coordination anchor. Others (e.g., DOG, MOOSE) start more conservatively and may free-ride early; they are likely to be targeted by punishment early on and then increase contributions. Punishment is used selectively—mainly directed at clear under-contributors—so average contributions rise over the first several rounds and then stabilize at a moderate-to-high level as players learn they will be disciplined for free-riding. Chat is sparse and mostly motivational or reactive (calling out free-riders, promising to punish, brief apologies).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round, and a highly productive public multiplier (6.6) that returns 0.66 coins per contributed coin to each player. Individually, each coin contributed reduces your private holding by 1 while raising your per-person return by 0.66, so the dominant individual incentive is to contribute 0 (defection), while the group optimum is that everyone contributes all 20. Because players see only their own net payoff after each round (not others' payoffs) but do know their own contribution, they can infer the group's total contribution from their observed net each round. That gives noisy but useful feedback enabling conditional strategies (like "if group sum drops, I will punish by contributing less"). Cheap-talk (chat) is allowed and can help coordinate cooperation early on, but it is nonbinding and some players may defect despite promises.

Given the player personas, I expect an initial burst of optimistic/cooperative contributions from several players (especially those with high patience/gain tendencies like DOG, RABBIT, MOOSE), mixed with lower initial contributions from more cautious or low-mental-accounting profiles (PARROT, DUCK). Over rounds, free-riding and uncertainty about others' intentions will lead to at least one visible defection which will be inferred from the private payoff signals and will trigger a downward drift in contributions. Occasional chat attempts at coordination will temporarily restore some cooperation, but overall contributions will trend down and approach low levels by the end of the (unknown-length) sequence. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to state promises, call out free-riders, or signal intentions.

A few avatars (notably those with lower patience or more risk-seeking cues) sometimes test lower contributions. When an obvious low contribution appears, other players—especially those with strong mental-accounting or high loss sensitivity—tend to punish, but punishment is costly and therefore used sparingly and mainly against clear outliers. Over rounds we should see a pattern of occasional defections followed by targeted punishments that bring contributors back toward the group norm; by the end of the 16 rounds, contributions converge toward higher, more cooperative levels.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a high multiplier (8.1) on the group pot. Each round each player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute (0–20). The pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among the 13 players; each player’s round payoff is (20 − own contribution) + (8.1 * total contributions / 13). The social optimum is everyone contributing everything (maximizes group payoff). The individual incentive in any given round is to contribute less than others (free-ride), because the private cost of contributing (1 coin foregone) yields only a 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 coin return to oneself per coin contributed — smaller than 1, so individually costly but socially beneficial.

Key likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: without direct peer visibility, players form priors from their types and the game structure; many conditional cooperators or loss-averse types will start with moderate-to-high contributions, while high gain-seeking or low mental-accounting types may free-ride from the start.
- Mid-game: because players only see their own net payoff (not others’ choices), they infer group behavior from that payoff and may slowly adjust. Expect a gradual decline in average contributions as free-riding dominates or uncertainty about others’ cooperation persists.
- Endgame: in the last several rounds, a classic “endgame” incentive to free-ride becomes stronger (no future rounds to reciprocate), producing lower contributions.
- Heterogeneity: some players (stable cooperators in persona) will keep contributing at moderate levels despite declines; some habitual free-riders (e.g., high gain-lottery, low accounting types) will remain near zero.

Uncertainties: Because players receive only private payoff feedback (not individual contributions of others), learning is noisy — players may misattribute their payoff to their own choice vs. group behavior, producing gradual and sometimes inconsistent changes in contributions.

Below is a simulated transcript of the 16-round game consistent with these incentives and the player personas.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round every player chooses how much of their private 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split evenly. Individually, a contributed coin returns 0.66 to the contributor through the pot (less than keeping it), so purely selfish incentives favor free-riding. However, many players in this group have priors (high patience, high loss-sensitivity, or strong mental accounting) that incline them toward conditional cooperation and norm enforcement: they will tend to contribute more when others do, and use punishment sparingly to discipline low contributors.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some variability. A few players (e.g., CHICKEN, PARROT, RABBIT, CHICK) start high and act as norm enforcers; a few (e.g., DUCK, SLOTH) begin with lower contributions. Enforcers may issue targeted, low-cost punishments to low contributors to signal that free-riding will be sanctioned.
- Middle rounds: contributions trend upward as punished players increase contributions to avoid future sanctions. Punishment frequency declines as cooperation strengthens.
- Late rounds: with no announced end, cooperative norms tend to stabilize; punishment becomes rare and used only for notable lapses. Occasional small defections may happen and be met with a single punitive response.

Punishment is costly to both sides (2 coins per unit to the source; 5 coins deducted per unit from the target), so players use it sparingly — typically 1–2 units per instance — and mostly against clear low contributors. Chat is sparse: short calls for fairness or praise for cooperation.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 players against a standard social dilemma: each coin contributed to the public pot is multiplied by 8.1 and shared equally, so each contributed coin returns only ~0.62 to the contributor but benefits the group. That creates an incentive to free-ride (keep your 1 coin and let others pay), while punishment provides a costly enforcement tool (3 coins spent by the punisher removes 7 from the target) that can sustain higher cooperation but is itself costly and therefore used sparingly.

Given the player personas, I expect a mix of steady cooperators (players with strong mental-accounting or loss-avoidance cues like PARROT, CHICK, and CROCODILE) who contribute fairly high amounts early on; conditional cooperators (many with medium patience) who mirror social signals; and a few opportunistic/low contributors who test the group (e.g., occasional very low contributions from SNAKE or MOOSE). Because contributions are revealed before punishment, initial punishment is likely targeted at obvious low contributors. Punishment will be used infrequently (a handful of single-unit actions), mostly early-to-mid game to deter persistent low contributions. As the game nears the final rounds, the endgame effect will push many players to reduce contributions, and players will be less willing to pay the cost of punishment when there is no future leverage — so punishment declines toward the end.

The transcript below simulates these dynamics over 16 rounds. Each round shows a short explanation of expected motives and adjustments, the contributions (in the exact player order), and any punishment tuples (source, target, unit). Punishment is sparse, targeted, and largely aimed at persistent low contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) from their endowment of 20. The sum of contributions is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across the 13 players. After each round every player sees only their own net payoff for that round (they do not see others' individual contributions), but because they know their own contribution they can infer the per-player share and thus the group's total contribution that round. That inference provides group-level feedback (not individual-level), so players can update expectations about how cooperative the group was.

Typical dynamics I expect here:
- Early rounds: many players start moderately cooperative (near the group's average), while some predisposed free-riders keep contributions low. The inferred total is visible to everyone (via their private payoff) so conditional cooperators will try to match what they infer others did.
- Mid game: there is usually a drift downward as free-riding becomes more tempting and conditional cooperators reduce contributions in response to lower inferred totals.
- Late rounds / endgame: because there are no future penalties for defecting and the game length is known, contributions tend to stay low or drop further; some steady cooperators may keep giving small amounts but most players prefer to keep private coins.
- Individual heterogeneity: personas incline some players to remain more cooperative (high patience, high loss-aversion or strong mental accounting may increase consistent contributions) while others (very low loss aversion, high gain-seeking, or low concern for public investments) tend to free-ride.

I use the provided personas as soft priors. For example:
- Players with high mental-accounting and patience (SLOTH, CHICKEN, COW, CROCODILE) start moderately and then adjust.
- Very risk-averse or loss-sensitive players who dislike gambles (e.g., RABBIT, PARROT) may contribute more early on.
- High gain-lottery or opportunistic profiles (GORILLA, DUCK) may contribute variably; Gorilla's very low loss-lottery score and earlier low contributions bias toward free-riding.
Overall, I predict a moderate initial total and then a gradual decline in average contributions across the 16 rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, each given 20 coins every round. Contributing helps the group (each coin contributed adds 6.6 total to the pot and thus 0.66 to each player), but costs the contributor 1 coin privately. The dominant private strategy each round is to contribute 0; cooperation requires players to accept a private loss for collective gain. Punishment allows players to impose costly sanctions: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 5 coins from the punished player's round payoff. Punishment can support cooperation by deterring free-riding, but it is also costly and sometimes targeted selectively.

Uncertainties for players include not knowing how many rounds remain and not seeing others' choices prior to deciding. That typically encourages some initial cooperation (hoping others will reciprocate) and then either decay toward lower contributions or stabilization at a moderate-to-high cooperation level if punishment effectively deters free-riding. Players with strong mental-accounting or norm-enforcement tendencies (e.g., OWL, PARROT, GORILLA, CHICK) are more likely to punish persistent low contributors. Risk-seeking or opportunistic players (e.g., RABBIT) may try free-riding intermittently. Punishment will be sparse and targeted rather than widespread because it’s costly; chat messages will be few and typically aimed at signaling expectations or calling out low contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round, players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to each player. Because that per-player return is less than 1, the individually dominant action is to keep all 20 coins (contribute 0), while the group optimum is full contribution. The presence of costly punishment (3 coins cost to the punisher per unit, 7 coins deducted from the target per unit) makes punishment an effective deterrent but also costly to use, so players typically punish sparingly and selectively — usually punishing persistent or extreme free-riders rather than minor deviations.

Expectations across rounds:
- Early rounds: many players with cooperative priors (high patience, strong mental accounting) will contribute moderately or highly, creating a pattern of initial cooperation. Risk-takers or low mental-accounting players (e.g., SLOTH, CROCODILE, CHICKEN, CHICK) are more likely to contribute little early on.
- Punishment: because punishers are identifiable and punishment is costly, it will be used intermittently — mostly targeted at clear, repeated low contributors. A few players (those who value cooperation and have higher patience) will absorb punishment costs to discourage severe defectors.
- Dynamics over time: contributions typically drift downward as the game's finite horizon and temptation to free-ride grow. Punishment can slow the decline by deterring extreme free-riding, but becomes less credible near the final rounds (end-game effect), so contributions fall toward the end.
- Uncertainties: individual idiosyncrasies matter. Because punishment is expensive to the punisher here, we should see relatively few punishment actions overall.
reflection:
This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round, players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to each player. Because that per-player return is less than 1, the individually dominant action is to keep all 20 coins (contribute 0), while the group optimum is full contribution. The presence of costly punishment (3 coins cost to the punisher per unit, 7 coins deducted from the target per unit) makes punishment an effective deterrent but also costly to use, so players typically punish sparingly and selectively — usually punishing persistent or extreme free-riders rather than minor deviations.

- Early rounds: many players with cooperative priors (high patience, strong mental accounting) will contribute moderately or highly, creating a pattern of initial cooperation. Risk-takers or low mental-accounting players (e.g., SLOTH, CROCODILE, CHICKEN, CHICK) are more likely to contribute little early on.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20-coin endowments each round, a pot multiplier of 6.6, and players do not know when the game will end. The per-coin private return to a contributor is 6.6/10 = 0.66 coins, which is less than the 1 coin they sacrifice when contributing. Thus individual incentives favor free-riding (contribute 0), while the group optimum is full contribution (20 each). Because players do not know the horizon, conditional cooperation (matching others when contributions are high) and occasional attempts to coordinate via chat are both plausible. Messages can sustain cooperation temporarily, but selfish incentives and uncertainty typically cause contributions to fluctuate and trend downward over many rounds; occasional spikes can appear when a persuasive or patient player advocates cooperation.

Given the provided personas, expect a mix: GORILLA and OWL (high patience) occasionally push bigger contributions; high mental-accounting players (SLOTH, DOG, MOOSE, PARROT) may treat the endowment as something to budget and sometimes contribute more; low gain-lottery profiles (PARROT, DUCK, CHICKEN) may be cautious or risk-averse in different ways that could reduce their willingness to experiment with high contributions. Chat will be sparse and strategic (requests, complaints, encouragements). The transcript below simulates likely contributions, short chat acts, and the private summary each player sees at the end of each round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with ten players who each get 20 coins per round and choose an integer contribution 0–20. The total contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and divided equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.66 to each player (a private return of 1 vs. a social marginal return of 0.66). Individually it is always optimal to contribute 0, but groups often sustain positive contributions early because of reciprocity, patience, and norms. Because players do not know the number of rounds, endgame collapse is less predictable; cooperation tends to decline gradually if some players start defecting or if people observe low rewards for contributing.

Given the supplied personas, I expect many players to start with moderate-to-high contributions (pledges and norm-following), with a subset (especially OWL) inclined to free-ride early. Over rounds we’ll likely see a slow decay in contributions as conditional cooperators reduce their giving after seeing low group-level signals or free-riding, with sparse, purposeful chat (pledges and occasional complaints). Chat is mostly used early to coordinate, and occasionally later to call out freeriding or to report one’s own payoff (players only see their own payoff at summary, but they may choose to disclose it in chat).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a multiplier (8.1) that makes each coin put into the public pot worth 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to the contributing individual. Private incentives therefore favor free-riding (keeping coins) because each coin you contribute lowers your private payoff by about 0.377 on net, even though group welfare is higher when people contribute. With no pre-play communication and only private feedback (each player sees only their own net payoff each round), dynamics typically move from some initial uncertainty and occasional cooperation toward low contributions as players infer others are free-riding.

Expectations and likely dynamics in this particular group:
- Early rounds: some players (particularly those with high patience or pro-social tendencies) may try moderate contributions to test others. Risk-seeking or high-gain-pref players may experiment with higher contributions early on.
- Middle rounds: as players decode group behavior from their own payoffs and see that the private return from contributing is negative, most will reduce contributions toward zero; a few conditional cooperators may keep contributing small amounts.
- Late rounds: toward the end, without future reciprocity, most players will defect (contribute 0). A small stable minority of cooperators (e.g., high-patience avatars) may still contribute small positive amounts in some rounds.

Uncertainty: individual personas (patience, gain/loss tendencies, mental accounting) inform likely tendencies but are not deterministic. Small variation and occasional small contributions are plausible throughout; the dominant pattern will be a steady decline toward mostly zero contributions.

game_explanation: This is a 13-player repeated public goods game with known horizon (16 rounds). Each coin contributed returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 coins to each player, so the private marginal return (<1) gives a strong short-run incentive to free-ride while the group return (>1 per coin in total) creates a collective incentive to contribute. Punishment is available but costly (3 coins per unit to the punisher, 7 coins lost by target), and players can identify who punished them. Typical dynamics: early rounds show uncertainty and some conditional cooperation (moderate contributions), occasional targeted punishment of clear low contributors stabilizes higher contributions in the middle rounds, and contributions tend to decline toward the end as backward induction reduces the incentive to punish and to contribute. Individual differences (patience, risk preferences, mental accounting) create heterogeneity: patient, account-keeping players (high mental accounting) tend to contribute more and punish free-riding sparingly; risk-seeking players are more likely to defect early. Punishment is expected to be used sparingly — enough to signal norms but not so much as to erode net payoffs.

Contributions decline modestly as end approaches. Players who view punishment as too costly relative to short remaining horizon reduce punitive actions; targeted punishment still occurs when a player drops notably below the prevailing level.

Penultimate phase: several players reduce contributions more sharply, anticipating final-round defection. A couple of last attempts at norm enforcement appear, but punishers are more constrained by the remaining rounds.

Near the end, cooperation unravels further. Punishment becomes less attractive (costs are immediate while deterrence of future rounds is small), so expect much lower contribution and only occasional small punitive acts.
With only one round left, backward induction predicts significant defection: contributions fall very low. Punishment is typically absent because its cost cannot be recouped via future cooperation.
Final round: nearly all players minimize contributions (defection dominant). Punishment is very unlikely as there is no future to influence.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and strong but individually suboptimal group returns: each coin put into the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and then split across 13 players, so each coin increases every player's share by about 0.623. That means the private return to contributing one more coin is negative (you lose 1 coin but gain ~0.623), so individual incentives favor free-riding. However, because players observe their own net payoff at the end of each round, they can infer the group's total contribution (though not who contributed what) and update expectations about others' cooperativeness. With heterogeneous priors (personas), I predict:

- Early rounds: many patient / prosocial-seeming players (GORILLA, MOOSE, OWL, PARROT, RABBIT, CHICKEN, SLOTH) will start with moderate-to-high contributions, while impulsive or opportunistic profiles (DOG, CROCODILE, SNAKE) will contribute little. This yields a reasonably high total in round 1.
- Middle rounds: once totals are revealed via each player's private payoff, conditional cooperators will adjust — sustaining cooperation if the inferred total is high, but scaling back if they believe others are freeriding. Because contributors can't identify free-riders, decreases are staggered but persistent.
- Late rounds: the standard end-game effect and continued incentive to free-ride will produce steadily lower contributions. Players with stable mental-accounting or strong reciprocal tendencies (MOOSE, PARROT, GORILLA to some extent) will reduce contributions more slowly; impatient or opportunistic players (DOG, CROCODILE, SNAKE) stay low throughout.
- Uncertainty and noisy priors produce some smoothing and slow learning rather than abrupt collapse. Because the private-payoff feedback fully identifies total contributions, adjustments are fairly consistent across rounds.

I simulate a plausible path consistent with these tendencies: moderate-high initial contributions, gradual decline across 16 rounds, with variation by persona.
