Final-round (end-game) effect: many players minimize contributions because there's no future to incentivize reciprocity. A few keep a small positive contribution if they have stable accounting preferences or intrinsic cooperativeness, but totals are at their lowest.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (keep your coin: each coin you keep yields +1 private) against a social multiplier (every coin placed in the pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split among the 10 players, so one contributed coin returns 6.6/10 = 0.66 to each contributor including you). The private marginal return (1) exceeds the private share of the public return (0.66), so a single-round selfish best reply is to contribute 0. Players who value cooperation, fairness, or who treat money in strict mental-accounting terms may contribute substantial amounts early on hoping others will reciprocate. Because players do not see others' choices, information is limited: each player only sees their own round payoff, not others' contributions or payoffs. This uncertainty makes it harder to sustain cooperation — typical patterns are initial moderate cooperation by intrinsically pro-social or patient players, followed by gradual decay as some free-ride and others respond by cutting contributions. Sparse chat can help coordinate, but promises are cheap and unverifiable; players with high patience/mental accounting (e.g., CHICKEN, DOG, RABBIT, SLOTH) are likelier to attempt stable contributions early, while high gain-seeking or low-accounting players (e.g., MOOSE, OWL, PARROT, GORILLA) are likelier to under-contribute. Because round count is unknown, we do not expect an end-game spike; instead, contributions usually drift toward low levels over repeated anonymous rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with costly punishment but no communication constraints beyond chat. Each coin you keep yields 1 privately; each coin you contribute yields 6.6 split among 10 players, so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to you — a private loss (−0.34) but a net benefit to the group. Without enforcement, the one-shot best response is to contribute 0; with repeated interaction and punishment, contributions can be sustained because defectors can be made worse off via punishment even though punishment is costly for the sender.

Expected dynamics here:
- Early rounds: many players with high patience and mental-accounting tendencies (DOG, CHICK, CHICKEN, PARROT, CHICK, RABBIT, MOOSE) start relatively high to signal cooperation; some more self-regarding types (GORILLA) may free-ride immediately.
- After initial contributions, cooperative players notice free-riding and selectively punish the biggest defectors. Punishment is visible (players learn who punished them), so punishment is an effective deterrent when used.
- Punishment is costly, so players use it sparingly and usually only against clear or repeated free-riders. Expect a few targeted punishments that shift behavior back toward higher contributions.
- Chat is sparse and typically used to coordinate expectations or call out free-riders; messages mostly occur right after a round where a defection is obvious or after a punishment.
- Over time, expect a moderate-to-high steady-state contribution level sustained by occasional, targeted punishments whenever individuals test the boundaries.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and fixed endowments of 20 coins per round. Each coin contributed to the shared pot is multiplied by 8.1 and then split equally among the 13 active players; that implies a private marginal return of 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 per contributed coin. Because 0.623 < 1, each player has a strict individual incentive to contribute 0 in every round (free-riding). However, if many players contribute, everyone’s absolute payoffs can be higher than 20 in that round. Players cannot see others’ contributions; after each round they only see their own net payoff for that round, which makes inference about others’ behavior noisy and coordination difficult.

Given the incentives and the limited feedback, a common unfolding is: initial experimentation (some players contribute nonzero amounts out of altruism, optimism, or uncertainty), then rapid learning that contributing reduces one’s own payoff (since private return per coin is <1), and convergence toward low contributions (often zero). Some players with stronger intrinsic prosocial tendencies or who “test” generosity sporadically may keep contributing small amounts occasionally, and occasional small experiments may appear later in the game. Uncertainty comes from limited feedback (private-only payoff signals) and heterogeneous priors about others’ cooperativeness; this produces variation in early rounds and mostly decay of contributions thereafter.

Some optimistic or prosocial players test cooperation by contributing substantial amounts; more self-interested players contribute little or nothing. Because no one yet has a private payoff history for comparison, we expect a mix of mid-to-high contributions from cooperative-types and zeros from obvious free-riders.

After Round 1 payoffs, players who contributed highly and observed relatively lower own payoffs (compared with non-contributors) tend to reduce contributions. Some conditional cooperators reduce moderately rather than immediately to zero; a few keep modest contributions to test.

Players observe that lowering contributions often increases their own payoff (because private return per unit <1). Many reduce further; a few still contribute small positive amounts as cautious experiments.

Convergence toward minimal contributions continues: most players contribute zero while a handful hedge with tiny amounts. With only private payoffs to learn from, players see diminishing returns from contributing and abandon large contributions.
As payoffs get close to the level of not contributing, almost everyone either contributes zero or only trivially. A few players with stronger prosocial priors or curiosity run very small tests.
Given the preceding payoffs, the dominant incentive is clear: contribute zero. Many players settle on zero contributions—this is the single-shot Nash action and stable under private feedback.
Some players occasionally “re-test” cooperation or experiment with small contributions despite the incentive to free-ride. A few small contributions appear while most remain at zero.
The occasional experiments do not produce sustained higher private payoffs for contributors, so most revert to the dominant strategy of zero contribution.

A few players make another small experiment—possibly those with stronger intrinsic pro-sociality or curiosity—while most stay at zero, reflecting a largely stabilized free-riding equilibrium with occasional deviations.
Seeing that small contributions only slightly change private payoffs (and often lower them relative to contributing zero), nearly everyone reverts to zero contributions again.

Late-stage small experiments persist among a minority: some players with enduring cooperative tendencies try again with modest contributions; others remain at zero since free-riding is individually optimal.
Most players again choose zero; after repeated private feedback showing little gain from contributing, zero is generally reinforced.
Minor experiments appear again from a handful of players who may be guided by intrinsic preferences or curiosity; however the majority stay at zero as it remains individually dominant.
With only a few rounds left and persistent private evidence that non-contribution is best, players overwhelmingly choose zero; endgame effects reinforce free-riding.
In the penultimate round, dominant incentives and accumulated private evidence keep contributions at zero for nearly everyone; the game is effectively in the free-rider equilibrium.

Final-round behavior often reflects either endgame free-riding or personal preferences; a small set of players with persistent pro-social tendencies may make a last-minute contribution even though it cannot be reciprocated.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game gives each player 20 coins per round. Each round a player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20. The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among all 10 players. Because each coin contributed costs the individual 1 coin but returns only 6.6/10 = 0.66 to that same individual, the private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). The social optimum is full contribution by everyone (maximizing total group payout), but without enforceable contracts or individual feedback about others' choices, cooperation is fragile.

Uncertainties players face here:
- They do not know how many rounds remain (so strategies that rely on end-game free-riding are less certain).
- They do not see others' contributions before deciding, and the summary only shows each player their own net payoff (players do not see peers' payoffs or contributions), so direct reciprocity and targeted punishment are limited.
- Chat is available, which allows promises and coordination attempts but is cheap talk (unenforceable). Some players will use it to try to sustain cooperation; others may exploit it.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix of persistent cooperators (those with strong mental-accounting tendencies who treat the endowment as “for the group”), conditional cooperators who respond to norms/messages, and a consistent minority of low-accounting or opportunistic players who contribute little. Early rounds will likely see moderate contributions bolstered by a few pledges. Over repeated uncertified rounds, contributions will drift down as free-riding dominates; occasional chat interventions can cause brief rebounds, but the long-run trend is downward.

game_explanation:
This 16-round public goods game will show the usual tension between private incentives to free-ride and social incentives to contribute for the group benefit. Early rounds typically feature fairly high contributions from cooperative/mental-accounting types and some low contributions from a few self-interested or impulsive players. When obvious free-riding appears, a small number of players (those who are patient, sensitive to social norms, or loss-averse) will pay the cost to punish defectors — punishment is costly, so it tends to be sparse and targeted. Punishment often reduces further free-riding for a while, but it can provoke occasional retaliation from punished players. Over the 16 rounds, expect cooperation to be relatively stronger in the middle rounds (after norm enforcement) and to decline toward the end as the finite horizon becomes salient (players reduce contributions in late rounds because future reciprocity no longer matters). Throughout, punishment is used primarily to enforce norms rather than to be frequent, and most players will punish only occasionally.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game gives each player 20 coins per round and a private incentive to keep coins (since each coin returned to the group yields only 6.6/10 = 0.66 back to the contributor). Left unchecked, the dominant selfish action is to contribute 0. However, players can punish after seeing contributions: punishment is costly for the punisher (2 coins per unit) but imposes a larger cost on the target (5 coins per unit). This creates scope for a minority of “enforcers” to sustain higher contributions by deterring low contributions, at the expense of some of their own resources. Players with high mental-accounting or high patience are more likely to contribute consistently; risk- and loss-sensitive players may also be willing to punish free-riding.

Given the personas, I expect an initial mixture of moderate contributions from conscientious players (CHICK, MOOSE, DOG, CHICKEN, RABBIT) and low contributions from more selfish/impulsive players (GORILLA, perhaps OWL early). A few players (notably CHICK, MOOSE, DOG, CHICKEN) will occasionally use small punishments to target persistent low contributors, leading to rising average contributions over time. Punishment will be sparse and targeted rather than constant. Chat messages will be few and purposeful (requests to contribute, short complaints when punished, occasional encouragement).

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated anonymous public goods game. Each round every player gets 20 coins and privately chooses how many to contribute (0–20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 8.1, then split evenly among the 13 players. So each player’s round payoff = (20 − own contribution) + (8.1 * total contributions / 13). Important incentives and likely dynamics:

- Free-riding is attractive when others contribute: because the multiplied pot is shared equally, someone who contributes less retains more private coins while still receiving the same share of the public benefit as contributors. If many others contribute, low contributors get higher net payoffs.
- Conditional cooperation and mental-accounting cues: players who treat money as earmarked (high mental-accounting) or who are more patient may be more likely to contribute early. Players with very low mental-accounting or strong tendencies toward risk/short-term choice are more likely to contribute little.
- Learning under limited feedback: players only see their own net payoff each round (not other players’ contributions). From that, they can infer the total public return (since they know their own contribution), but they can’t observe individual contributions. Early rounds often show higher average contributions if some participants start cooperative; once free-riding appears profitable (cooperators get lower net payoffs than low contributors), many will reduce contributions over time. Because players can't directly see others' contributions, the decline can be rapid once the pattern of payoffs indicates low returns to cooperation.
- Uncertainty: initial rounds can vary depending on how many start cooperative. Later rounds typically settle into a low-contribution equilibrium. Toward the very end, there is little future to incentivize cooperation, so contributions often fall further.

Given the provided personas, I predict an initial moderate-to-high average contribution driven by patient/mental-accounting players (SLOTH, CHICKEN, CROCODILE, COW, CHICK). Players with low mental-accounting or very short-term tendencies (DUCK, PARROT, SNAKE) will contribute little or zero. Observing relatively high payoffs as free riders in early rounds will push many to reduce contributions, producing a steady decline toward near-zero contributions by the middle rounds, with only a few small residual contributions from the most cooperative types. By the final rounds contributions are expected to be zero.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments (20 coins each round), anonymous simultaneous contributions, a high multiplier (6.6) split equally, and a costly punishment stage. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so there is no known end-game; that typically supports stable, conditional cooperation over time rather than unconditional free-riding at the end. Punishment allows cooperators to discipline low contributors but is costly (2 coins per punishment unit to the punisher; each unit subtracts 5 coins from the punished), so punishment tends to be used sparingly and mostly by players who care about maintaining high group contributions or who feel wronged.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: some uncertainty; a few players will contribute generously (high patience/mental-accounting types), while a few will test the waters with low contributions.
- After seeing revealed contributions, cooperators will occasionally use punishment to signal that low contributions are costly. Punishment will be modest (mostly 1 punishment unit per incident) because it is costly and identification of who punished you provides feedback.
- Over rounds, contributions will converge toward conditional cooperation: moderate-to-high contributions from most players, with a small number of inconsistent low contributors (e.g., SLOTH and occasionally RABBIT) who are sometimes punished. Chat will be sparse and used mostly for short exhortations, calls for norm-following, or brief responses to punishments.
- Because players don't know when the game ends, we should not expect a predictable collapse of cooperation at the very end; contributions should remain fairly stable once a norm is established.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This public-goods game pits private incentives (keep your coins) against the group benefit (multiplying and sharing contributions). Rational self-interest favors contributing 0 each round, because each coin you contribute costs you 1 but yields only 0.66 back to you. However, if many people contribute, everyone can earn substantially more than the 20-coin endowment (because the multiplied pot gets shared). Communication (cheap talk) can help coordinate cooperation, but messages are nonbinding and some players will be tempted to free-ride on others' contributions. Because players only see their own net payoff after each round (and not others' individual choices), they can nonetheless infer the group's total contribution from that payoff and use it to adjust strategies. Expect early optimism and moderate contributions from many players, some exploitation by a few low contributors, attempts at coordination, temporary recoveries, and ultimately a decline as free-riding and uncertainty erode cooperation — all tempered by the variety of player priors (some are more willing to risk cooperating, others are more likely to defect or to punish defectors).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) from their endowment of 20 coins. The group pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all 13 players; each player's round payoff is (20 − own contribution) + (8.1 * total contributions / 13). Key incentives: contributing helps the whole group (large multiplier) but is individually costly — the private marginal return from contributing 1 coin is 1 coin less retained but increases each player's share by 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, so individually it's a net loss (you lose 1, gain ~0.623), incentivizing free-riding. Uncertainty: players do not see others' contributions during decision-making; at round summary they only see their own net payoff, so inference about others is indirect. Expect initial moderate-to-high contributions from persons characterized as cooperative or high mental-accounting/patience (CHICK, PARROT, CROCODILE, OWL, GORILLA), and low contributions from risk-seeking/low-accounting types (CHICKEN, DUCK). Over rounds, because players can infer group behavior only from their own payoff, a general pattern of gradual decline in contributions is likely as the private incentive to free-ride dominates and some players adjust downward when their own payoff is higher when contributing less. However, strongly cooperative types (e.g., CHICK) tend to remain high throughout; some moderate types hold middle ground.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 players against the temptation to free-ride. Each round everyone privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns less than 1 coin to its contributor (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623). Individually the dominant action is to contribute 0, but group payoffs are highest when everyone contributes. Costly punishment allows players to impose penalties on low contributors: each punishment unit costs the punisher 3 coins and cuts 7 coins from the target. That makes punishment powerful but itself costly.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: heterogeneous priors and social preferences lead to a mix of moderate contributors and some clear free-riders. Conditional cooperators and high mental-accounting players (e.g., CHICKEN, CHICK) start relatively high and are willing to punish obvious low contributors to enforce norms.
- Middle rounds: occasional, targeted punishment of persistent low contributors encourages higher contributions and stabilizes cooperation among many players. Punishment is used sparingly because it is costly; most punishments are single-unit responses aimed at signaling norms rather than maximizing damage.
- Late rounds: toward the end (the “endgame”), forward-looking players reduce contributions because future retaliatory costs from punishment are less effective; cooperation erodes. Punishment becomes rarer as players are less willing to bear costs for future reputation gain.
- Uncertainties: individuals differ in patience, risk preferences, and mental-accounting tendencies; some players who are naturally selfish may briefly raise contributions after being punished, others may persist in low contributions and be repeatedly (but lightly) punished. Predicting exact timing of the endgame decline and which defectors will be targeted has uncertainty.

The transcript below simulates these tendencies round-by-round. For each round I give a brief explanation, the ordered contributions matching the player list, the (sparse) punishment actions as tuples (source, target, unit), and a placeholder summary stage shown to players.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round players decide how many of their 20 private coins to contribute to a common pot multiplied by 6.6 and split evenly across all active players. Because contributions are privately chosen and the multiplier returns less than the per-player marginal benefit of giving (6.6/10 = 0.66 < 1), the one-shot incentive is to contribute zero. But social incentives (higher group payoff if many contribute) and costly punishment (each unit costs the sender 2 and removes 5 from the punished player) can sustain higher cooperation: a small amount of punishment is enough to make free-riding costly for targets.

Given the player personas, I expect:
- A fairly cooperative baseline: several players (GORILLA, PARROT, DUCK, DOG, CHICK) have tendencies that support steady contributions.
- A few occasional low contributors (MOOSE, sometimes SLOTH, maybe CHICKEN or CHICK) who fluctuate between low contributions and partial cooperation. MOOSE appears the most persistent low contributor.
- Punishment will be used sparingly and targeted: when a player repeatedly free-rides, a few norm-enforcers (GORILLA, PARROT, DOG) will apply small punishment units to deter them. Retaliation can occur but is costly; most retaliatory moves will be rare and small.
- Chat will be sparse and functional: short coordination appeals, calls out low contributors, or short expressions of displeasure after punishments.
- Over the 19 rounds, expect contributions to trend toward moderate-to-high levels (often 12–20), with occasional defection spikes by persistent low contributors and targeted punishments to enforce the norm. Because players do not know when the game ends, dramatic end-game collapse is less likely than in a finitely known-horizon game.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round one-shot-style public goods game played anonymously online. Each round every player privately has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group sum of contributions is multiplied by 8.1 and divided equally among the 13 players; each player’s round payoff equals (20 − their contribution) + (8.1 * total contributions)/13. Because 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 < 1, every coin you put into the public pot reduces your private payoff by 1 but returns only ~0.623 to you directly; so the dominant selfish incentive is to contribute 0 (free-riding). Social preferences, reciprocity, mental-accounting, and expectations about others’ behavior can drive positive contributions despite the private cost.

Uncertainties players face here:
- They do not see others’ contributions before deciding; they only learn their own net payoff after each round (from which they can infer total contributions but not individual choices).
- The game lasts 16 rounds (finite horizon), so end-game effects (decline toward 0) are likely.
- Player personas (prior tendencies like patience, risk-taking, and strength of mental-accounting) act as informative priors for who is more likely to contribute. reflection:
This is a 16-round one-shot-style public goods game played anonymously online. Each round every player privately has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group sum of contributions is multiplied by 8.1 and divided equally among the 13 players; each player’s round payoff equals (20 − their contribution) + (8.1 * total contributions)/13. Because 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 < 1, every coin you put into the public pot reduces your private payoff by 1 but returns only ~0.623 to you directly; so the dominant selfish incentive is to contribute 0 (free-riding). Social preferences, reciprocity, mental-accounting, and expectations about others’ behavior can drive positive contributions despite the private cost.

Uncertainties players face here:
- They do not see others’ contributions before deciding; they only learn their own net payoff after each round (from which they can infer total contributions but not individual choices).
- The game lasts 16 rounds (finite horizon), so end-game effects (decline toward 0) are likely.
- Player personas (prior tendencies like patience, risk-taking, and strength of mental-accounting) act as informative priors for who is more likely to contribute. Players with higher mental-accounting or strong cooperative tendencies (e.g., PARROT, RABBIT, CROCODILE, COW) are more likely to persist in contributing, at least early on; risk-seeking or low-mental-accounting types (e.g., MOOSE, SLOTH) are more likely to free-ride or cut contributions quickly.

Likely unfolding: modest-to-moderate average contributions in early rounds driven by cooperators and uncertainty about others, producing large shared returns and high round payoffs for everyone; as players observe their own payoffs and realize the private return of contributing is negative, many reduce contributions over rounds. Some players with strong cooperative priors maintain small positive contributions (or taper gradually), while others fall to 0—especially toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game (PGG) runs 16 rounds with 13 players who each receive 20 coins each round and choose integer contributions 0–20. The shared pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally: the marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is about 0.623, so contributing strictly lowers a player's private payoff but raises group payoff. Because contributions are not observed before choices, players face uncertainty about others' choices each round. The punishment stage (cost 3 per punishment unit to sender; each unit removes 7 from the target) allows contributors to discipline low contributors, which typically supports higher cooperation over repeated interaction.

Given the player personas, many players have high patience and strong mental-accounting tendencies (e.g., SLOTH, DUCK, CHICKEN, CHICK, RABBIT, SNAKE, COW, PARROT) and thus are predisposed to contribute relatively high amounts and to punish clear free-riders sparingly. A few players (GORILLA, MOOSE) are more likely to test the cooperative norm initially or to contribute low amounts; they can be targeted with light penalties. Punishment is typically used selectively — several players may punish a clear defector in early rounds, after which defectors often raise their contributions to avoid further costs. Over rounds we therefore expect: (1) initial mixed contributions with a few low contributions, (2) selective punishments targeted at obvious free-riders, (3) subsequent conformity to higher contributions by previously low contributors, and (4) occasional one-off defections and isolated retaliatory punishment but generally stabilizing toward higher average contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly peer punishment and an unknown horizon. The private incentive each round is to contribute 0 (each coin yields 0.66 to each player, less than the private value 1), while the group optimum is to contribute all 20. Players have heterogeneous priors about cooperation and punishment tendencies: some (e.g., DUCK, RABBIT, DOG, OWL, CHICKEN, CHICK) are predisposed to contribute more and to enforce cooperation; others (e.g., PARROT, MOOSE, sometimes SLOTH or GORILLA) are more likely to test and free-ride. Because punishments are visible and identify sources, we expect early “testing” defections, a few targeted punishments that raise some low contributors, occasional retaliation by punished players, and then a dynamic where punishment maintains moderate cooperation for a while but is used sparingly because it is costly. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to coordinate, request cooperation, or react to punishment. Over 19 rounds a gradual drift downward in average contributions is likely, with short-lived recoveries when key contributors or punishers reassert norms.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, each given 20 coins per round and choosing an integer contribution 0–20. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 6.6, and split equally. Because each player’s private remainder plus their equal share equals 20 - own_contribution + 0.66 * total_contribution, players can infer the group's total contribution from their own observed net payoff and their chosen contribution. That makes learning fast: even without seeing others' choices, each round everyone learns the group's aggregate behavior exactly (but not individual choices).

Typical dynamics here are:
- Early rounds often start with moderate cooperation from some players and low contributions from others (mixed motives). With messages, small coordination attempts can occur.
- Because free-riding gives a private advantage (reducing your contribution increases your private remainder while only modestly changing your share), contributions often drift downward unless players find and sustain an enforcement or reciprocity pattern.
- Because players can compute total contributions from their net payoff, they will adjust strategically each round. Occasional messages can temporarily revive cooperation, but absent strong enforcement, contributions tend to fall toward low values mid-game. Late in the sequence we sometimes see a brief cooperative resurgence if a few players commit to larger contributions and others reciprocate.
- Uncertainty: players do not know how many rounds there will be, so they can't condition on a final round. That tends to preserve some cooperative incentives compared to a known-end small-horizon game, but the tendency to free-ride still undermines sustained high contributions.

Below is a simulated transcript across 19 rounds using the given player personas as priors. Chats are sparse and purposeful: a few signals, proposals, and occasional payoff reports.

game_explanation:
This is a standard simultaneous public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round, and a strong multiplier (6.6) split equally. Each coin you contribute returns 0.66 to each player (6.6 / 10), so a one-coin contribution increases the group's total payoff but reduces your individual payoff by 0.34. That creates a tension: the social optimum is for everyone to contribute a lot (total payoff rises sharply), but the dominant individual incentive is to contribute nothing and free-ride.

Because players do not see others' contributions directly but do see their own round payoff, they can infer the group's total contribution from their payoff (payoff = 20 - your contribution + 0.66 * total contributions). Typical dynamics in such games: an initially cooperative phase (moderate-to-high contributions) is often followed by a gradual decline as some players reduce their contributions to exploit others, causing reciprocation and further decline. Messages can help sustain cooperation but are usually sparse and may contain promises, warnings, or short appeals; players will be cautious about trusting messages because they cannot verify them directly. Because players do not know the number of rounds, there is no guaranteed terminal "endgame" drop, but uncertainty about others' behavior tends to produce a slow erosion of contributions.

I will simulate 19 rounds. Contributions are integers 0–20 and are given in the player order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK. After contributions I will show each player's own net payoff for that round (players see only their own payoff).

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives against group welfare. Each round, players decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; each contributed coin returns only about 0.623 coins to the contributor after multiplication and equal splitting, so contributing is socially efficient but individually costly. Because the horizon is finite (16 rounds), we expect early rounds to show more cooperative/mid-range contributions from patient or loss-averse players, with some individuals tempted to free-ride. Punishment is available: it is costly to the punisher (3 coins/unit) and highly punitive to the target (7 coins/unit), so punishments typically appear sparingly and targeted at persistent low contributors to enforce norms. Over time, contributions often drift downward (end-game effect) unless punishment sustains cooperation. Given the player priors, some players (COW, SLOTH, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, SNAKE) are biased toward higher contributions; others (GORILLA, DUCK, CROCODILE, OWL, CHICK) fluctuate or tend lower. Punishments will be used occasionally to discipline clear under-contributors; they will be limited in number and size.

# GAME STARTS

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits 13 players, each with 20 private coins per round, against the incentive to free-ride: each coin given to the pot returns 8.1 total, but only ~0.623 to each contributor (8.1/13), so the private marginal return is <1. That creates a classic social dilemma: group-optimal behavior is to contribute a lot; individual short-run incentive is to contribute nothing. Because players see only their own round payoff (and know parameters), they can infer the group's total contribution each round from that payoff and their own contribution. That feedback supports conditional cooperation: many players who expect others to give will match perceived group generosity early on, then slowly adjust if they infer low contributions. Some players have priors (personas) that favor patience and larger contributions (e.g., PARROT, GORILLA, OWL, CHICK), while others (RABBIT, CROCODILE) are more prone to free-riding. I predict reasonably high initial contributions by several cooperative-leaning players, followed by a gradual decline as low contributors persist and average group contribution falls. Because total pot returns are large in absolute terms, even low contribution rounds still yield sizable payoffs, so declines will be gradual rather than collapsing immediately.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20 private coins each round, and a multiplication factor of 6.6 on the group pot that is split equally. Individually, contributing a coin reduces your private holding by 1 but increases each player's return by 0.66 — so the private marginal return of contributing is negative (-0.34 per coin). Socially, full contribution yields much higher group payoffs. Because players only see their own net payoff after each round (but can compute total group contributions from that), uncertainty about others' individual choices remains high; cheap talk (messages) can help coordinate but is not binding.

Typical unfolding: some players will start reasonably cooperative (pledges or moderate contributions), others will free-ride. When players deduce from their private payoff that many others contributed little, cooperators will tend to reduce contributions over time. Occasional messages or conditional reciprocity can cause temporary spikes in contributions, but without enforceable commitment, the general tendency is a gradual erosion of contribution levels across rounds. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is no last-round unraveling effect anchored on round count; instead behavior evolves from observed returns and messages.

Below is a simulated transcript for rounds 1–19. Short explanatory notes precede each round to justify the expected behavior; chat is kept sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses 0–20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all 13 players. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) per coin is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, which is less than 1: individually, any coin you keep yields more private payoff than any coin you contribute, but if many players contribute the group benefits. Because players only see their own round payoff (not others' contributions or payoffs), feedback about others' behavior is limited and learning is noisy.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix of behaviors. A subset of players with high patience and strong mental-accounting cues (CHICKEN, CHICK, CROCODILE, COW) will tend to contribute more persistently; some risk- or loss-averse types (DUCK, MOOSE in places) will often free-ride or contribute little. Because there is no public record of others' contributions, average contributions will likely start moderately (some optimistic cooperation), then decline as free-riding dominates. Occasional temporary recoveries are possible when cooperators try to sustain the public good, but overall the trend is downward, approaching low contributions by the final rounds (and zero in the last round is the typical backward-induction prediction).

Trend of modest decline continues. Some persistent cooperators keep contributing above average; freeriders remain at or near zero.
Contributions slide further as uncertainty about others persists; a handful of cooperative players still give sizable amounts, but the group pot shrinks.
Midgame free-riding increases; average contributions fall further. Cooperators still contribute but are fewer and smaller in magnitude.
As players update only on their own payoff, many move to low contributions. A few remain willing to contribute modestly.
Some cooperative players attempt to nudge the average back up; others maintain low contributions. Expect a modest rebound but not to early-round levels.
A temporary cooperative push (some players reverting to higher contributions) produces a clearer uptick in the pot; nevertheless many keep contributing modestly or not at all.
Despite the temporary rise, free-riding resurges; many fall back to low contributions. The average declines again.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round repeated public goods game. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20 out of a 20-coin endowment. All contributions are pooled, multiplied by 8.1, and split equally among the 13 active players. Because the marginal per-coin return to the group (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623) is less than 1, the one-shot individually rational choice is to keep all coins (contribute 0). Collective payoff is maximized if everyone contributes their whole endowment (20 each), but that requires cooperation against the temptation to free-ride.

Key uncertainties here:
- Players do NOT see others' individual contributions or payoffs. At summary they only see their own net payoff for that round (private feedback). That limits direct reciprocity and observational learning: players can only infer others' behavior indirectly from how their own net payoff changes, and those inferences are noisy.
- Given the player personas (patience, risk tendencies, mental-accounting), we should expect heterogeneity. Patient, pro-social or high mental-accounting players (e.g., CHICK, OWL, CROCODILE, DOG) are more likely to start higher and maintain contributions. Low-patience or strong mental-accounting players who treat money as private (e.g., SLOTH, PARROT, MOOSE) are likely to contribute little.
- Because peers' contributions are not shown, there is limited basis for tit-for-tat. Over rounds we expect an overall drift toward lower contributions (learning the dominant strategy or risk-averse adjustments). Some high-cooperators will hold steady; some moderate cooperators may slowly reduce contributions. Final-round behavior may slightly converge toward lower but not perfectly zero if some players persist in contributing.

Below I simulate a plausible transcript from round 1 through round 16 using the given personas, with small gradual declines in several players' contributions over time and relative stability in more patient/cooperative personas. Each round shows a short round-specific explanation, the contributions in the exact avatar order, and then the private round summary (each player sees only their own net payoff).

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 players, each with 20 coins per round, against the temptation to free-ride on others' contributions. Because contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split evenly, a fully cooperative group benefits everyone, but each player gains more individually by keeping coins (since the marginal per-coin return to the group is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, less than 1). Players with high patience or strong loss-sensitivity (e.g., OWL, COW, DUCK, CHICK) are more likely to contribute early and sustain cooperation; players with high gamble-seeking or low patience (e.g., RABBIT, MOOSE) are more likely to defect intermittently. Punishment is costly to send (3 coins per unit) but can strongly deter low contributions (subtracts 7 coins per unit from the target) and is therefore used sparingly and typically in response to repeated low contributions from identifiable defectors.

I expect an initial norm of moderate-to-high contributions that is eroded by occasional low contributions from a few opportunistic players. The group will sometimes use punishment to discipline persistent low contributors; punishments are targeted and limited in size to avoid excessive private cost. Over the 16 rounds cooperation drifts downward (end-game effect), with more noise from players who have consistent tendencies toward low contributions. Punishment frequency will be low-to-moderate and concentrated on a few repeat low contributors (notably RABBIT and MOOSE).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly peer punishment. The private incentive each round is to free-ride (each contributed coin returns less than 1 coin to its contributor once redistributed), while the group incentive favors high contributions. Punishment lets players impose larger losses on low contributors at a personal cost, so it can sustain cooperation early and in the middle of the game. Expect an initial mix of contributions (some high cooperators, some low free-riders), a few targeted punishments of clear under-contributors, then rising average contributions as punishment and reciprocity enforce norms. Toward the end (final rounds) the incentive to defect increases (end-game effect) so contributions tend to fall and punishment becomes less frequent or cost-ineffective. There is uncertainty about exact responses to punishment: some low contributors respond by raising contributions, others retaliate (sending small punishments back). In general, punishment will be used sparingly and mostly directed at obvious free-riders (e.g., SLOTH, CHICKEN early on), with cooperation sustained midgame and unraveling late-game.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 coins each round. Each coin a player contributes reduces their private holding by 1 but increases the shared pot by 6.6; the pot is split equally among the 10 players, so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to each player. That means contributing is socially beneficial if others also contribute, but individually costly (marginal private return 0.66 < 1). Because players do not see others' contributions and see only their own round payoff, direct conditional cooperation is limited: players can't reliably reward or punish peers based on observed contributions. Chat provides a communication channel for promises or coordination, but messages are unverifiable.

Given the player personas, I expect a mixed pattern. Some players (e.g., GORILLA, RABBIT, DUCK) lean toward higher contributions early on (higher patience or gain-seeking), while others (MOOSE, OWL, PARROT) treat money more earmarked or are risk-averse and contribute little. Because players only see their own payoff, several dynamics are likely:
- Early rounds: a handful of cooperative or optimistic players try higher contributions and may send short pledges; others hedge by contributing little.
- Middle rounds: contributors who see their own payoffs decline relative to keeping money will gradually reduce contributions; low contributors will mostly stay low.
- Late rounds: without clear signals, overall contributions drift downward. Occasional chat attempts to boost cooperation can temporarily increase contributions from a few players but won't sustain full cooperation.

Uncertainties: players may experiment unpredictably (e.g., increase a contribution to test others), chat content can be deceptive, and individual updating based only on private payoffs can both under- or over-react. The transcript below uses these priors to make plausible contribution and chat predictions over 19 rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with punishment. Each round every player privately chooses how much of their 20 coins to contribute; the total is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players may spend coins to punish others (each punishment unit costs 2 coins to the sender and removes 5 coins from the target). Players do not know how many rounds remain, which tends to favor cooperation sustained by informal enforcement (punishment) rather than last-round defections. Expect:

- A baseline of moderate-to-high contributions from players with strong "mental-accounting" or patient tendencies (SLOTH, PARROT, RABBIT, CHICK, CHICKEN).
- Some opportunistic low contributions from risk-takers or impatient types (MOOSE, OWL, intermittently DUCK or GORILLA), followed by targeted punishment from cooperators to enforce norms.
- Punishment will be sparse and targeted: small punishment units aimed at occasional free-riders, enough to deter repeated defection but not so frequent as to be costly for punishers.
- Chat messages will be rare and purposeful (calls for cooperation, mild reproach after obvious free-rides, brief apologies).

Below is a simulated round-by-round transcript for rounds 1–19. Contributions are shown in the exact avatar order given. Punishment lists include only non-zero entries and are shown as tuples (SOURCE, TARGET, UNIT). Each player's round summary reports their net payoff, the coins they used on punishment that round, and the coins deducted from them by others' punishment that round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with six players who each decide whether to contribute their full endowment (20) or keep it (0). Contributing is socially efficient (the group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and shared), but individual incentives favor free-riding because a contributor pays the full 20 while the shared return per player is 3.7*20/6 ≈ 12.33 — so a single contribution raises each player's payoff but costs the contributor. Punishment is available: each punishment unit costs the sender 2 and removes 5 from the target. Players can see who punished them and see round summaries (their net payoff, punishment sent and received). They do not know the total number of rounds.

Likely dynamics: early rounds are uncertain — some players will contribute to signal cooperation (those with higher patience/mental-accounting), while a few opportunistic or "lazy" players test free-riding. Punishment will be used sparingly but decisively to deter persistent free-riding: cooperative players with stronger norms (PARROT, DOG, GORILLA) are likelier to punish one-unit infractions, producing a quick move toward stable cooperation. Because punishment is costly, punishers will usually use only 1 unit per violation and target repeat defectors. Occasional slips or tests will produce further one-off punishments; full collapse of cooperation is unlikely because punishments make free-riding relatively unattractive over repeated interactions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with a costly, pro-social reward stage. Each round every player has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group contribution is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly over the six players; the private return from contributing (marginal per-coin) is 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 < 1, so the one-shot monetary incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Social preferences, reciprocity and the reward stage can sustain positive contributions: rewards cost 4 to the giver and give 3 to the recipient, so giving rewards is itself costly and typically used sparingly to reinforce cooperation or reciprocate kindness. Because the game lasts only two rounds, strategic reciprocity matters: people who receive rewards or see others contribute in round 1 may boost contributions in round 2 to be rewarded or to reciprocate. Uncertainties include whether free riders (DOG, SLOTH tendencies) will exploit cooperators, and whether costly rewards are used enough to change behavior. Given the personas, expect PARROT and GORILLA to contribute relatively high and to reciprocate rewards; DUCK and CHICKEN to contribute moderately; DOG to free-ride; SLOTH to contribute little. Rewards should be few and targeted at the high contributors (reciprocal exchange between the main cooperators).

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with anonymous contributions followed by costly positive rewards. With a 2.2 multiplier and 6 players, full cooperation is jointly profitable (each cooperative 20 yields +24 net if everyone else also cooperates), but individual incentives favor free-riding in a single round. Because the game is only two rounds, reputational incentives are limited but still present: people who cooperated in round 1 can be rewarded in round 1 and may expect reciprocity in round 2; free-riders can be punished by withholding rewards or by others reducing their contributions in round 2. Players with high "mental accounting" and patience (PARROT, CHICKEN) are relatively likely to contribute more and use rewards to reinforce cooperation; more impatient or less accounting-oriented players (DOG, GORILLA) are more likely to keep private coins or under-contribute. Rewards are costly (cost 4 to send 1 unit, gives recipient +3), so players will reward sparingly and mostly to reinforce visibly high contributors. Chats are expected to be sparse but can be used to signal intentions or call out free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, 19 rounds. Each round each player decides how many of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) vs withdraw for private use. The total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After payoffs are calculated players may spend costly reward units (2 coins per unit) to give 2 coins per unit to others. Players never see others' contributions directly in the summary — they only see their own round payoff and the amount they spent on rewards and amount they received. Rewards are anonymous to recipients (they cannot identify who sent them).

Key likely dynamics and incentives:
- The marginal private return from contributing is 0.3 per coin (1.8/6), less than the private payoff of keeping a coin, so pure self-interest favors zero contribution. With no direct observation of peers' choices, players face high uncertainty about others.
- Social preferences (reciprocity, fairness, conditional cooperation) and public messages can sustain cooperation early, but anonymity of contributions and anonymous rewarding weakens the feedback that would sustain conditional cooperation.
- Rewards provide a tool to sustain prosocial behavior, but each unit costs the sender as much as it benefits the receiver (2 coins out, 2 coins in), and because rewards are anonymous and relatively costly, they will be used sparingly.
- Expect an initial phase where cooperative-minded players (CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, DUCK) contribute moderately/highly, SLOTH contributes little, and GORILLA is mixed. Over rounds, contributions will tend to drift downward as uncertainty and free-riding accumulate. Occasional rewards and short chat messages will appear in attempts to support cooperation, but overall decline is likely, with near-zero contributions by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with private 20-coin endowments each round and a binary contribution choice (0 or 20). The group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the six players, so each coin contributed returns 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167 coins to every player. That creates a classic tension: contributing reduces a player's private holding by 20 but increases everyone's payoffs; free-riding (contributing 0) is individually attractive each round because noncontributors keep their 20 and still receive the shared return.

Key likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players who tend toward cooperation or who signal will contribute (trying to build mutual gains). Others will test by free-riding because they immediately earn more when many contribute.
- Information: after each round every player sees everyone's net payoff for that round. Because payoffs are shown, contributions can be inferred, so behavior becomes common knowledge round to round.
- Communication: sparse, purposeful chat can be used to propose or promise cooperation. Because players don't know when the game ends, there is no known last round to force an endgame collapse; still, repeated exploitation often leads to declining contributions.
- Individual tendencies (personas) bias likely choices: SLOTH, GORILLA, and DUCK are more patient and more likely to try contributing early; DOG and PARROT are more likely to free-ride; CHICKEN is mixed/conditional and may respond to others' choices.

Overall prediction: an initial cluster of contributors, followed by gradual decline as free-riders exploit cooperators, with one or two short-lived re-coordination attempts via chat. By late rounds contributions are likely low or zero.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with six players, 19 rounds, and endogenous sanctions (punish and reward). Each coin left in the pot yields 1.8 coins total that are shared equally among the six players, so each coin contributed returns 0.3 coins to every player (including the contributor). The private return to keeping a coin is 1.0, while the private return from contributing it is only 0.3, so the dominant selfish action is to withdraw everything (contribute 0). However, conditional cooperation and the possibility of punishment/reward can sustain substantial contributions: players who expect others to contribute may contribute too; players who value fairness or reciprocity may punish persistent free-riders or reward high contributors. Because punishment costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target, it is a powerful deterrent even when used sparingly. Rewards cost the source 2 coins and give the target +2, a gentler positive incentive.

Given the provided personas, I predict an initial phase of moderate cooperation (many players contributing well above zero), some targeted punishments toward low contributors (especially PARROT and occasionally DUCK), and sparse rewards to reinforce cooperation. Over the long finite horizon (19 rounds), there will be a gradual erosion of contributions toward the end (the classic end-game decline), with fewer punishments as the cost of enforcing cooperation becomes less attractive. Chat will be sparse and typically used to encourage or call out low contributions.

This 2-round public goods game creates a tension between private incentives (keep your 20 coins) and group incentives (contribute to the pot, which is multiplied by 2.2 and shared equally). Contributing benefits the group but reduces your private holding; low contributors (free riders) can earn more individually in a single round unless the group reciprocates or punishes through rewards. The reward stage allows players to pay 4 coins per reward unit to give 3 coins to a chosen peer — it is costly to the sender and increases the recipient's payoff but lowers total group earnings by 1 coin per reward unit. Because rewards are visible as actions but the recipients cannot see who rewarded them in their summary, senders can condition behavior on contributions and use rewards to signal approval or attempt to influence future play. Uncertainty comes from not observing others' choices before deciding and from not knowing whether rewarding will effectively change others' behavior in round 2.

Given the provided personas, I expect generally cooperative behavior from SLOTH, PARROT, CHICKEN and moderately cooperative from DUCK and DOG. GORILLA is more likely to free-ride or contribute little. Rewards will be sparse and targeted at higher contributors to reinforce cooperation; some players who invest in rewarding may not be directly reciprocated, so they may adjust in round 2. Because the game is only two rounds, reputation-building can occur but is limited — players who rewarded in round 1 may continue to reward cooperators in round 2, while low contributors may be ignored (not rewarded).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public goods game with 6 players, binary contributions (0 or 20) each round, and a multiplier of 3.7 split equally. Individually, contributing is costly: each 20-coin contribution increases everyone's final share by 3.7*20/6 = 12.33 coins, so a contributor loses 7.67 coins relative to keeping the 20. The dominant-strategy prediction is therefore to free-ride every round. Socially, however, full cooperation (all contribute) would yield 74 coins per player, much higher than the default 20.

Given the personas:
- PARROT and CHICKEN have high patience/risk-taking and are most likely to contribute repeatedly (they value the group payoff or have strong prosocial tendencies).
- SLOTH shows patience and strong mental accounting, so may try cooperating initially but will shift if exploited.
- DUCK is mixed/conditional and may experiment with contributing some rounds.
- GORILLA (low patience, low mental accounting) and DOG (very strong mental accounting, avoids losses) are most likely to free-ride consistently.

Because players see round-by-round payoffs of all players, conditional cooperation is possible: contributors can be identified and rewarded or punished in later rounds. Expect initial partial cooperation (a few contributors) followed by decline as free-riders earn more, with occasional attempts at coordination via sparse chat. PARROT and CHICKEN will sustain contributions more than others; SLOTH and DUCK may cycle between cooperating and defecting depending on perceived fairness. GORILLA and DOG are likely to almost always keep their 20.

game_explanation: This is a 2-round public goods game with six players. Each player has a private incentive to contribute 0 (because each coin returned to the individual yields only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins), while the group would benefit if everyone contributes more (the group return per coin is 2.2 > 1). The availability of costly punishment (4 to punish, -10 to target per unit) and costly reward (4 to reward, +3 to target per unit) creates social enforcement possibilities: punishment can strongly deter low contributions (it hurts targets much more than it costs punishers), while rewards are expensive to send relative to the benefit they give the target (costly to the sender, modest benefit to the receiver). Because the game lasts only 2 rounds, players will treat round 1 as a place to signal intentions and test others; in round 2 they can respond to observed behavior (increase contributions to avoid punishment, reward cooperators, and punish persistent low contributors). Chat is cheap talk and typically sparse; punishments and rewards are used sparingly in real data, so I predict a few targeted punishments of very low contributors and a few small rewards to signal appreciation for high contributors.

game_explanation:
This 2-round public goods game creates a tension between private incentives (keep your 20 coins) and collective gains (contribute so the shared pot grows). Each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 to each player, so the private return per coin is 0.367 (less than 1), while the group return per coin is 2.2 (greater than 1). That means the social optimum is to contribute more, but individually each player has an incentive to free-ride.

Uncertainties and behavioral forces:
- Players do not observe others' contributions before choosing, so first-round choices reflect priors, norms, and brief chat signals rather than reactions.
- Punishment and reward make cooperative behavior more attractive: rewards (costly to giver, beneficial to receiver) and punishments (very costly to punished player) can stabilize cooperation but are themselves costly and reduce total group payoff.
- Because the game lasts only 2 rounds, second-round behavior can diverge: some players may treat round 2 as a last chance to extract gains (reduce contributions), while others reciprocate prior treatment even in the final round.
- Personas matter: some players are more likely to contribute or to punish/reward. We expect a mix of moderate contributions in round 1, selective rewarding of high contributors, sparing punishment (due to high cost), and modest adjustments in round 2 (punishments drop; some who were punished raise contributions).

I predict: moderate-to-high contributors (PARROT, DUCK) will stand out; a few low contributors (DOG, CHICKEN) may get singled out. Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce high contribution; punishment will be rare but may target a particularly low contributor once. Round 2 will see some convergence toward higher contributions by those punished or praised in round 1, but final-round incentives may keep some free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, 19 rounds, and a reward stage after contributions. Each round players choose how many coins to withdraw (so contributions = 20 - withdrawal). The shared pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally (so everyone gets 0.3 × total contributions each round). Because the public return to each player is 0.3 per coin contributed, contributing is individually costly (you lose 1 coin privately) but benefits the group (0.3 to each of 6 players totals 1.8). Without other mechanisms this favors free-riding, but the reward stage allows players to transfer value to others (each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Rewards can sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors; they can also be rare because they cost senders. Players know the horizon (19 rounds), so we expect some end-game decreases in contributions.

Given the six personas:
- SLOTH (patient) will tend to contribute moderately-high and reward cooperators sometimes.
- GORILLA (impatient / loss-sensitive) will contribute low and rarely reward.
- DUCK (patient, risk-taking) will be a consistent high contributor and a frequent target of rewards.
- CHICKEN (high gain-seeking) contributes high early and rewards reciprocally; may be strategic.
- DOG (mixed patience, high mental accounting) will be moderate and occasionally reward cooperators.
- PARROT (very loss-sensitive, low patience) will contribute low-to-moderate and mostly not reward.

So the likely dynamics: early rounds see moderate-to-high contributions by SLOTH, DUCK, CHICKEN, and DOG; GORILLA and PARROT contribute less. Cooperators use sparse rewards to bolster high contributors (mostly DUCK and CHICKEN); rewards are few (1–3 small gifts per round) because they cost senders. Over rounds contributions fluctuate but remain moderate because the reward mechanism partially sustains cooperation; toward the final rounds contributions drift down as end-game effects appear and rewards become less frequent.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game where each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses either to contribute the whole 20 or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across all 6 players, so each contribution raises the group's pot by 74 and yields each player about 12.33 coins of benefit (12.333... × number of contributors). Because the per-person return from one contribution (≈12.33) is less than the private value of 20, the individually rational action in a one-shot sense is to keep the 20 (free-ride). However, people may try to build cooperation by contributing early, signaling, and reciprocating. Over repeated anonymous rounds (players don't know how many rounds remain), common patterns are: some initial cooperation from norm- or reputation-oriented players, quick exploitation by free-riders (who see higher payoffs), then erosion of contributions over time; occasional attempts to re-start cooperation by one or more players are possible.

Given the six personas:
- SLOTH (patient, risk-seeking) is relatively likely to try to sustain cooperation or test it repeatedly.
- GORILLA (somewhat patient, high mental accounting) may reciprocate early but will switch to free-riding when exploited.
- DUCK and CHICKEN (high loss sensitivity and clear mental accounting) tend to be cautious and likely to keep their 20s unless convinced cooperation is stable.
- DOG (impatient, highly loss-sensitive) is very likely to keep the 20 every round.
- PARROT (moderate patience, risk-averse) tends toward keeping the private money.

I therefore predict a modest level of early cooperation (1–2 contributors), rapid exploitation by free-riders, a general decline to mostly zero contributions by mid-game, with one or two occasional re-coordination attempts (a few rounds where a small coalition experiments with contributing). Chat will be sparse and mostly commitment/response statements rather than long discussions.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round each player chooses how many of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (their contribution). The group total is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each coin you contribute returns 0.30 to you personally but 1.8 to the group. The selfish short-run incentive is to withdraw everything (contribute 0) because you personally get less than 1 back per contributed coin; the cooperative social optimum is to contribute more. Because players play repeatedly and can send costly rewards to each other, we expect a mix of conditional cooperation, strategic testing, and attempts to sustain contributions: cooperators will reward other cooperators (sparingly, because each reward unit costs 2 to give and gives 2 to the recipient) to encourage continued high contributions; some players (free-riders) will withhold contributions and rarely reward. Communication is allowed but public and non-binding, so messages will be sparse signals, promises, or short criticisms.

Typical unfolding I predict:
- Early rounds: modest-to-strong cooperation from several players (SLOTH, GORILLA, CHICKEN, PARROT), with DOG tending to free-ride. Players use a small number of positive rewards to signal appreciation for high contributors.
- Middle rounds: contributions fluctuate. When a few players reduce contributions, others retaliate by withholding their own contributions; rewards are used to prop up cooperators and to show norms. Conditional cooperators (PARROT, DUCK) respond to observed patterns.
- Late rounds / end-game: tendency toward decline in contributions as the horizon nears, but a couple of consistently cooperative players still give modest contributions and occasional rewards. Overall rewards remain sparse and targeted.

The private incentive is to withdraw everything (keep 1 coin instead of contributing, since each contributed coin returns 1.8/6 = 0.30 to the contributor), while the social incentive is to contribute because the group benefits. Because contributions are revealed each round and punishment/reward are possible, players can condition behavior on others' past contributions: we should expect moderate initial cooperation, targeted punishment of clear low contributors, occasional small rewards for cooperators, and some gradual erosion of cooperation toward the end as the horizon approaches — though with sporadic punishments/rewards sustaining cooperation at an intermediate level. Chat will be sparse and used to signal intentions or protest/appease after observed defections. Punishments will be used sparingly (1–2 small, targeted actions per round) and rewards even more sparingly.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, binary contributions (0 or 20), a relatively large multiplier (3.7), and costly punishment (2 coins per punishment unit for the sender, 5 coins deducted per unit from the recipient). Because players do not know the total number of rounds, cooperation can be sustained by reciprocity and the threat of punishment. Expect overall moderate-to-high cooperation: several players have priors that favor contribution (GORILLA, CHICKEN, PARROT, SLOTH, DOG) while DUCK has a somewhat lower tendency to delay / cooperate. When someone defects (contributes 0) they gain a clear short-run advantage (they keep 20 and still share the public pot), so occasional one-off defections are plausible. Other players will sometimes punish defectors to enforce norms; punishment tends to be relatively sparse and small (1–2 units) because it is costly to the punisher. PARROT’s high loss-sensitivity suggests they may punish more strongly when they see repeated defections. GORILLA and CHICKEN, with higher mental-accounting or pro-social tendencies, are likely to enforce cooperation by punishing defectors occasionally. Chats will be short — warnings, reproaches, or brief commitments — used sparingly to coordinate. Over 12 rounds I therefore predict mostly cooperative rounds with a few isolated defections (mainly by DUCK and sometimes DOG), targeted punishment each time, then return to cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 coins each round and a binary contribution choice (0 or 20). The public pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the 6 players, so each coin contributed returns 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167 to each player. Individually, contributing 20 yields an expected private return of about 12.33 from the public pot — less than the 20 you keep by free-riding. Thus the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride, but the group maximizes total payoffs if many contribute. Because players do not know how many rounds remain and can send cheap-talk messages, we expect some conditional/cooperative behavior early (cheap promises, a few contributors), but also exploitation: contributors will often earn less than free-riders, causing cooperation to partially unravel. Given the personas:

- CHICKEN (very high mental-accounting) and PARROT (high patience and loss-sensitivity plus strong mental-accounting) are most likely to contribute consistently.
- GORILLA (patient) may try cooperating initially but will adjust if exploited.
- SLOTH (patient) is somewhat uncertain: may test cooperation but often freerides.
- DUCK and DOG show weaker cues toward consistent cooperation and are likely to freeride most rounds.
- Because messages are cheap talk, expect short, purposeful chat: proposals to cooperate, reports of past actions, occasional grievances. Overall, contributions will be sparse and fluctuate, with CHICKEN and PARROT as steady cooperators, occasional cooperation from GORILLA and SLOTH, and persistent free-riding from DUCK and DOG. Over 12 unknown rounds we expect modest decline or fluctuation rather than a sharp end-game collapse.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard binary public goods game with 6 players. Each round every player has 20 coins and can either contribute all 20 to the public pot or keep all 20. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the 6 players. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) of a single contributed coin is 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167, so each 20-coin contribution returns 20 * 3.7 / 6 = 12.333... coins to each player. That means an individual who contributes loses 20 privately but generates 12.33 for themselves (and the same for each other player). Individually, defection (keeping the 20) is the better choice given others' contributions — free-riding pays more in the short run — but group payoff is higher when more people contribute.

Given the unknown number of rounds, we expect:
- Early rounds: some attempts at cooperation may occur, especially from players dispositionally more prone to pro-social behavior or who send a coordinating message.
- Quick learning: when defectors earn more in early rounds, contributors are likely to reduce contributions in subsequent rounds.
- Communication can sustain brief pockets of cooperation, but with no enforcement and observed payoff signals showing defectors gaining, cooperation tends to erode and drift toward universal defection.
- Occasional experiments (single players or pairs contributing to "test" whether others will follow) are likely but usually fail to sustain long-term cooperation.

I will run through 12 rounds. At each round I include a short explanation for why players act the way they do, then the (sparse) chat, the contribution vector (in SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT order) and the round summary showing each player's net payoff that round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public‑goods game with costly, targeted punishment and sparse chat. Each round players choose either to contribute their full 20 coins or keep it; contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. Punishment is costly: each unit sent costs 2 coins to the sender and removes 5 coins from the target. Players do not know when the experiment will end, so early rounds often test norms and use light sanctions; if punishment can selectively reduce free‑riding it may sustain cooperation, but punishment itself is costly and can prompt retaliation. Expect:
- A core of relatively consistent cooperators (SLOTH, GORILLA, CHICKEN often contribute) who will sometimes punish defectors to enforce norms.
- A couple of more opportunistic players (DUCK, PARROT, occasionally DOG) who defect intermittently and may retaliate when punished.
- Sparse, purposeful chat: a few requests to cooperate or short complaints after being punished.
- Punishment is used sparingly but strategically: contributors will punish defectors in several rounds; defectors may retaliate in the next round, causing short cycles. Over 12 rounds we should see cooperation fluctuate — occasional full cooperation rounds (no punishments), punctuated by rounds with isolated defectors and targeted punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, known horizon (19 rounds), and peer punishment and rewards that are costly to the sender. Typical dynamics: early rounds start with uncertainty and some tentative cooperation; conditional cooperators (those who value future returns and mental accounting) will contribute relatively high amounts and sometimes use costly sanctions to discourage free-riding. Free-riders (those who frequently withdraw much) get higher private payoffs in the short run but attract punishment; punishers incur costs immediately but often sustain higher group contributions over the middle rounds. Rewards are used sparingly, generally to reinforce returning cooperators. Toward the end (final few rounds), forward-looking punishment weakens and contributions tend to drift downward (end-game effect). Given the personas, expect DUCK, GORILLA, and PARROT to be steady contributors and to enforce norms occasionally; SLOTH and DOG to be moderate and responsive; CHICKEN to test cooperation and sometimes defect, drawing occasional punishment.

Expect an initial cluster of contributions, followed by fluctuations as free-riders earn higher immediate payoffs; occasional messages will attempt to restore cooperation and some players will respond.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game pits short-term incentives (free-ride for personal gain) against group incentives (higher total return if most contribute). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20) and the pot is multiplied by 3.7 then split equally, a lone defector benefits in the immediate round (keeps their 20 and still gets a share of the multiplied pot). However, cooperators can use costly punishment to deter free-riding: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 5 coins from the target. Expect early testing/defection, targeted punishment by players who favor cooperation or who were directly wronged, and a pattern where persistent punishments push some defectors to switch to contributing. Chats are sparse and typically used to signal intentions or to reprimand. Punishments are used sparingly because they are costly; when they appear they tend to focus on repeat defectors. Over 12 rounds we should see cycles of occasional opportunistic defection followed by a few punishments, and an overall drift toward stable cooperation once punishers enforce norms.
