game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with a small group (6 players). The private incentive is to contribute 0 (each coin kept returns 1 privately) while the group incentive is to contribute everything (each coin returned to the group yields 2.2 total, but split among 6, so each cooperator receives only ~0.37 per coin contributed). Because the game lasts only two rounds, forward-looking enforcement (threats of future punishment) is weak but not absent — players may punish or reward in round 1 to influence round 2, or act out of fairness/retaliation even in the final round. Punishment is relatively costly to the punisher (4 coins per unit) but inflicts a larger harm on the target (10 coins per unit); rewards are also costly (4 coins to give 3 coins to someone). Messages can be used to signal intentions but are cheap and unverifiable.

Uncertainties here include: how strongly each player values norm enforcement vs. immediate earnings, whether low contributors will change their behavior after being punished, and whether players will use costly punishment in the final round (often reduced). Given the provided personas, I expect a mix: some high contributors who try to sustain cooperation (CHICKEN, SLOTH), some moderate contributors (GORILLA, DOG, DUCK), and at least one low initial contributor (PARROT). Punishment/reward should be sparse: a couple of punishments targeted at the clear defector in round 1, and a few selective rewards for high contributors; in round 2, fewer punitive actions and possibly a small forgiveness reward if a punished player increases contribution.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone chooses how much of their 20 coins to keep (private withdrawal) and the remainder is contributed to the group pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can punish or reward others. Punishment is costly and reduces the target more than it costs the punisher; rewards cost the same to the sender as they benefit the recipient.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some uncertainty about others' contribution levels. A core of patient, high–contributing players (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) will tend to contribute a lot from the start; others (SLOTH, DOG) will be moderate. PARROT, who is less patient and loss-sensitive, is likely to start lower and may be tempted to free-ride.
- Strategic responses: when a low contributor persists, a few players—especially those who value cooperation—may punish (sparingly) to signal that free-riding has costs. Small rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce consistent cooperation.
- Conditional cooperation: most players will adjust their contributions up when they see others cooperating and down if they see persistent free-riding. Punishments are sparing and generally targeted at repeat low contributors (first PARROT, sometimes SLOTH).
- End-game effect: as the final rounds approach (especially round 19), expect some deterioration of contributions — players reduce enforcement because retaliation cannot be reciprocated in future rounds.

I will simulate plausible contributions, sparse chat, and occasional punishment/reward actions consistent with these priors and the personas.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with a reward stage after contributions. Each round, players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a public pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally — so contributions benefit everyone but are costly to the contributor. After seeing contributions, players can spend reward units (each costs 4 coins to the sender and gives 3 coins to the target) to reward others; rewards are anonymous in the summary, so social signaling via chat and visible contributions matter. Because the game lasts only two rounds, forward-looking incentives weaken in round 2 (there is no future retaliation or repayment), so contributions will likely be higher in round 1 and tend to fall in round 2 — but individual differences will matter.

Personas suggest several players are dispositionally cooperative (GORILLA, DUCK, DOG, PARROT — high patience and mental-accounting), so they are likely to contribute substantially and reward visible cooperators. SLOTH has lower patience and may contribute modestly; CHICKEN shows low pro-social cues and low mental accounting, and is likely to freeride. Rewards will be sparse and targeted to contributors to express approval; because rewarding wastes 1 net coin per unit for the group (4 paid, 3 received), players will use rewards sparingly, mainly to signal appreciation rather than to change future behavior (especially in round 2).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 rounds, 6 players, and a modest multiplication of the group pot (1.8). Early on, uncertainty about others' contributions makes cooperation fragile: many players will lean toward moderate contributions (to support the group return) while a few (notably the more selfish or impatient persona signals) will test free-riding. Because rewards are costly (2 coins per reward unit given) and provide only symmetric benefits (+2 to the recipient), rewards are used sparingly — mainly to signal appreciation for steady contributors or to selectively encourage cooperation. There is no direct punishment (negative transfers), so the main enforcement mechanism is withholding rewards (and social signals in chat), which reduces incentives for unconditional cooperation.

Given the personas:
- SLOTH and GORILLA are somewhat steady cooperators and occasionally reward cooperators.
- DUCK is the most likely to free-ride intermittently.
- CHICKEN tends to contribute steadily and sometimes rewards reciprocally.
- DOG and PARROT fluctuate: sometimes cooperative but sometimes reduce contributions when others defect.

Expectations across the 19 rounds:
- Early rounds: generally positive contributions, occasional free-riding by DUCK and sometimes DOG; sparse chat calling out defectors.
- Middle rounds: oscillations in contributions as players react to observed totals; occasional small rewards to reinforce contributors.
- Late rounds: gradual decline in contributions as the horizon shortens, with a few last-round attempts to reward cooperators but often an increase in free-riding near the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 6-player public goods game with 19 rounds. Each round every player effectively decides how much of their 20 coins to keep privately (20 - contribution) and how much to put into the shared pot (contribution). The multiplied pot (x1.8) is split equally, so individual incentives push toward free-riding (keep more privately) while group payoffs rise when many contribute. Because there is a reward stage (costly but positive: each unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins), players can sustain cooperation by selectively rewarding contributors. Rewards are visible as aggregate pay/receive numbers, but players cannot see who sent rewards, so reputational signaling is imperfect; chat messages can be used to coordinate or shame, but identity-blind rewards reduce direct targeted retaliation.

Given the player personas: GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, and PARROT are relatively patient and likely to contribute more and reciprocate. SLOTH tends to be less patient and more likely to free-ride; DOG is mixed/moderate. I therefore expect early rounds to show high contributions from GORILLA, CHICKEN, DUCK and PARROT, moderate contributions from DOG, and occasional low contributions by SLOTH. Players will use sparse rewards to reinforce high contributors (a few 1-unit rewards or occasional 2-unit gestures). Over time, if free-riding by SLOTH persists, others will generally withhold rewards from them (social sanction by omission). There may be some stabilization of cooperation mid-game via small reciprocal rewards among cooperators; toward the end (final rounds) some decline in contributions occurs (endgame effect), but because rewards remain available, modest mutual rewarding can sustain some cooperation through the last round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with six players. Each round every player privately gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2.2, then split equally — so each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 to each player, which is far less than the private cost of 1. That makes the Nash (self-interested) prediction 0 contributions, while the group optimum is full contribution. Because the multiplier is relatively low and the game lasts only two rounds, incentives to free-ride are strong, especially in round 2 (no future rounds to reward/reciprocate).

Rewards let players transfer value after contributions: each reward unit costs 4 to the source and gives 3 to the target. Rewarding is costly for the rewarder (private loss of 4) and slightly less beneficial to the recipient (gain of 3), so rewards reduce total surplus but can be used to reward cooperators or signal intentions across rounds. With only two rounds, rewards can create limited reciprocity: players who benefited in round 1 may reward cooperators and those rewarded may reciprocate in round 2. Because of the high cost of rewarding, expect only a few small reward units used.

Given the player priors:
- DOG, DUCK, PARROT, and (to a lesser extent) GORILLA are more likely to contribute; DOG and PARROT have high patience and (for DOG) strong mental accounting — both tend toward cooperative contributions. DUCK is patient, so likely contributes moderately-high. GORILLA is moderate. SLOTH and CHICKEN have weaker pro-social/motivational cues and are likely to free-ride.
- Therefore expect a mix of contributions in round 1 (some high, some zero), sparse 1-unit rewards directed to clear cooperators after round 1, and reduced contributions in round 2 (but not complete collapse — a few cooperative players may still contribute), with a small number of last-round rewards.

This is a two-round public goods game with six players. Each round every player privately gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2.2, then split equally — so each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 to each player, which is far less than the private cost of 1. That makes the Nash (self-interested) prediction 0 contributions, while the group optimum is full contribution. Because the multiplier is relatively low and the game lasts only two rounds, incentives to free-ride are strong, especially in round 2 (no future rounds to reward/reciprocate).

Rewards let players transfer value after contributions: each reward unit costs 4 to the source and gives 3 to the target. Rewarding is costly for the rewarder (private loss of 4) and slightly less beneficial to the recipient (gain of 3), so rewards reduce total surplus but can be used to reward cooperators or signal intentions across rounds. With only two rounds, rewards can create limited reciprocity: players who benefited in round 1 may reward cooperators and those rewarded may reciprocate in round 2. Because of the high cost of rewarding, expect only a few small reward units used.

Given the player priors:
- DOG, DUCK, PARROT, and (to a lesser extent) GORILLA are more likely to contribute; DOG and PARROT have high patience and (for DOG) strong mental accounting — both tend toward cooperative contributions. DUCK is patient, so likely contributes moderately-high. GORILLA is moderate. SLOTH and CHICKEN have weaker pro-social/motivational cues and are likely to free-ride.
- Therefore expect a mix of contributions in round 1 (some high, some zero), sparse 1-unit rewards directed to clear cooperators after round 1, and reduced contributions in round 2 (but not complete collapse — a few cooperative players may still contribute), with a small number of last-round rewards.

PARROT and DUCK are inclined to contribute substantially. GORILLA will be moderate. SLOTH and CHICKEN will likely free-ride. After contributions are revealed, a few players who value cooperation will spend one 1-unit reward (cost 4) to acknowledge strong contributors. Rewards will be sparse (1-unit each) because of their high cost.

game_explanation: This two-round public goods game puts individual incentives (keep coins) against group incentives (contribute to the pot). Each coin contributed yields 2.2 total to the pot but only about 0.367 back to each player, so the private best response is to contribute 0 and free-ride. Because the game includes costly punishment (source pays 4 to remove 10 from a target) and costly rewards (source pays 4 to give 3 to a target), players who care about norms or reciprocity can enforce cooperation, but enforcement is typically sparse because punishers pay real costs. Uncertainty matters: in round 1 players only have priors about others’ motives and may experiment; punishments in round 1 serve as signals that can change behavior in round 2. Expect a mix: some players (high mental-accounting, high patience) tend to contribute a lot; some (risk/gain-seeking or low norm-salience) contribute less. After round 1, cooperators will likely punish low contributors to deter free-riding, and round 2 contributions will tend to rise as punishment/reward expectations take effect. Chat is usually brief: short announcements, norm appeals, or apologies.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits short-term private incentives against the group benefit from mutual contributions. Each round every player chooses either to contribute their full 20 coins to the public pot or keep them privately. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the six players, so a single contributed 20 increases the group pot by 74 and yields a per-person gain of 12.33 on average — enough that collective contribution benefits the group, but individual defection (keeping 20) often yields a higher private payoff in the short run unless punishments make defection costly.

Key likely dynamics:
- Some players (high mental-accounting, patient types) are more likely to contribute consistently and to pay small costs to punish free-riders to sustain cooperation.
- Risk-seeking or high gain-preferring players may try to free-ride repeatedly as it can remain profitable unless the group punishes them sufficiently.
- Punishment is costly to the sender (2 coins per unit) but can impose a steep penalty on targets (5 coins per unit). Expect punishments to be used sparingly at first and to target clear defectors; if punishments are coordinated they can shift incentives toward contribution.
- Because players do not know the horizon, there is no guaranteed end-game unraveling, but occasional defections and targeted punishments are likely throughout.
- Chats will be sparse and purposeful — reminders, calls to cooperate, or brief complaints after seeing free-riders.

Given the personas, my prediction: SLOTH, DOG, PARROT (high mental-accounting and/or patient) will tend to contribute and punish defectors sometimes; GORILLA (risk-seeking) and CHICKEN (low mental-accounting) will be more likely to free-ride early on; DUCK is mixed and may defect occasionally. Punishment will focus on GORILLA and CHICKEN, growing in intensity early on and then stabilizing once cooperation is established.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players for 19 rounds. Each round players privately withdraw some of the 20 coins (keeping that money) and leave the rest as their contribution; the group pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally (so each player's share = 0.3 × total contributions). After redistribution, players may spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs the giver 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Players only see their own round payoff and their reward activity (what they gave and what they received), not who gave them rewards. They can also chat.

Key likely dynamics and incentives:
- Because players cannot directly observe others' contributions in the summary, behavior will be driven by priors, short messages, and stable personal tendencies rather than full conditional strategies. Expect relatively stable contribution levels that reflect each persona (GORILLA high, PARROT/SLOTH moderate, DUCK/CHICKEN mixed, DOG low), with modest fluctuations.
- Rewards are costly but provide clear signals. They'll be used sparingly to recognize cooperators or encourage modestly higher contributions from trusted peers. Because rewards are anonymous in summaries, they serve mostly as public signals rather than precise reciprocity.
- Uncertainty about others' actions and the anonymity of reward sources dampens retaliation/punishment; rewards will appear intermittently, not every round.
- Near the end of the 19-round game, we may see a small uptick in cooperation as some players make a final cooperative push; however, end-game selfishness can also appear. Expect modest chat (short encouragements or complaints) and sparse reward exchanges targeted at consistent contributors.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game sets a classic tension: each player keeps 1 coin for themselves if they contribute 0, but every coin contributed returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to each contributor — so individually the dominant action is to contribute 0, while collectively everyone is better off if everyone contributes more. Because the group multiplier (2.2) is less than the number of players (6), private incentives favor free-riding.

However, players also have a costly reward stage after the public-good payoff is allocated. Rewards are costly to send (4 coins per reward unit) but give a positive benefit to the recipient (3 coins per unit). Costly rewards can serve as a signaling and reciprocity device: generous players can be rewarded to encourage cooperation in the second round, while defectors can be ignored (or, in other designs, punished if punishment were available). Because the game lasts two rounds, first-round behavior is driven by uncertainty and priors about others; second-round choices commonly respond to observed group contributions and to who gave/received rewards. Rewards are typically sparse because they directly reduce the sender’s payoff.

Given the player personas:
- GORILLA, DUCK, and CHICKEN are relatively patient and risk-tolerant; they are more likely to contribute strongly in round 1.
- SLOTH, PARROT, and DOG are more mixed or conservative; they may contribute moderately or free-ride.
- Players who reward in round 1 (and whose rewards are noticed by recipients as amounts received) will tend to elicit higher cooperation in round 2 from those they rewarded; players who spent a lot on rewards without reciprocity may reduce contributions in round 2.

I therefore predict moderately high contributions from GORILLA, DUCK, and CHICKEN in round 1, mixed contributions from SLOTH, DOG, and PARROT, a few targeted reward units sent to the highest contributors in round 1, and adjustments in round 2 consistent with rewards and payoffs observed in round 1.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone can withdraw coins privately (which lowers their contribution) and whatever remains is multiplied and split equally. Because the game has a fixed horizon, there is a tension between short-run private incentives (take more private coins) and long-run group incentives (keep contributions high to raise everyone’s returns). Two mechanisms change incentives here: (1) rewards (costly to giver, beneficial to receiver) and (2) punishments (costly to giver, harmful to receiver). Both are observable only as aggregated outcomes, not linked to identities in players’ summaries, so social enforcement is possible but noisy.

Likely dynamics
- A subset of players (DUCK, PARROT, DOG) are relatively more likely to cooperate (higher contributions) and to use small rewards to encourage cooperation. DUCK and PARROT are especially consistent contributors; PARROT often uses small positive reinforcement.
- A few players (CHICKEN, sometimes SLOTH) are prone to lower contributions. Others may punish them sparingly when free-riding becomes visible.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly — typically 0–2 units per edge — because each unit is expensive for the sender. Rewards tend to appear as modest 1-unit gestures to sustain cooperation; punishments appear when repeated low contributions occur.
- Over the 19-round finite horizon we expect some cooperation early/mid-game sustained by occasional rewards and a few punishments. Toward the end, contributions drift down as the backward-induction pressure strengthens; the final round will likely see minimal contributions and little sanctioning (no future to influence).

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with a contribution phase followed by optional punishment/reward. Incentives: contributing reduces your private holding but increases the public pot; because the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, a single coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.37 to each player, so a contributor never gets back the full private value of their coin — the private best response is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, punishment (costly to sender, large harm to target) and reward (costly to sender, modest benefit to receiver) create social enforcement possibilities: punishments can deter low contributions; rewards can encourage high contributions. Uncertainties include anonymity (players can't see who punished/rewarded them) and private information about motives, so responses are probabilistic.

Given the player personas:
- Some players (DUCK, DOG, SLOTH) lean toward higher contributions; others (GORILLA, PARROT) are more likely to contribute less.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly in typical experiments; expect a few targeted punishments of the lowest contributors and a couple of small rewards to encourage cooperators.
- Because the game lasts two rounds, early punishment/reward can shift behavior in round 2 (moderate increase in cooperation is likely).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with six players who each decide every round whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across all active players. The immediate monetary incentive favors free riding: a non-contributor keeps 20 and still collects an equal share of the multiplied pot; a contributor forgoes their private 20 but receives the same share of the pot. Because players do not know when the game will end, strategic dynamics are driven by a mix of motives and learning: some players are conditional cooperators who will contribute if others do (and may try to use chat to coordinate), some are persistent cooperators who continue contributing despite exploitation, and some exploit opportunities to free-ride. Typical empirical patterns are initial cooperation that is eroded by free-riding once the payoff differences become visible; sporadic attempts to re-establish cooperation can occur via chat, but without credible enforcement cooperation tends to decay to universal defection.

Given the provided personas:
- SLOTH (patient, risk-tolerant) is likely to try cooperating repeatedly (persistent cooperator).
- GORILLA (loss-sensitive, strong mental accounting) may start cooperative but switch to safe free-riding when exploited.
- DUCK (patient, moderately loss-averse) will try cooperating early but is responsive to others’ behavior.
- CHICKEN (impatient) will likely free-ride from the start.
- DOG (moderately patient, very gain-seeking) will often free-ride when it’s profitable.
- PARROT (gain-seeking) may encourage cooperation early but will also switch if exploited.

Expect: some initial cooperative contributions, visible higher payoffs for free-riders causing defections, a few isolated attempts to restart cooperation via chat, and then convergence toward zero contributions across later rounds.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game pits 6 players against a simple tension: contributing 20 coins to the shared pot helps the group (each contributed coin returns 3.7 total, split across players) but is individually costly (a contributor gets back less than she put in). Free-riding (keeping your 20) is tempting because you still receive a share of others' contributions. Because players can punish after seeing contributions, punishments act as a social enforcement mechanism: cooperators may spend coins to deter free-riding, at a cost to themselves. Punishment is costly (2 coins per punishment unit to the punisher) but hurts the target more (5 coins deducted per unit), which can make a single unit an effective deterrent. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so we expect conditional strategies: cooperation can be sustained if defections are punished promptly, but occasional defections and small, targeted punishments are likely. Chat messages are rare and typically justify or critique behavior; punishments are typically sparse and targeted at repeat or obvious free-riders.

Given the player personas, I predict:
- DUCK, DOG, PARROT and (often) CHICKEN lean toward cooperation initially (high patience/mental accounting), and will punish sparingly to enforce norms.
- SLOTH is more variable and will test free-riding early; repeated punishment will push SLOTH toward cooperation but SLOTH may lapse intermittently.
- GORILLA is patient but loss-averse; GORILLA may experiment with free-riding and respond strongly to being punished.

The transcript below simulates rounds 1–12. Each round shows an explanation for the expected dynamics, any chat lines, the contributions (in the player order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT), explicit punishments as (source, target, unit), and a summary that lists for each player: contribution, coins spent on punishment, coins deducted by punishment, and net payoff for the round. (Share per player = 12.3333 * number_of_contributors; net payoff = (20 - contribution) + share - coins_spent_on_punishment - coins_deducted_by_punishment.)

game_explanation: This is a short (2-round) public-goods game with costly punishment and reward. Because the game ends after round 2, forward-looking incentives decline: players who care about future reciprocity or reputation will be more willing to contribute in round 1 than in round 2. Players with strong “mental accounting” and high patience (e.g., SLOTH, DUCK) are more likely to contribute relatively high amounts early; more opportunistic or risk-taking players (e.g., CHICKEN) are more likely to free-ride. Because punishment is expensive to the punisher and reduces the target by more than the cost to the punisher, we expect punishment to be used selectively against clear free-riders; rewards will be used sparingly to acknowledge strong contributors. Chat will be sparse and typically either pledges ("I’ll contribute X") or short calls for cooperation or reaction to punish/reward outcomes. Overall: moderate cooperation in round 1 with a small number of targeted punishments/rewards; lower contributions in round 2 and relatively little reward, with punishment possibly used again against persistent free-riders.

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 rounds. Each round every player decides privately how many coins to withdraw (so contribution = 20 - withdrawn). The public pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally; that creates a social return (0.3 per contributed coin to every player) but an individual has an incentive to withdraw (private use). Because players can send costly rewards (2 coins cost per reward unit, gives 2 to the target), rewards serve as a way to reinforce cooperative choices or thank contributors. There is no direct punishment, and reward sources are anonymous.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players start relatively cooperative (DOG, PARROT, DUCK, CHICKEN) while a few (SLOTH, sometimes GORILLA) try to free-ride. Small, selective rewards will appear to encourage high contributors.
- Mid game: conditional cooperation means contributions gradually drift downward as free-riding pays off and rewards are sparse; occasional rewards try to sustain cooperation.
- Late game: toward the end (last few rounds), contributions typically fall further; rewards become rare and symbolic. Some players with high patience (DOG, PARROT) still contribute more than average, and occasional small rewards acknowledge those efforts.
- Uncertainty and anonymity of reward source keep social signaling imperfect; chat is sparse and largely appeals for cooperation or brief thanks.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a one-shot-style repeated public goods game with 6 players per round. Each round every player receives 20 private coins and must either contribute all 20 to the public pot or keep all 20. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across all six players. Because 3.7/6 ≈ 0.617 < 1, the individual monetary incentive is to keep the 20 each round (keeping yields a higher personal return regardless of the group's contributions). However, some players have priors and personality tendencies that make them more likely to try cooperating (patience, pro-social leanings, or high loss sensitivity leading to conservative choices); others are more gain-seeking or impatient and will favor free-riding.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: a mix. Some patient or norm-driven players (SLOTH, CHICKEN, PARROT, possibly DUCK) try contributing to signal cooperation. Gain-seeking or impatient players (GORILLA, DOG) are likely to defect (keep 20).
- After the first payoff summary, contributions are transparent through payoffs (contributors get a lower net than non-contributors when not everyone contributes), so free-riders quickly look attractive. Observing that contributors get lower payoffs tends to reduce cooperation.
- Attempts to coordinate (chat) can produce short-lived cooperation bursts, but because choices can't be conditioned and players don't know the number of rounds, cooperation typically declines across rounds toward full defection.
- Messages will be sparse and purposeful: a few appeals to reciprocity, one or two attempts to restart cooperation, and some taunts or acceptance of the selfish strategy.

I simulate these tendencies: initial partial cooperation, a few attempts to sustain it, then gradual collapse into widespread defection by the middle rounds, with occasional short-lived attempts to restart cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, each endowed with 20 coins per round and a binary contribution choice (0 or 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so each contributed 20 yields a group return of 20*3.7/6 ≈ 12.33 to each player (but the multiplier means cooperating usually raises group payoffs even if it costs the individual 20 up front). Because players do not know the number of rounds, we should expect conditional cooperation: many players will contribute if they see cooperation established earlier and punish occasional free-riders to sustain the norm. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) and expensive to the target (5 coins lost per unit), so punishers typically use small amounts and target repeated or obvious free-riding. Chat messages are typically sparse and used to signal norms, warn, apologize, or threaten punishment.

Given the player personas:
- DUCK, DOG, and PARROT show higher patience; DUCK and DOG are more likely to cooperate consistently; PARROT is risk-seeking and may test by defecting occasionally.
- GORILLA has strong mental accounting and is likely to enforce norms with occasional punishment.
- SLOTH is mixed and may respond to being punished by cooperating.
- CHICKEN is risk-seeking and may test free-riding sometimes but will respond when punished.

Predicted dynamics over 12 rounds: cooperation starts mixed, early punishments target obvious defectors. After a couple of enforcement rounds most players settle into a pattern of high cooperation (most contributing 20) with occasional single-person defections that trigger small, targeted punishments. Punishment is used sparingly but consistently enough to maintain a generally cooperative level across rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so each coin a player contributes returns only about 0.367 coins to themselves (2.2/6). That makes free-riding (contributing 0) the privately rational move, but total group payoffs are higher if people contribute more. After seeing contributions, players can spend costly reward units (cost 4 each) to give others +3 each; rewards are therefore costly to the giver and provide less gain to the recipient than the giver loses, but rewards can encourage cooperation over rounds.

Typical dynamics: in round 1 many subjects contribute a moderate fraction (not full free-riding), reflecting some cooperative preferences or uncertainty about others. Rewarding is used sparingly — usually 0–2 small positive rewards sent to notably high contributors. Because rewards are costly, players tend to reward only when they see substantial contributions or to reciprocate past rewards. Round 2 choices will adjust: those who received rewards or saw others contribute more are likely to increase or maintain contributions; known free-riders may be punished by omission of rewards rather than large costly sanctions. Given the 2-round horizon, forward-looking incentive to build longer-run cooperation is limited, but immediate reciprocity and social preferences still matter.

Using the provided personas as priors: CHICKEN (risk-taking, patient) and DOG (very patient) are relatively likely to contribute more. DUCK and GORILLA (high mental accounting for GORILLA, DUCK) may contribute moderately. SLOTH and PARROT are middling. Expect moderate contributions in round 1, a few 1-unit reward gestures to higher contributors, and modest adjustments in round 2 (slight increases by cooperators).

game_explanation:
This PGG runs 19 rounds with 6 players. Each round players decide how much to withdraw privately (so contribution = 20 - withdrawal). The public pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally; because all rounds here keep the sum of contributions at 60, each player's share from the public good is constant at 0.3*60 = 18. That means a player's round payoff before rewards is simply 18 + (20 - contribution) = 38 - contribution. Rewards are the only direct way players can transfer payoff to one another: each reward "unit" costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Rewards are scarce (costly) and anonymous in the players' private summaries, so players use them sparingly to signal approval of contributions or to indirectly incentivize cooperation.

Given the personas:
- SLOTH, DUCK, PARROT are generally patient and inclined toward cooperation; they often contribute relatively high amounts and reward contributors.
- GORILLA is patient but shows risk-avoidant cues; they may oscillate between moderate cooperation and opportunistic moves.
- DOG is moderately risky and exploratory, sometimes contributing less or experimenting with rewarding.
- CHICKEN is impatient and consistently more selfish, contributing low amounts and rarely rewarding.

Because rewards are costly and anonymous, expected dynamics are:
- Early rounds: cooperators test each other with high contributions and small positive rewards; free-riders (CHICKEN) benefit monetarily, making enforcement necessary to sustain cooperation.
- Middle rounds: cooperative clusters (SLOTH, DUCK, PARROT) mainly reward one another while withholding rewards from low contributors; GORILLA may respond to payoff signals.
- Late rounds: endgame effects reduce incentives to invest in costly rewards; some cooperative maintenance persists but with fewer rewards in the final rounds.

Now the round-by-round transcript follows. At the start of each round I give a short explanation for the predicted behavior, then the contributions, any chat, rewards, and the per-player summaries (each player sees their own net payoff and their own reward sent/received counts).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, 19 rounds. Each round each player decides how much to withdraw (private) so the remainder is the contribution. Contributions are aggregated, multiplied by 1.8, and split equally: each player's share equals 0.3 times the total group contribution. After returns are distributed, players can pay to punish (2 coins per punishment unit; each unit removes 3 coins from the target) or reward (2 coins per reward unit; each unit adds 2 coins to the target). Players see only their own round payoff and totals of punishment/reward activity affecting them, not who acted.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds tend to see fairly high contributions as players test each other and try to build cooperation.
- Some players (especially the more impatient or high-gain-risk types here) will be tempted to withdraw more and free-ride. Those low contributions are likely to be met with sparse but targeted punishment from players who prefer cooperation, which tends to stabilize contributions at a moderate level.
- Rewards are used even more sparingly, mainly to signal appreciation for high contributors and to support enforcement.
- Because players cannot identify who punished them, punishment functions as a group signal leading to gradual adjustments rather than direct tit-for-tat.
- Near the final rounds contributions often decline (end-game effect) and punishment declines as future retribution/reciprocity becomes less relevant.

I simulate a plausible run: early cooperation with a few free riders (DUCK and CHICKEN at times), occasional targeted punishments to deter low contributions, modest use of rewards to reinforce cooperators, and gradual erosion of cooperation toward the end. Chat is sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with contributions multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly among six players (MPCR = 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367). Individually, giving any coin to the public pot reduces a player's private payoff (since MPCR < 1), so the selfish Nash action is to contribute 0. However, contributions create group value (the multiplier > 1), and some players are dispositionally more cooperative or concerned about norms. The punishment and reward stage lets players impose norm-consistent costs or benefits on others: punishments are costly to give (4 coins) and impose a heavy hit on targets (-10 coins per punishment unit), while rewards also cost 4 to give and grant modest benefit (+3 to the recipient). Because the game lasts only two rounds, forward-looking reputational incentives are limited, but players can use messages and first-round sanctions to shape behavior in round 2.

Given the player priors: DUCK and PARROT are the most likely to contribute highly; SLOTH tends toward patience and moderate cooperation; GORILLA and DOG are more mixed/moderate; CHICKEN has high risk-seeking and may be most likely to free-ride. Punishments and rewards are expected to be used sparingly: a couple of targeted punishments toward clear free-riders and a few rewards for high contributors.

game_explanation:
This 2-round public goods game sets up a short-run tension between private incentive (keep coins) and group benefit (contribute to the pot). Because contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split among six players, each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 to its contributor via the public pot — so the individually dominant choice is to free-ride (contribute 0) but the group is best off when everyone contributes more. The punishment/reward stage creates conditional cooperation possibilities: players can deter low contributions with costly punishment (4 to sender, -10 to target) and encourage high contributions with costly rewards (4 to sender, +3 to target). Because punishments are costly and anonymous in the summary, players punish sparingly and typically only on clear low contributions; rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce high cooperators.

Given the provided personas: PARROT, DUCK, GORILLA and DOG are relatively patient and inclined to cooperate; CHICKEN is moderate; SLOTH is cautious and values mental accounting (more likely to contribute little). Expect SLOTH to under-contribute in round 1 and to be targeted by a small number of punishments. High contributors will mostly continue contributing in round 2; punishments will decline in round 2 and a few small rewards will be used to reinforce cooperative behavior. Chat will be sparse and purposeful — a couple of pre-commitment/announcement messages and a short reaction after summaries.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 known rounds. Each coin contributed benefits everyone (the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally), but each contributor loses more privately than they gain personally from their own contribution, so the one-shot incentive is to withdraw everything (contribute 0). That makes cooperative contributions fragile unless group enforcement (punishment/reward) or reciprocity supports them.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: some players who favor cooperation or treat money in consistent mental accounts (SLOTH, DOG, DUCK) will contribute moderately (around half or more of their endowment). Players with stronger gain-seeking tendencies (PARROT, GORILLA) will test lower contributions. CHICKEN shows a history of strong negative responses to perceived short contributions and is more likely to punish low contributors to enforce norms.
- Punishment will be used sparingly (it is costly to the punisher) and targeted at repeat or salient low contributors. Small positive rewards will occasionally be used to recognize consistent contributors.
- These enforcement actions will stabilize contributions in the middle rounds around moderate values (roughly 8–15), but as the known end approaches players discount future enforcement and contributions drift downward. In the final rounds, expect lower contributions as backward-induction incentives dominate and punishment loses future leverage.
- Messages will be sparse and primarily used to signal intent or justify punishment (e.g., "I punished low contributors"), not full negotiations.

Uncertainties: how quickly GORILLA and PARROT respond to punishment (increase contributions) and whether CHICKEN's repeated punishing provokes retaliation (rare but possible). Overall I predict moderate cooperation early-to-mid game, targeted punishment by CHICKEN (and occasional retaliation), a few reciprocal rewards among cooperators, and declining contributions toward the end.

game_explanation: This is a two-round public goods game with costly peer rewards. Each round, players choose how many of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot (multiplied by 2.2 and split equally). Monetary incentives push toward free-riding (each coin returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.366 to the contributor), while social preferences and the reward mechanism can encourage contributions: players can spend 4 coins to give a peer +3 coins. Because the game lasts only two rounds, forward-looking incentives to build cooperation are limited — players may contribute more in round 1 to elicit rewards or reciprocal cooperation, then reduce contributions in round 2. Rewarding is costly and typically used sparingly (often to reward high contributors or respond to perceived fairness), and because reward senders are anonymous, players must infer motives only from contributions and their own payoff summaries. Expect moderate contributions in round 1 from the more cooperative personas (SLOTH, CHICKEN, GORILLA), low contributions from more self-regarding players (DOG, PARROT), and very sparse rewarding concentrated on those who contributed most. In round 2, expect an overall reduction in contributions and fewer rewards, though some reciprocation or moral-motivated rewarding can still occur.

game_explanation:
This is a six-player repeated public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20). Individually, each player can gain by free-riding (contribute 0) because the pot is multiplied and shared; but if many free-ride the group payoff falls. Punishment lets contributors reduce a free-rider's payoff at personal cost (2 coins per punishment unit) while imposing a larger loss on the target (5 coins per unit). Because punishments are costly to send, we expect only sparse, targeted punishments: players punish clear or repeated defectors to enforce cooperation, not every small deviation. Messages can coordinate expectations and threats, but players do not know the game length so they treat each round as strategically useful.

Given the players' priors (many are patient and favor mental-accounting/cooperation), we predict a baseline of high cooperation with occasional opportunistic defections (most likely from risk-seeking CHICKEN or an opportunistic GORILLA). Other players (SLOTH, DUCK, DOG, PARROT) will punish those defectors sporadically (usually 1 unit each), which often still leaves a single defector with a higher round payoff, but raises the social cost enough to limit repeated large-scale free-riding. Chat will be sparse and purposeful: brief calls to cooperate, short calls-out when someone defects, occasional apologies.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT), lasting 19 rounds. Each round every player effectively allocates 20 coins between a private withdrawal and a group contribution; the total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally (so each player's public-share equals 0.3 × total contributions). After payoff redistribution, players may pay 2 coins per reward unit to grant 2 coins to some other player. Rewards are costly to send and transfer value one-for-one, so they serve primarily as social signals (encouragement/reciprocity) rather than net group gains.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: modest-to-moderate contributions from generally cooperative players (SLOTH, DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT) and lower contributions by more selfish types (GORILLA, DOG). Players experiment with small positive rewards to incentivize cooperators.
- Middle rounds: contributions drift slightly as players react to others; rewards are used sparingly to reinforce reciprocity. Because reward is costly but neutral in net group sum, it is used chiefly by those who want to signal appreciation or sustain cooperation.
- Late rounds: with a known end at round 19, some players may mildly reduce contributions, but established reciprocity and a few steady contributors can maintain moderate contributions. Chat is sparse and mostly used to signal intentions or thank others; rewards remain infrequent and targeted.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with optional costly rewards. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After seeing contributions and the redistributed payoffs, players can spend 4 coins per reward unit to give 3 coins to another player (rewards are visible in the transcript but, in players' private summaries, the sources of incoming rewards are not shown). Messages can be sent at any time.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Cooperative players (those with high patience and strong mental accounting) will tend to contribute fairly high amounts in round 1 to try to build a cooperative norm; this raises the group return but leaves contributors vulnerable to free-riders.
- Players who contributed a lot and received positive response may continue contributing in round 2. Players who see they were exploited (others contributed little but kept high payoffs) may either reduce contributions (retaliation/defection) or continue cooperating if they value mutual gains.
- Rewards are costly (4 coins) and give less benefit to the recipient (3 coins), so they are used sparingly — mainly as social incentives: to thank high contributors or to encourage cooperation. Because rewards are inefficient from the giver’s personal balance, expect only a few 1-unit rewards targeted at the highest contributors.
- Free-riders (low contributors) tend to get high immediate payoffs unless others use social sanctions or withhold rewards; because this game only has rewarding (no punishment), players will mostly withhold rewards from free-riders rather than directly subtract their payoff.
- Uncertainty: players do not observe others' choices before deciding, so early chat promises may influence but not perfectly coordinate contributions. In round 2, observations from round 1 will strongly influence adjustments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round players decide how many of 20 coins to withdraw (private use); the remainder is contributed to the public pot. Contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and shared equally, so each player's return from others' contributions is 0.3 times the group total. That creates a tension: contributing reduces your private take but increases everyone’s shared payoff. The reward stage allows positive transfers: each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Because rewards are costly to the sender and anonymous to recipients, they tend to be used sparingly — typically to thank consistent cooperators or to try to sustain cooperation.

Given the personas, I expect CHICKEN and PARROT to be relatively steady cooperators (high mental accounting and pro-social cues), SLOTH to be cooperative but somewhat cautious, DOG to be moderate, and GORILLA and DUCK to oscillate more (sometimes freeriding). Early rounds should show reasonably high average contributions as players test each other; cooperators will occasionally reward one another. Over the middle rounds cooperation may taper a bit if free-riding persists, but targeted rewards from cooperators help stabilize contributions. In the final round, standard backward-induction incentives push toward lower contributions (no future to reward), so contributions should drop. Chat will be sparse and mainly encouragement, short comments about cooperation, or occasional calls-out when freeriding is obvious.

This public goods game gives each player a simple binary choice each round: keep all 20 coins or contribute all 20 to the group pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly, so a single contributor benefits the group but is individually exploited in the short run if others free-ride. The punishment stage allows contributors to impose costly sanctions on free-riders: each punishment unit costs 2 to the sender and removes 5 from the target. Players see who punished them and see round summaries, but they do not know how many rounds the game will last.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often start with some cooperation as norms are tested. Contributors who get exploited may then use punishment to enforce cooperation.
- Punishment is costly, so it will be used sparingly and targeted (usually 1 unit per transgression from a few players). If punishment successfully changes behavior, defectors will switch to contributing in subsequent rounds.
- Some players (based on personas) are more likely to contribute consistently (DUCK, DOG, CHICKEN), while others are more opportunistic or risk-averse (SLOTH, GORILLA, PARROT) and may test defection occasionally.
- Over 12 rounds we expect a pattern of mostly cooperation, occasional defections by one player at a time, and selective punishment in response. Retaliatory punishment is possible but will be uncommon because punishers pay a cost and punishers are identifiable.

game_explanation: This is a two-round public goods game where each player gets 20 coins per round, privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot, the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally across the six players, and then players can punish or reward others (punishment: source pays 4 to cost and target loses 10 per unit; reward: source pays 4 and target gains 3 per unit). Because the game lasts two rounds, players can use Round 1 to signal cooperativeness and influence Round 2 behavior via reciprocity, rewards, and the threat of punishment. Expect some players with cooperative priors (DUCK, PARROT) to contribute relatively high amounts in Round 1, some with neutral-to-moderate priors (GORILLA, DOG) to contribute moderate amounts, and less patient/mental-accounting-focused players (SLOTH, CHICKEN) to contribute less. Punishments and rewards are typically sparse: a few 1-unit actions to signal approval/disapproval rather than heavy multi-unit attacks. In Round 2 (the final round), forward-looking incentives weaken, so contributions may drop slightly overall, though reciprocity and reactions to Round 1 punishment/reward can moderate that decline.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a short (2-round) public goods game with a reward stage. Typical dynamics:
- In round 1 many players will try a cooperative contribution (moderate-to-high contributions) but some will free-ride. Cooperators earn smaller private returns immediately but increase the shared pot for everyone.
- Because rewards are costly to send (4 coins per reward unit) and inefficient at the group level (each unit reduces total welfare by 1 coin: -4 +3), players reward sparingly — usually a small number of 1-unit rewards to signal appreciation for strong contributors or to reciprocate perceived cooperation.
- Knowing the game lasts only two rounds, players update in round 2: cooperators who were exploited tend to reduce contributions or shift to rewarding cooperators instead of rewarding free-riders. Persistent cooperators (those who contributed high in round 1) are likeliest to be rewarded in round 2.
- Chat is used rarely — short normative signals like "let's cooperate" or "I'll reward cooperators" are plausible.

I will simulate plausible choices consistent with these priors: a mix of high contributions from GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT; moderate from SLOTH; low from DOG. Rewards will be sparse 1-unit gifts directed at reliable cooperators; no one will explicitly reward the low contributor (DOG). Summaries show only each player's own net payoff, reward spent, and reward received (no attribution or peers' details).

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment and no known end. Players face the usual tension: contributing 20 helps the group (multiplied by 3.7 and shared) but costs the individual, while free-riding (contributing 0) keeps private money and benefits from others' contributions. Because players can punish after seeing contributions, cooperation can be sustained by threat of punishment — but punishments are costly to send (2 coins per punishment unit) and expensive for the target (5 coins lost per unit), so we expect punishments to be relatively sparse and targeted. Early rounds typically serve as tests: some players try free-riding to see tolerance; others enforce norms by punishing defectors. Over time we expect conditional cooperators (who respond to others) to stabilize contributions, while a few risk-seeking or low mental-accounting players may intermittently defect and receive light punishment or switch back to cooperation. Chats are usually short and reactive (calls to cooperate or complaints about defectors). Punishment often focuses on repeat defectors or on those who defect when most others cooperate.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players with varied social and economic tendencies against a simple binary decision each round: keep 20 coins privately or contribute 20 to a group pot. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so group contributions increase everyone's payoffs but create an incentive to free-ride. Because players do not see others' choices before deciding and rounds are of unknown length, we expect a mix of initial cooperation from conditional cooperators, occasional defections by more self-interested players, and sparse punishment used to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly (2 coins per unit to the punisher, 5 coins deducted per unit from the punished), so players will use it sparingly — usually to target repeat free-riders or unexpected defections. Chats are available and will be used occasionally to encourage cooperation or call out defectors.

Given the personas:
- GORILLA (patient, low risk-taking) tends to cooperate and punish free-riders.
- CHICKEN (patient, risk-taking) often cooperates but will punish to enforce norms.
- DUCK and PARROT are moderate; DUCK’s strong mental accounting leans toward predictable, rule-following contributions and occasional punishment.
- SLOTH and DOG are more variable: SLOTH medium/conditional, DOG more opportunistic and likely to test free-riding but may be shifted by punishment.

Predicted dynamics: early rounds show substantial cooperation from GORILLA, CHICKEN, DUCK, and PARROT; SLOTH and DOG test defection early and get lightly punished. Punishment is targeted and small (1–3 units), enough to encourage subsequent cooperation. Cooperation stabilizes for several rounds, with occasional one-off defections that are met with brief, targeted punishment. Towards the end, a few players test defection again, provoking small retaliatory punishments.

This is a repeated public-goods game with six players who choose each round whether to contribute their entire endowment (20) to a common pot or keep it. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. Because returns to individual contributors are lower than returns to the group, free-riding (contributing 0) usually yields higher private payoff in a single round. In repeated play without knowing the total number of rounds, a few dynamics typically appear:

- Early rounds: conditional cooperators (those who value collective outcomes or who react to social cues) are likely to contribute as an experiment to establish cooperation. Risk-tolerant or self-interested players may free-ride, earning more individually and creating tension.
- Middle rounds: contributions often fall as contributors react to free-riding; some "attempts" to restore cooperation (messages, occasional contributions) may occur.
- Late rounds: without a known end, cooperation can persist a bit, but tends to erode; by the final rounds many players often default to free-riding.
- Messaging can briefly sustain cooperation if persuasive, but messages are usually sparse and targeted (requests, nudges, mild reproaches).

Using the provided personas, I expect DUCK and PARROT (high patience, lower mental accounting) to be more willing to experiment with cooperating persistently; GORILLA may be a conditional cooperator, contributing early but cutting back when exploited; CHICKEN is more likely to free-ride; DOG and SLOTH may fluctuate — DOG is strongly loss-averse and mental-accounting high (likely cautious about giving up money), SLOTH is mixed and may contribute occasionally but respond to incentives.

Now the transcript from round 1 through round 12.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with binary choices each round (contribute 20 or keep 20). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the six players, so individual payoff from others' contributions can look attractive if many contribute, but any single player's best-response is to keep their 20 (free-ride) given uncertainty about others. Early rounds often see some cooperation as players test each other or follow promises; if free-riding appears, cooperation typically declines. Chat can sustain cooperation if a few credible, consistently contributing players signal and build trust; but anonymous, simultaneous choices and limited messages make sustained high cooperation unlikely. Expect an initial attempt at cooperation, responses to observed free-riding with reduced contributions, occasional attempts to revive cooperation via messaging, and a general drift toward more free-riding by later rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and secretly chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to each player — less than the private cost of 1 coin. That creates the familiar collective-action tension: the group does best if everyone contributes, but individually it's usually optimal to contribute nothing. Because the game repeats for only one additional round, social incentives (reciprocity, conditional cooperation) and observed payoffs will shape Round 2 choices.

There is also a costly reward stage: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the target 3 coins. Rewarding lets players reward cooperators or try to signal approval, but it's expensive. Players see contributions and the reward actions in the record (we show them here), but in their personal round summary they only see how much they spent and how much they received, not who gave them rewards. Messages are allowed and can influence expectations, but messages are cheap talk and may be sparse.

Given the player personas, I expect a mix of cooperative and free-riding choices. Some players (PARROT, SLOTH) will tend toward higher contributions; others (DUCK) are more likely to defect or contribute little. Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation; players who contributed a lot (especially PARROT) will attract a few one-unit rewards. After Round 1, free-riders will see high payoffs and may continue to free-ride in Round 2; cooperators who were rewarded will mostly maintain or slightly increase contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public-goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone starts with 20 coins and chooses how many to withdraw (so the remainder is contributed). Contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each player's public-return equals 0.3 times the group total. Private withdrawals give immediate return but lower the public pot; small punishments and rewards (costly to the sender) let players try to enforce cooperation. Players cannot identify who targeted them, so sanctions act as anonymous social feedback.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: many players start moderately cooperative (contributions above half). Free-riding (low contribution) yields higher immediate payoff, so early low contributors appear and draw sparse punishment from more cooperative players.
- Punishment and occasional reward are used sparingly to deter persistent low contributions. Punishments are costly for the sender, so they are applied only when contributors drop noticeably.
- Over the long finite horizon (19 rounds), we should see a gradual decline in average contribution as the end approaches — classic end-game effect. Punishment slows the decline but cannot fully prevent it.
- Chat is sparse and typically used to call out defection or encourage others; it occasionally accompanies sanctioning rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each coin a player contributes increases the shared pot by 2.2 coins but each player's private return from one contributed coin is only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.366, so the dominant (selfish) incentive is to free-ride and keep the 1 coin privately. Socially, full contribution is best. Because punishment is costly to the sender (4 coins per unit) but very costly to the target (-10 coins per unit), and rewards are also costly to the sender (4) but give modest benefit to the receiver (+3), sanctions and rewards can enforce cooperation—but they’re used sparingly in real games.

Given the personas: GORILLA, PARROT, and CHICKEN are relatively inclined to cooperate; SLOTH is moderately cooperative; DUCK and DOG are more likely to contribute little (DOG especially low). Expect first-round heterogeneity: several fairly high contributions from pro-social types, low contributions from DOG (and maybe DUCK). That will likely trigger a small number of punishments targeted at obvious low contributors and a few rewards for high contributors. Because the game is only 2 rounds, players focus on immediate payoffs; punishment in round 1 can induce higher contributions in round 2. Overall: modest cooperation in round 1, selective sanctions/rewards, and higher average contributions in round 2 following feedback.

game_explanation: This is a repeated 19-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone starts with 20 coins; what they don't withdraw becomes the public contribution. The group payoff is amplified (×1.8) and split equally, so high total contributions raise everyone's share — but individual incentives favor withdrawing more (free-riding). Because the game has a known finite horizon (19 rounds), we expect some cooperation early on (trust/higher contributions), occasional deviations (low contributors), and selective use of costly punishments and occasional rewards to enforce or encourage cooperation. Punishment is costly (2 coins per unit to the punisher, −3 coins per unit to the punished), while reward is also costly (2 coins per unit, +2 to the recipient). Players typically punish and reward sparingly. Over time we expect some decay in contributions as the end approaches, with periodic punishments used to discipline repeat low contributors and occasional small rewards to recognize high contributors. Chat is possible but typically sparse and strategic (requests, calls-out, thanks).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a classic public goods dilemma: each player faces a private incentive to keep their 20 coins (contribute 0) while the group as a whole benefits if many contribute (each contribution raises the pot by 20, the pot is multiplied by 3.7, and the proceeds are split equally). The marginal personal return to contributing is negative (one contributor adds 74 total to the pot that gives each of 6 players +12.33, so a contributor loses 20 but everyone gains ~12.33). So free-riding is individually dominant, but if many coordinate to contribute, everyone earns much more.

Given the six personas, I predict an initial cluster of cooperation driven by patient / pro-social-leaning types (CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, and sometimes DUCK), while opportunistic or low-mental-accounting types (GORILLA, SLOTH) tend to free-ride. Over rounds you should see cooperation fall when contributors are exploited, with occasional attempts to restart cooperation (by CHICKEN or PARROT), and one coordinated successful surge when players briefly coordinate on a round offering large group gains. Chat will be sparse and mostly used for short promises or frustration.

game_explanation:
This is a standard finite public goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each coin kept privately yields 1 to the chooser, while each coin left in the shared pot yields 1.8 total but is split across 6 players (so each contributed coin returns 0.3 to each player). That makes contribution privately costly (you lose 1 but get only 0.3 back yourself), so the static incentive is to withdraw all coins. Social incentives and the optional rewarding stage can sustain cooperation: players can reward higher contributors at personal cost (2 coins per reward unit) to give targets 2 coins, creating reputational and immediate payoff motives to contribute.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation from several players (people with high patience or strong mental-accounting tend to contribute more).
- A persistent low contributor (here DOG) will earn higher immediate payoffs, which other players notice via conversations or the pattern of payoffs; peers may withhold rewards from that player and reward cooperators instead.
- Rewards will be used sparingly and targeted to high contributors. Rewarding tends to stabilize cooperation among reciprocal players (SLOTH, DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT, GORILLA at times), but cannot fully eliminate free-riding.
- Over rounds we expect fluctuations: cooperation stabilizes at a moderate level with occasional declines when free-riding seems profitable, and occasional recoveries after positive reinforcement via rewards or short chats urging cooperation.

game_explanation:
This 6-player repeated public-goods game balances private withdrawals (immediate private payoff) against a shared return that depends on group contributions (multiplied by 1.8 and split equally). Contributors sacrifice private coins to boost everyone's shared return; withdrawing yields immediate private money but reduces the public pot. Because rewards are costly (2 coins to giver per 1 reward unit, and give 2 coins to the recipient), they are used sparingly — typically to encourage cooperators or reciprocate generosity, not as a continual enforcement tool. Players know the game lasts 19 rounds, so end-game effects (declining contributions toward the last rounds) are likely.

Given the provided personas:
- GORILLA, PARROT, and CHICKEN are relatively patient and loss-sensitive, so they tend to make higher contributions early and respond to reciprocity.
- SLOTH and DUCK are moderate cooperators (mental-accounting tendencies and medium risk patience); they contribute reasonably but may adapt based on feedback.
- DOG is impatient/impulsive and likely to free-ride (low contribution), especially early and in late rounds.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds: moderate-to-high contributions from most except DOG. A few targeted rewards to recognize high contributors will emerge (sparingly).
- Middle rounds: some variability — cooperative pockets persist, but occasional declines appear if players see free-riding. Rewarding remains selective (1–2 units) to reinforce contributors.
- Late rounds: unraveling toward the end; contributions drop as players anticipate others' defection. A small attempt to encourage cooperation may appear mid–late, but the final round typically shows widespread free-riding and little rewarding.

game_explanation: This is a two-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins (0–20) to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After contributions are revealed and redistributed, players may spend costly reward units (cost 4 to sender, +3 to target). Because the game ends after round 2, players balance short-run payoff incentives (keeping coins) against social preferences (rewarding or contributing to raise group returns). Expect heterogeneous behavior: some players with higher cooperative or risk-taking priors (e.g., CHICKEN) are likely to contribute more; more cautious or “mental-accounting” types may contribute moderately (SLOTH, DUCK) or conservatively (GORILLA, PARROT). Rewards are costly and irreversible in the last round, so they will be used sparingly — mainly to reciprocate or to signal approval after round 1, and reduced in round 2. Chat will be sparse and mostly strategic or short social comments.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game lasts 14 rounds with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute all 20 coins or to keep all 20. Contributions go into a pot that is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all players, so individual incentives favor free-riding (keep 20 and still earn a share of the multiplied pot) while group payoff is highest when many contribute. After redistribution, players can spend coins to punish or reward others (3 coins per unit spent). Punishment units subtract 11 coins from the target; reward units add 4 coins to the target. Punishment and reward are anonymous at the summary stage.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some cooperation as players test each other and try to establish norms; occasional punishments or rewards to signal approval/disapproval of others' choices.
- Mid game: cooperation tends to erode as free-riders gain short-term advantage and enforcement (punishment) is costly for those who use it; punishments and rewards remain sparing because they cost the sender.
- Late game and final round: cooperation declines further because future retaliation or reputation effects are limited; last round especially tends toward defection, and players rarely spend on punishment/reward in the final stage.

I use the given player personas to bias likely behavior: reliably patient or pro-social-looking players (GORILLA, PARROT, MOOSE, CROCODILE, COW) are more likely to contribute early and sometimes punish or reward; more opportunistic or less patient players (SLOTH, RABBIT, SNAKE) are more likely to free-ride intermittently. Punishments and rewards are kept sparse and targeted at repeat free-riders.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players and 26 rounds. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contributes 20) or withdraws them for private use (contributes 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally across all players, so contributing is collectively beneficial but individually costly when others free-ride. Because contributions are binary (0 or 20) and reward is the only direct social sanction/support (1 coin per reward unit, paid by sender and received by a target), the likely dynamics are:
- Some players with stronger prosocial tendencies will initially contribute consistently; others with more self-interested or impatient priors will withdraw.
- Because players cannot directly see who contributed (they only see their own summary), coordination and conditional cooperation are imperfect; communication (chat) and reward transfers are the main instruments to encourage cooperation.
- Rewards are costly to send, so they will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation: mainly small positive gifts from cooperators to other cooperators.
- Cooperation commonly starts higher and drifts downward over the long horizon as free-riding becomes tempting; occasional messages or targeted rewards can produce temporary rebounds. Near the end of 26 rounds, many players will defect (withdraw) to capture immediate private gains; a few persistent cooperators may keep contributing late into the game, and sometimes a small last-round cooperative gesture appears.

I simulate a plausible trajectory that reflects these tendencies: an early phase with many cooperators (especially DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE), a mid-game slump with some experimentation and returns after occasional coordination via chat/rewards, and a gradual decline into the endgame with mostly withdrawals, then a brief last-round cooperative gesture.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and binary contribution choices each round (either contribute the full 20 to the group pot, or withdraw it all and contribute 0). The public pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so full group cooperation yields a high social return, but any single defector gains by free-riding on others. Players can also punish (costly to source, costly to target) or reward (costly to source, beneficial to target) after seeing the aggregate redistribution results. Messages are allowed but identities of who punished or rewarded each player are not revealed in the round summaries, making targeted reciprocity harder.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players start cooperative (high patience and loss tolerance in the roster), while a few (those with high gain-seeking or strong mental accounting for private money) defect immediately. Small, targeted punishments by cooperators will show up to deter persistent free-riding.
- Middle rounds: if punishment and occasional rewards are used, cooperation can stabilize or increase (cooperators invest in enforcement, some defectors switch to contributing). Punishment is usually sparse (players are reluctant to pay cost unless defection is persistent).
- Late rounds: as endgame approaches, incentives to defect rise; punishments become less frequent and cooperation tends to decline. Some players continue cooperating out of norms or habit, while others switch to free-riding.
- Messages will be sparse and purposeful — short accusations, calls for cooperation, short thanks or threats — and not every round will have chat.

I will simulate the full transcript round-by-round. Each round shows a brief explanation of the expected forces at work, any chat messages, the contributions in the exact avatar order given, the (sparse) punishment/reward tuples, and the round summary stage.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 anonymous players against a repeated social dilemma. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and can either contribute all (20) or keep all (0). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and divided equally, so group earnings are maximized when many contribute, but each individual has a short-run incentive to free-ride. Because decisions are simultaneous and private within each round, players must form expectations about others' behavior and respond to the limited feedback: their own round payoff and aggregate-looking information about who contributed / how much they punished or rewarded (but not which individual sent which sanction).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically show some cooperation mixed with a few free-riders. Small, targeted punishments and occasional rewards are used sparingly to enforce cooperation.
- Punishment is costly to the sender, so it tends to be limited in frequency and intensity. Rewards are also used but less often.
- If punishment and rewards successfully deter free-riding, cooperation can stay high for several rounds. If sanctions are inconsistent or too costly relative to benefits, free-riding drifts upward.
- Toward the final rounds, backward induction incentives predict unraveling: defection becomes more common as the end approaches because future retaliation is less credible. Thus we expect cooperation to decline in the last few rounds.
- Uncertainties: individual differences (patience, risk preferences) and stochastic choices mean some players may sporadically defect or punish even late; punishments or rewards may appear in rounds where they are not strictly needed.
