game_explanation: This is a 26-round public goods game with 12 players. Each round players decide privately whether to contribute all 20 coins to the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraw all 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so defectors (who keep 20 privately) earn the private 20 plus their equal share of the multiplied pot, making defection individually attractive in a one-shot sense. Because players never see others' individual choices — they only see their own round payoff and reward activity — the main social signals are: sparse chat messages and the presence or absence of rewards. Rewards are costly to give (1 coin per reward unit) but directly increase a recipient's earnings by the same amount; they can be used to encourage cooperators or tacitly signal disapproval by withholding rewards from defectors. Over repeated rounds, we expect an initial norm of cooperation among several players (especially those with patient or strong mental accounting tendencies), with a minority of early defectors. Without identifiable punishment, cooperation tends to drift downward as free-riding pays off, but selective rewarding can temporarily sustain cooperation among clusters of reciprocating players. Chat will be sparse and often used to request norms, call out behavior in general terms, or thank. Rewards will be used sparingly — typically 1–2 units — mostly to reinforce cooperating peers; some players may experiment with defecting and returning to cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round public goods game with 13 players who each decide whether to contribute their full endowment (20 coins) or keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so cooperative contributions help the group but are individually costly if many others free-ride. Players can reward others after seeing contributions; each reward unit costs 3 to the giver and gives 4 to the recipient. Rewards are costly and typically used sparingly to encourage cooperators or reciprocate kindness.

Likely dynamics: a core of players with stronger pro-social or mental-accounting tendencies will contribute early, while more opportunistic or low-accounting players will free-ride. Because players see round outcomes, contributions will tend to decline over time as free-riding is observed and, importantly, because the finite 14-round horizon encourages unraveling. Occasional reward transfers will be used to reinforce cooperation early on (small 1-unit tokens), but reward use will become rarer as contributions fall. A highly consistent, rule-driven contributor (e.g., MOOSE in these priors) will likely sustain contributions longer than others. By the final rounds, cooperation will be low or zero.

game_explanation:
This 12-player public goods game will show the usual tension between short-term private incentives and longer-run group benefits. Every round each player either withdraws the full 20 coins (keeps 20 privately; contributes 0) or leaves the 20 in the pot (contributes 20). The sum of contributions is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly — so one cooperator's contribution benefits everyone, but contributors bear the immediate private cost. Punishment and reward after each redistribution allow conditional strategies: a few players will punish repeated free-riders (at a cost to themselves) and some will reward cooperators. Because punishments are costly and anonymous on the receiving side, they are used sparingly and mainly against persistent defectors; rewards are rare but used to encourage cooperation. Chat appears occasionally to coordinate, call out defectors, or signal intentions. Expect early rounds of mixed cooperation with targeted punishments of clear free-riders, a mid-game period with higher cooperation as punishments and rewards stabilize behavior, and a gradual rise in defection toward the last rounds as endgame incentives kick in.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits strong group gains (contributing 20 yields a multiplied shared return of 2.9 split across 12 players) against a powerful individual temptation to withdraw privately (keep your 20 plus still receive a share of others' contributions). Expect an initial period of mixed play while players learn who will be punished for free-riding. A subset of players with cooperative priors (high patience/mental-accounting or social inclinations) will try to sustain contributions; others with stronger self-interested or risk-taking priors will defect. Punishment and occasional rewards will be used sparingly by cooperators to deter persistent free-riders. Over many rounds we typically see: some rise in cooperation once punishments bite, a stable cooperative phase if enforcement holds, then a late-game decline as the finite horizon leads some to stop paying enforcement costs. Chats will be scarce and purposeful (calls to punish or promises to cooperate). Punishments will be targeted at repeat defectors and remain modest in magnitude; rewards will be rare and small.

# GAME STARTS

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 14-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately decides whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (20) to the group pot or keep it (0). Each contributed 20 is multiplied by 2.6 and the total is split equally among all 13 players; that arithmetic implies each contribution increases every player's payoff by 4 coins (52/13 = 4). Thus the private cost of contributing (20) exceeds the personal return (4), so the dominant individual incentive is to keep (free-ride). However, each contribution benefits the group, so cooperation can raise total group payoffs.

After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish or reward others. Punishment is costly to the sender (3 coins per punishment unit) and very costly to the target (11 coins deducted per punishment unit). Reward is similarly costly to the sender (3 coins per reward unit) but benefits the target (4 coins per reward unit). Because punishments and rewards are public but targets cannot tell which specific peer(s) produced the effect on them, players can still see who spent coins on punishment/reward (and how much) and can infer enforcement/rewarding tendencies.

Likely dynamics: initial rounds typically show mixed behavior—some players test cooperation while many free-ride. Because contributors earn less immediately, contributors occasionally use small punishments or rewards to shape behavior: sparse punishment directed at persistent free-riders (to signal that free-riding will be costly) and sparse rewards to encourage contributors. Punishments are used sparingly (costly to the sender), usually targeted at recurring non-contributors (e.g., early, visible free-riders). Rewards are also used sparingly to sustain cooperation. Over rounds, we expect modest increases in contribution as a few enforcers/rewarders change incentives; occasional relapses occur. In the final rounds, contributions may rise due to established norms or fall if players anticipate no future enforcement—both are possible. Below I simulate a plausible unfolding with sparse, targeted punishments and occasional rewards consistent with the player priors and the general tendencies described above.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

game_explanation: This public-goods game pits private incentives (keep 20 coins) against a group benefit (contributing 20 increases the shared pot, which is multiplied by 2.6 and split across all 13 players). The private incentive to free-ride is strong: keeping your 20 always yields 20 coins more than contributing, regardless of others' behavior. Because the game repeats for 14 rounds, some players with prosocial preferences or concern for reputation may contribute early to signal cooperation; others will exploit that by free-riding. Historically, contributions tend to start moderate and decline as free-riding is observed. Rewarding lets players transfer costly positive incentives to others (cost 3 per unit, beneficiary gains 4 per unit), which can partially offset the cost of contributing and support cooperation. However, rewards are given anonymously from the recipient's point of view (players see amounts received but not sources), reducing direct reciprocity signals. Given the provided personas, I expect an initial cluster of contributors composed of the more prosocial/mentally-accounting players (e.g., DUCK, MOOSE, OWL, RABBIT, some high-patience types), then a gradual decline as defectors accumulate higher round payoffs; sparse, small positive rewards will mostly target contributors early on and occasionally sustain a few cooperators mid-game. Rewards will be scarce and small (1 unit most of the time).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This public-goods game (14 rounds) combines a simple binary contribution (0 or 20) with an endogenous reward stage (costly prosocial transfers). Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds: many players who are patient or have strong loss/gain signals (and those who treat money as a collective category) tend to contribute, while a subset (low patience / opportunistic types) free-ride. Because choices are simultaneous, contributions initially reflect priors about others rather than immediate reciprocity.
- Reward use is sparse and targeted. Players who contribute are selectively rewarded (usually small units) by a few other contributors to signal approval and sustain cooperation. Senders weigh the 3-coin cost per reward unit against the 4-coin benefit to the recipient, so they rarely give large amounts.
- Over rounds: if contribution levels stay high and reward feedback is observable (aggregate received but not from whom), conditional cooperators keep contributing and reward selectively. If free-riding persists, cooperation erodes slowly as conditional cooperators withdraw contributions or stop rewarding.
- Endgame (last rounds): the finite horizon pushes down contributions and reward activity. Towards round 13–14, expect more defection and fewer rewards, though a small core of intrinsically cooperative players may continue contributing.
- Uncertainties: specific turns where individuals experiment (briefly switch between contributing and defecting), and occasional rewards from unexpected senders. Overall pattern should be moderate cooperation early, maintained by sparse rewards, then gradual decline and stronger defection near the end.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game presents a clear tension: each round every player must either keep the full 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave the full 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so a single player's contribution increases everyone’s share but costs the contributor more than the personal return. With 12 players the marginal per-player return from one contributor is about 4.83 coins — good for the group, costly for the individual. Because the game runs 26 rounds, players can condition on past outcomes and use the reward stage to encourage cooperation indirectly: rewards are the only direct transfers available and must be positive (you can only give, at a cost to yourself).

Likely dynamics
- Early rounds: uncertainty leads to mixed play. Some typically pro-social, patient players test cooperation by contributing; others test by free-riding. Sparse, targeted positive rewards will be used to signal appreciation for cooperation (and to build reciprocal relationships), but since players cannot see exactly who rewarded whom in the group summary, reward signaling is noisy and partial.
- Middle rounds: if a critical mass of contributors emerges and reciprocating rewards reach cooperating players, conditional cooperators will maintain contributions. Rewarding will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation (small units to a few players). Defectors who repeatedly get no rewards may be indirectly discouraged, though direct punishment is impossible.
- Late rounds: finite horizon effects make defection more attractive toward the end. The last few rounds often see falling contribution rates as forward-looking incentive to sustain cooperation diminishes. Players may reduce rewarding as well when they anticipate fewer future returns.
- Uncertainties and heterogeneity: players differ in patience, risk preferences, and mental accounting. game_explanation:
This PGG pits private incentives (keep 20) against a group benefit: any 20 contributed is multiplied by 2.6 and shared across the 13 players. Individually, defecting (contribute 0) yields a higher immediate private holding unless many others contribute; contributions are socially efficient but personally costly. Punishment (cost 3 to sender, -11 to target per unit) can strongly deter free-riding but is itself costly. Reward (cost 3, +4 to recipient per unit) can encourage cooperators at lower harm to others. Because choices are simultaneous and anonymous, players face uncertainty about others each round; they observe only round outcomes and anonymous sanctioning. Typical dynamics: an initial mix of contributions and defections, occasional targeted punishments of persistent defectors, some small rewards to consistent cooperators, and a drift toward higher cooperation by mid-game if punishments are applied; occasional lapses can occur. Endgame incentives can push some to free-ride late, but if punishing is credible, cooperation tends to remain high.

game_explanation:
This 26-round public goods game gives each player a simple binary choice each round: keep all 20 coins privately (withdraw = 0 to the shared pot) or leave them all in the shared pot (contribute = 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally among the active players, so cooperation can raise everyone's return but is vulnerable to free-riding. Because choices are simultaneous and anonymous, cooperation must be sustained either by intrinsic preference or by post-decision incentives: sparse but targeted punishment (costly to the punisher, more costly to the punished) and occasional rewards. Players know the horizon (26 rounds), so we should expect:

- Early rounds: some experimentation and norm-setting. A cluster of reliably cooperative players (high patience, strong mental accounting) will try contributing; a few opportunistic or impatient players will defect. Cooperators may use light punishment or rewards to signal enforcement or appreciation.
- Middle rounds: if punishment/reward proves credible, cooperation can stabilize and become the group norm. Punishment will be used sparingly (only when defectors persist); rewards will be rare but used to reinforce cooperators.
- Late rounds (endgame): incentives to free-ride grow. Expect a gradual erosion of contributions in the final 3–6 rounds as forward-looking players stop investing in future enforcement. Some committed cooperators still continue, and a few players will attempt last-minute enforcement, but with diminishing effectiveness.

Given the 12-player composition and the provided personas (some highly patient/cooperative players like OWL, CROCODILE, DOG, SNAKE, and some more opportunistic players like PARROT and a mixed SLOTH), I predict an early cooperative majority with a small set of repeat defectors (notably PARROT, and occasional SLOTH lapses), light punishment targeted at repeat defectors, and a gradual decline in contributions approaching the final rounds.

The group pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so cooperation benefits the group but is individually costly if others defect. Because players cannot identify who punished or rewarded them, sanctions are anonymous and work by changing expected payoffs: occasional targeted punishment of defectors can sustain high cooperation; sparse rewards can reinforce cooperators. Early rounds are uncertain — some players "test" others — so we expect a mix of cooperation and defection. A small set of norm-enforcing players (those with higher patience/discipline in the personas) are more likely to impose punishments or give rewards. Punishment use will be cautious (costly to the punisher), and rewards will be even sparser. As the horizon becomes known, cooperation tends to erode toward the end (endgame effect), so cooperation should be highest in the middle of the game and decline in the final rounds. Chat messages will be infrequent and strategic (requests, warnings, thanks, or explicit last-round statements).

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a clear social dilemma. Each player gets 20 coins each round and can either contribute all (20) or nothing (0). The group pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among 13 players. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) from contributing is 2.6/13 ≈ 0.20 per coin, so a 20-coin contribution increases each player's share by about 4 coins. Contributing is welfare-enhancing if many contribute (everyone gets more than if no one contributed), but individually costly: if others contribute, a single defector gets higher payoff in that round than a cooperator. Because contributions are binary and observable before rewards, players can use the reward stage to promote cooperation (reward contributors) or withhold rewards to discourage free-riding.

Uncertainties: some players are dispositionally more cooperative or patient (higher probability to contribute and to reward), others are more tempted to free-ride. Because the game lasts 14 rounds and rewards are costly (3 coins per reward unit) but effective for the target (4 coins per unit), we expect rewards to be used sparingly to signal approval of cooperators and sustain cooperation early on. Typically cooperation will start relatively high (many participants contribute) and then erode as some players experiment with free-riding and reciprocity/retaliation weakens—especially approaching the final rounds. A small core of consistently cooperative players may continue contributing and occasionally exchange small rewards with each other. Reward actions should be few and targeted (1–2 units), often from cooperators to cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 14-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round, everyone decides privately whether to contribute all 20 coins or keep them. The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly, so defecting (keeping 20) gives a higher private return in the short run whenever many others contribute. Because reward is costly for the sender (3 coins per reward unit) but generous to recipients (+4 per unit), rewards are most useful to selectively encourage contributors (not to punish defectors directly). Typical dynamics: early rounds start with some cooperative contributions, free-riders earn higher immediate payoffs and cause cooperation to decline, and a few players may use sparse rewarding to sustain cooperation among a small core. Rewards are usually rare and targeted to repeat cooperators; because recipients cannot identify who paid them, reputational enforcement is partial and noisy. Over 14 rounds I predict an initial moderate level of cooperation that drifts downward, occasional revivals as some players (especially those with higher patience/mental accounting cues) reward core cooperators, and relatively sparse reward edges each round.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public goods game with 12 players. Each round every player effectively chooses whether to keep the 20 coins privately (contribute 0 to the public pot) or to leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally across all players, so each coin placed in the pot returns roughly 0.242 coins to each player — individually costly but collectively productive. Because the game is finite and players know it ends after round 26, there is pressure toward free-riding (taking the 20 privately) as the horizon approaches. However, many players have pro-cooperation priors (high patience, risk-taking linked to cooperation), and the presence of a reward stage (positive transfers to others at one coin per unit) allows cooperators to reward each other and try to sustain contributions.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds: several patient/cooperative players will contribute, and a few defectors will exploit the pot. Cooperators will sometimes reward other cooperators (small amounts) to sustain cooperation.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators will react to observed contributions. If cooperation holds, some maintained contributions continue; if exploited repeatedly, cooperators begin to drop out.
- Late rounds: as the end approaches, contributions decline substantially; rewards become rare because sending costs reduce already small gains and there's less incentive to encourage future cooperation.
- Chats will be sparse and purposeful: brief expressions of appreciation, requests for reciprocation, or complaints about free-riding.
- Rewards will be sparse, targeted, and small (1–3 units) and mostly directed to those who contributed in that round.

Transcript follows, round-by-round. Avatar order (for contributions) is always:
SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public goods game with 12 players making a binary choice each round: either keep 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave the full 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so cooperators generate group returns but have an immediate private cost. Punishment (cost 1, target loses 2 per unit) and reward (cost 1, target gains 1 per unit) are available after contributions and can be used to enforce norms or thank cooperators. Players see only their own round net payoff and how much they spent/received; they do not see who targeted whom.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others leads to mixed behavior, but with many players having high patience and strong mental accounting, cooperation should be common early. A few risk-prone or low-accounting players may defect occasionally; these will sometimes be met with small punitive actions.
- Midgame: if punishment/reward is used credibly, cooperation stabilizes. Rewards are generally rare; punishment is used sparingly but effectively against repeat defectors.
- Endgame: as the horizon approaches, strategic incentives to defect grow. Cooperation tends to erode in later rounds, especially in last few rounds where punitive threat is muted. Chat is sparse and purposeful (calling out defectors, brief appeals).
- Overall: relatively high cooperation in rounds 1–12 with occasional defectors punished lightly; maintenance through midgame; increasing defection and fewer punishments in rounds 22–26 as endgame effects dominate.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits 13 anonymous players against the incentives to free-ride. Each round players choose 0 or 20 from their private 20-coin endowment. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so a contributor helps the group but personally sacrifices private coins. After payoff redistribution, players may spend costly punishment or reward points to influence future behavior: punishments are costly to the sender and very costly to the receiver; rewards are cheaper and benefit the receiver. Because play lasts 14 rounds and choices are anonymous, we expect:

- Early rounds: relatively high cooperation as many players try contributing, testing others' responses.
- Enforcement: a few norm-sensitive players (high mental-accounting or loss-sensitivity) will punish occasional free-riders, but punishment will be used sparingly (small units, targeted).
- Reward usage: infrequent, small positive rewards to encourage sustained contributors.
- Conditional behavior: many players will respond to punishment by shifting toward contribution; opportunistic players may defect repeatedly.
- Endgame decline: near the final rounds, contributions fall as backward-induction incentives dominate; punishments become rare in the last round since they no longer affect future behavior.
- Uncertainty: because punishers/rewarders are anonymous, players face uncertainty about who enforces norms, producing variability in responses; overall cooperation tends to drift down across rounds but is stabilized somewhat by occasional enforcement.

Players see one or two free-riders from round 1; some norm-enforcers begin targeted, low-cost punishments to discourage free-riding. A small positive reward appears to encourage a reliable contributor.
With a second round of targeted punishment at an obvious free-rider, enforcers escalate slightly but remain sparing. A small reward is used to reinforce cooperative behavior.

The targeted free-rider responds to pressure and switches to contribution. With near-universal cooperation, players mostly refrain from punishment and occasionally send a small reward to signal approval.
After a round of full cooperation, a couple of opportunists test the waters again. Norm-enforcers respond with small punishments; another small reward appears for a steady contributor.
Punishment signals work for many short-term defectors; most return to contributing. Players relax enforcement for this round.

With the endgame approaching, a cluster of three players (DUCK, RABBIT, COW) defect together. Enforcers still respond, but punishment frequency remains low because sanctions are costly for senders.
Penultimate round: many anticipate final-round collapse and some stop contributing. Enforcers still deliver a few isolated punishments, but players conserve resources.
Final round: backward-induction incentives dominate. Most players expect no future returns to reputation-building, so nearly everyone free-rides; no costly punishment/reward is expected.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and a 2.9 multiplication of the shared pot. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 (by withdrawing 0) or takes the full 20 privately (contribution 0). The private, individual incentive is to take the 20: the personal return to one extra contribution is 20 * 2.9 / 12 ≈ 4.83, well below the 20 cost. So unilateral defection is individually tempting every round.

However, players can send costly rewards after seeing contributions: each reward unit costs 1 for the sender and gives 1 to the recipient. Rewards allow cooperators to be compensated directly and can sustain cooperation if some players are willing to pay to encourage contributors. Because players cannot see who rewarded them in the summary, rewards act as private transfers that signal or sustain norms only indirectly (senders know who they rewarded, but recipients only see an aggregate received amount). Chat is allowed and may be used sparingly to coordinate or signal intent, but messages are public and non-binding.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix of behavior. Several players have high patience and high mental accounting (e.g., MOOSE, RABBIT, SLOTH) and are likely to contribute repeatedly or to reward cooperators. Some players show high gain-seeking or low aversion (e.g., DOG, SNAKE), suggesting occasional free-riding. CHICK and CHICKEN (high patience, strong loss aversion) may contribute frequently. PARROT and CROCODILE are somewhat inconsistent; PARROT's low mental-accounting score suggests more opportunistic choices. Overall dynamic likely: moderate cooperation early as some players try to establish a norm, small positive rewards to encourage contributors, occasional breakdowns as players test the norm, mid-game stabilization when rewarders step in, and then a gradual decline toward the end as final-round incentives to defect loom — though stubborn cooperators (especially MOOSE and some others) may continue contributing late into the game. Chat will be sparse and typically short encouragements or complaints. Rewards will be used sparingly and targeted at consistent cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public-goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds. Each round players choose either to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment or keep it (0). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all 13 players, so a cooperating player's direct monetary return (from the pot) can be lower than a defector's if many free-ride. After payout, players can spend costly reward units (cost 3 to giver, benefit 4 to a target) to reward others; rewards are private (players see amounts received but not who sent them). Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Social dilemma: Contributing benefits the group but is individually costly. With 2.6 multiplier and 13 players, the per-player return from one contributor is 2.6/13 = 0.2 coins — less than the 20 coins cost, so individually, defection (keeping 20) is attractive.
- Heterogeneous priors: Many players have high mental-accounting or high patience tendencies (SLOTH, OWL, CROCODILE etc.), making them more likely to start cooperative. Some players (DUCK, CHICK, COW, CHICK) have lower patience or other cues and are more likely to free-ride.
- Reward mechanism can sustain cooperation: Cooperators will sometimes reward other cooperators modestly to encourage them; because rewards are costly, players typically reward sparingly (1 token or occasionally 2). Rewards are positive-only, so defectors are rarely punished directly — lack of reward functions as weak social sanction.
- Learning & end-game: Early rounds often show higher cooperation (experimentation and social preferences). Seeing that defectors earn more in a given round tends to push conditional cooperators toward defection in subsequent rounds. As the fixed horizon (14 rounds) approaches, backward induction and selfish incentives increase defection (end-game effect). Expect cooperation to be relatively high in early rounds, wobble midgame as players react to payoffs, then decline near the end. Rewards will be sparse and concentrated among stable cooperators; mutual small reciprocation is likely among a few players.

Some midgame updating: a couple of previously cooperative players (PARROT, MOOSE) experiment with defection after observing defectors got larger private payoffs. Conditional cooperators keep contributing but the pool shrinks a bit. Rewards are still sparse and mainly among stable cooperators.

As more players see the payoff gap favoring defectors, a few additional conditional cooperators with weaker incentives switch to free-riding. The pot shrinks further; reward activity declines as cooperators are more cautious about spending limited round earnings.

A modest recovery: some players who briefly defected return to cooperating (perhaps nudged by prior warm reciprocity or expectation of future rounds). Cooperators reciprocate with a few small rewards to signal continued mutual support.

A relatively stable cooperative plateau; players who have been reliably cooperating continue to do so. Rewarding remains cautious but reciprocal among a couple of pairs.
As the horizon shortens, some players again defect (or revert to defecting) to grab the private benefit; cooperation dips. A small number of cooperators still reward trusted partners.

The trend toward more defection continues: a few previously cooperative players (e.g., PARROT, MOOSE) stay out, further reducing the public pot. Given shrinking returns and concerns about end-game, players largely withhold costly rewards this round.

A small bounce — a couple of players briefly return to cooperating (or maintain cooperation) and a modest, targeted reward appears to support a cooperating partner. But overall the downward pressure on cooperation continues as the final rounds approach.
Late-stage collapse becomes visible: many players choose to defect to secure immediate gains. A small handful of steadfast cooperators still trade minimal rewards among themselves.

Very few cooperators remain (some players who strongly prefer keeping accounts intact or who have consistently cooperated). Those remaining cooperators briefly exchange small rewards to reinforce mutual cooperation, but the general trend is toward defection.
With only 2 very persistent cooperators left, mutual small rewards persist between them. Most players have switched to defection anticipating the end of the game.

Final round: standard end-game logic predicts widespread defection because there is no future to incentivize cooperation. Rewards are absent because giving is costly and no future reciprocity is expected.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and a strong individual incentive to withdraw (defect). Each contributed 20 increases the group pot by 20*2.9 = 58 which is split among 12 players (≈4.83 to each player). So contributing is socially efficient but individually costly; the dominant one-shot action is to withdraw (contribute 0). Because players see only aggregated outcomes and anonymous punishment/reward amounts, enforcement depends on visible norms, early signaling, and occasional costly sanctioning or rewarding.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often start with a modest cluster of cooperators who test mutual cooperation; a handful of defectors exploit the group. Cooperators may use sparse punishment and occasional small rewards to try to maintain cooperation.
- Punishment tends to be targeted at obvious free-riders (repeated defectors), but costly punishment is used sparingly — enough to signal consequences but not so much that cooperators bleed themselves.
- Rewards are used even more sparingly, typically to reinforce newly cooperative or consistently cooperative players.
- Over time, if punishment/reward is insufficient or if defectors proliferate, cooperation decays. Near the known end (final rounds), contributions typically collapse as forward-looking incentives disappear.
- Expect chat to be sparse and purposeful (requests to cooperate, brief complaints or explanations, occasional encouragement).

I predict moderate cooperation at the start (several contributors), some directed punishment and occasional rewards in early-to-mid rounds, gradual decline in contributions, and near-zero cooperation in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game sets up the standard tension: each round everyone gets 20 coins and can either keep it (0 contribution) or put it into a shared pot (20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all 13 players, so contributors increase the group return but individually create an incentive to free-ride (keep 20, still receive the shared return). That creates recurring strategic uncertainty: cooperators want others to contribute, defectors want to exploit cooperators.

Two post-contribution instruments change dynamics: (1) punishment (cost to sender 3, -11 to target per unit) and (2) reward (cost 3, +4 to target per unit). Both are anonymous (players see amounts but not who targeted whom), which reduces direct retaliation but still transmits a group norm via aggregate punishment/reward patterns. Because punishments are costly to send but impose heavy losses on targets, a small number of punishment acts can deter repeated defection. Rewards are cheaper as incentives to encourage cooperation but give a smaller per-unit effect.

Given player priors, expect a mix of behavior. Some avatars (high patience, strong mental accounting) are predisposed to sustain contributions and to use punishment sparingly to enforce norms (e.g., CROCODILE, OWL). Others with lower patience or more risk-seeking tendencies may test free-riding early (e.g., GORILLA, PARROT sometimes). Typically cooperation starts moderately, rises if transgressors are punished or cooperators reward each other, then declines somewhat near the end (endgame effect) when future reciprocation is worthless. Punishment/reward events will be sparse and targeted, not wholesale: real players punish/reward infrequently.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players for 26 rounds. Each round every player either leaves the entire 20-coin endowment in the shared pot (contributes 20) or withdraws it all (contributes 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so when many people contribute the per-person share is much larger. Because withdrawing gives you 20 coins privately while contributing forgoes that 20, the short-run private incentive is to withdraw (defect). Rewards are costly (1 coin per reward unit; the recipient gains 1), and are the only mechanism for peer sanctioning or encouragement. Players can see revealed contributions each round and can adapt their behavior; rewards and chat are sparse and typically used to reinforce cooperation. Toward the middle of the session we expect some defections to appear as players test whether cooperators will be rewarded enough to offset the temptation to withdraw. Rewards will be used sparingly to encourage contributors early on, then drop as cooperation unravels; in the final rounds the incentive to defect rises (fewer future rounds to influence) so cooperation typically falls further.

Below I simulate likely dynamics given the provided player priors. Early rounds (1–8) show broad cooperation among most players except a couple with low patience; modest rewards flow between cooperators. Mid game (9–18) a subset of players keeps contributing while others defect; rewards are smaller and mostly internal to the cooperating subgroup. Endgame (19–26) shows cooperation concentrated among very few players and minimal rewarding. Chat is sparse and purposeful (praise, short calls to cooperate, or short protests), and rewards are used sparingly.

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a tension between private incentives (keep 20 coins) and collective benefit (each contributed 20 is multiplied by 2.6 and shared). Because the multiplier (2.6) divided across 13 players yields a small per-player return on any single contribution, the individually rational choice is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, repeated interaction across 14 known rounds and the presence of costly punishment and rewarding allow conditional cooperation to emerge: some players will contribute if they expect enough others to do so, and they may use small punishments to deter persistent free-riders or small rewards to encourage cooperators. Punishment is costly to the sender (3 coins per unit) but strongly harmful to the target (−11 per unit), so it is used sparingly and targeted. Rewards are likewise costly (3 per unit) but slightly beneficial to the recipient (+4 per unit), so they are also used selectively.

Uncertainties: who is a persistent free-rider versus a conditional cooperator is not directly observable before choices, so players form priors based on early rounds and respond. Early rounds typically show higher cooperation; mid-game can see defections or enforcement; end-game (round 14) often shows a collapse toward defection because there are no future rounds to influence. I will simulate a plausible trajectory: many players begin by contributing; a small set of persistent defectors appears; cooperators use selective punishment or small rewards to shape behavior; cooperation fluctuates and then falls toward the end as the finite horizon is reached.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players and 26 rounds. Each round each player either contributes the whole 20 coins (cooperate) or withdraws it all (defect). The group pot is multiplied by 2.9 and shared equally, so when many contribute the group payoff is high and individual payoff from contributing can exceed keeping the 20. Players can send small positive rewards to others after seeing contributions; these rewards serve as incentives for cooperation (rewarding cooperators) or selective exclusion (not rewarding defectors). Players know the horizon (26 rounds), so strategic decline in cooperation toward the end is possible (endgame effect). Given the personas, I expect:

- Early rounds: many players (those with high patience / mental-accounting) will try contributing, while a few impulsive or low-patience profiles (RABBIT, CROCODILE, SNAKE) may free-ride. Cooperators will use sparse rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- Middle rounds: reward-driven reciprocity will raise cooperation among social types (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT, MOOSE, OWL, CHICK), but occasional defections persist. Some players (SLOTH, DOG, CHICKEN) will often cooperate but respond to lack of reciprocity with reduced rewarding.
- Later rounds: cooperation drifts downward as the end approaches; rewards are used less, and more players defect in the final rounds. Chat is sparse and targeted (calls for cooperation, mild admonitions, short promises).

game_explanation:
This 26-round public goods game pits a dozen distinct personalities against the classic temptation: keep the 20 coins privately (defect) or leave them in the shared pot (cooperate). With the pot multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, cooperation benefits the group but individual gain is larger if you withdraw while others contribute. Because contributions are binary (0 or 20), decisions are stark: full cooperation or full private take each round.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: Many players will test, and a handful of initial free-riders will be met with sparse punishments and occasional rewards to signal norms. Cooperative, patient players (GORILLA, DOG, OWL, CROCODILE) will tend to contribute; impulsive or high risk players (SLOTH, DUCK, SNAKE) are more likely to defect early.
- Enforcement: Punishments (costly, anonymous to recipients) will be used sparingly to deter free-riding. Rewards will be used even more sparingly to encourage cooperators.
- Stabilization: If punishments effectively deter defectors, cooperation can persist for many rounds. If punishments are under-used or too costly, cooperation begins to erode.
- Endgame: Knowing the game ends after round 26, forward-looking players will gradually reduce enforcement and some will defect in later rounds. Cooperation typically declines toward the final rounds (a classical endgame effect).
- Uncertainty: Because punishers are anonymous and players only see their own net payoff, second-order beliefs (who will punish later) will shape choices. Expect small, purposeful chat messages rather than long debates.

The transcript below simulates these forces: sparse chat, selective punish/reward actions, early norm enforcement, a period of high cooperation, and gradual erosion toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits 13 players against a simple social dilemma each round: each can keep 20 coins or contribute all 20 to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns only 0.2 coins to its contributor — individually costly but collectively beneficial. Because contributions are all-or-nothing and players see contributions before a small-cost/high-benefit reward stage, we should see these tendencies:
- Some players with stronger pro-social/mental-accounting tendencies will contribute early to sustain mutual gains; others who are more opportunistic or low in mental accounting will free-ride.
- The reward stage provides a targeted incentive to sustain cooperation: cooperators who reward (or are rewarded) can be stabilized. But rewarding is costly (3 coins per reward unit) so rewards will be sparse and mostly 1-unit gifts to reinforce reciprocation or reward visible cooperators.
- Over repeated rounds, absent strong, consistent rewarding, cooperation typically drifts downward as free-riding pays off, so contributions are likely to decline across the 14 rounds. Early rounds will show more cooperation and reciprocal rewards; later rounds will show fewer contributions and rarer rewards.
- There's uncertainty: some players (high patience or high mental-accounting) may stick to contributing longer, and occasional reputational reciprocity in rewards can sustain small cooperative clusters. Final-round behavior is also uncertain: some may "leave a legacy" by contributing, others will defect knowing there's no future.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, binary contribution choices (0 or 20), a pot multiplier of 2.6, and voluntary costly positive rewards (each reward unit costs the giver 3 and grants the receiver 4). Individually, contributing is costly: a 20-coin contribution returns only 4 coins to the contributor (because each contributor raises everyone’s share by 4), so free-riding (keeping your 20) dominates in the one-shot sense. Socially, if enough players contribute, everyone can earn substantially more than the 20 endowment.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show a mix of cooperation and defection. Many players with high patience/other cues are biased toward contributing; a subset with low patience or high risk-taking may defect.
- Conditional cooperation is common: many players will match others’ observed contributions from previous rounds. Because contributions are revealed after the contribution stage, players can reciprocate in the next rounds.
- Costly positive rewards are used sparingly to reinforce cooperators. Typical reward behavior in experimental PGGs: modest, selective rewards to cooperators (1 or sometimes 2 units), rarely to defectors. Rewards help sustain cooperation, but they are limited because each unit costs the sender.
- Uncertainty and heterogeneity (different priors/personas) create fluctuations — occasional defections, occasional returns to cooperation. Toward the end of a finite-horizon game, cooperation typically unravels (fewer contributions and fewer rewards), with the final round often showing heavy defection.
- Because players cannot identify who rewarded them, mutual reciprocity emerges indirectly (reciprocation tends to be directed toward players who contributed, rather than toward specific rewarders).

Below is a simulated round-by-round transcript that follows these patterns: early moderate-to-high cooperation, sparse selective rewards reinforcing cooperators, occasional switches, and a gradual endgame decline in cooperation and rewards.

This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, a binary contribution choice each round (contribute all 20 or withdraw all 20), a multiplier of 2.9 split equally, and a costly reward stage (1 coin spent = 1 coin given). Key dynamics to expect:

- Early rounds: many players will try cooperating (contribute 20) to reap the group return; a few low-patience or opportunistic players will free-ride (contribute 0). Cooperators will use small positive rewards to reinforce other cooperators.
- Middle rounds: cooperation tends to erode if free-riding persists and rewarding is sparse or unrewarded; some players will switch between strategies (conditional cooperators who reward those they think deserve it). Because reward sources are anonymous, targeted reciprocity is blurred — players can see how much they received but not who sent it — so rewards tend to be modest and dispersed.

- Uncertainties: some high-patience/high-mental-accounting players may sustain cooperation longer; others may defect earlier if they see little difference in payoff or if they anticipate decline. Chat messages are likely sparse, with occasional exhortations or calls-out.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and the option to reward others after contributions are revealed. Each round players choose whether to leave the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw them (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so an individual who contributes loses private payoff relative to free-riding but helps the group. Rewards are costly to the giver and beneficial to the recipient (1 coin out, 1 coin in), and are visible as transfers but recipients do not know who gave them. Because players know the horizon (26 rounds), we expect:

- Early rounds: uncertainty about others’ intentions but some stable cooperators (high patience / prosocial priors) will contribute. Those cooperators will occasionally reward other cooperators to signal appreciation and sustain cooperation.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators respond to others’ behavior. If cooperation holds, rewards help sustain it; if defections appear, cooperation can unravel as conditional cooperators withdraw their contributions.
- Late rounds / endgame: as the end approaches, incentives to free-ride increase. Contribution rates will tend to decline, and rewards become rarer. By the final round most players plausibly defect (contribute 0).
- Chat is sparse and mostly used to declare intentions or comment on trends. Rewards are used sparingly and target contributors to encourage cooperation; punishment via negative transfers is not possible, so non-reward (withholding reward) serves as the primary sanction.

Below is a round-by-round transcript (round explanations, occasional short chat, contributions in avatar order, sparse rewards, and the summary stage).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with 13 players and 14 rounds. Each round every player privately decides whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to a shared pot (contribute = 20) or keep it (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all 13 players, so a single contributed 20 coin returns 20 * 2.6 / 13 ≈ 4 coins to each player (total ≈ 36 coins back to everyone if many contribute). Individually, keeping your 20 and letting others contribute often yields a higher immediate payoff (you keep 20 plus your share of the multiplied pot), so the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride. Punishment and reward are available afterwards: each punishment unit costs the source 3 coins and deducts 11 coins from the target; each reward unit costs the source 3 coins and gives the target +4. Because punishments and rewards are anonymous in the summary, players cannot target-react to specific senders, but they can adapt their play based on how often they were punished/rewarded overall.

Given the one-shot advantage to free-riding and the finite 14-round horizon, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds often show relatively high cooperation from many players (noise and social preferences), with some defectors who exploit cooperators.
- Sparse punishment and occasional rewards are used to discourage free-riding and encourage cooperators; because punishment is costly to the punisher, it is used sparingly.
- Over rounds, cooperation tends to decline as some players learn that defecting yields higher payoffs, especially toward the end (end-game effect). Punishment can slow the decline but usually does not fully stop it.
- Rewards are used less often than punishments but can appear to reinforce steady contributors.
I will simulate a plausible sequence consistent with these tendencies: an initially high contribution rate that gradually drifts down with occasional targeted punishments and modest rewarding.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game pits short-term private incentives (keeping your 20 coins) against the group benefit of contributing to a shared pot that gets multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20) and players cannot observe others' choices before deciding, there is temptation to free-ride: if enough others contribute, a defector earns more that round. At the same time, contributors can use costly punishment (3 coins per unit, -11 to the target per unit) and costly reward (3 coins per unit, +4 to the target per unit) to shape future behavior. Punishment is effective but costly, so players use it sparingly; rewards are cheaper for the target than punishments are harmful, so they are used occasionally to reinforce cooperation.

Given the player personas, many participants have a tendency toward patience and mental accounting, which biases toward contributing, while a minority with lower patience or low mental-accounting tendencies are more likely to defect. Early rounds typically show relatively high contribution rates as norms and reciprocity expectations have not yet eroded; occasional punishments and rewards help sustain cooperation. Midgame, some players test the waters and cooperation can dip. Toward the final rounds, the incentive to defect strengthens because future retaliation is less credible, so cooperation often falls — especially in the very last rounds, when punishment and reward largely disappear.

Uncertainty is important: players cannot see previous round punishers/rewarders linked to their identities, so punishment serves as a group-level signal but not a personally identifying threat. I expect sparse, targeted punishments of clear defectors and occasional rewards to standout cooperators (especially by strongly prosocial personas like MOOSE, OWL, and GORILLA). Below is a round-by-round transcript predicting contributions and the sparse pattern of punishment/reward actions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, binary contributions each round (either full 20 or 0). Because returns from the public pot are diluted (2.9 multiplier split across 12), the individually dominant action in a single round is to withdraw 20 (defect) and keep it privately. However, cooperators who contribute raise everyone’s shared payoff, and punishments and rewards provide social incentives to sustain cooperation over the 26 rounds.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players will contribute out of intrinsic cooperation (or expectations), while others free-ride. With contributions visible, cooperators will start using light punishment and occasional rewards to discourage defectors and encourage contributors.
- Middle rounds: targeted punishment and strategically placed rewards will shift some previously defecting players toward contributing. Cooperation tends to rise if punishment is credible and not too costly to administer.
- Late rounds: as the end (round 26) approaches, forward-looking selfish players might revert to defection (end-game effect). However, if cooperation has become the norm, most players may continue contributing, with only occasional defections that draw a small number of sanctions.

Uncertainties:
- Who responds strongly to punishment and who tolerates it is uncertain. Some players (with higher loss aversion or mental-accounting tendencies) are more likely to adjust behavior when sanctioned or rewarded.
- Punishment is costly to the punisher and often sparse; rewards are cheaper to sustain cooperative norms in some groups, but less punitive for defectors.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from Round 1 through Round 26. The avatar order is fixed. Contributions are 0 or 20. Punishment/reward tuples are (source_avatar, target_avatar, unit) with negative unit = punishment (cost 1 per unit to source, -2 per unit to target) and positive unit = reward (cost 1 per unit to source, +1 per unit to target). Only non-zero actions are shown. Chat is kept sparse and purposeful.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, each choosing either 0 or 20 per round. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so the group return for a single 20 contribution is 2.6*20 / 13 ≈ 4.0 to each player — a social benefit but individually dominated by keeping the 20 (private return 20 vs shared return ≈4). That structure makes contribution individually costly but beneficial for the group.

Because the game lasts a known finite horizon (14 rounds), standard strategic incentives push toward more defection late in the game. However, the presence of a costly positive-reward stage (each reward unit costs 3 to giver and gives 4 to recipient) creates an informal mechanism to sustain cooperation: contributors can reward other contributors, offsetting some personal costs and signaling reciprocity. Rewards are costly, so they are used sparingly and mainly by players who care about reciprocity, fairness, or maintaining cooperation.

Given the player personas:
- Several players (GORILLA, SNAKE, CROCODILE, RABBIT, MOOSE, DOG) show patterns (high patience, high risk-taking, or strong task-consistency) that make them relatively likely to contribute early and to use small rewards to support cooperators.
- A small cluster (SLOTH, PARROT, OWL, sometimes DUCK) are more likely to free-ride persistently.
- Some players who endorse strong mental accounting (CHICKEN, CHICK, COW, SNAKE, CROCODILE) will tend to stick to a consistent strategy (often cooperative), especially early/midgame.
- Expect modest positive feedback: a core of contributors reward each other lightly (1 unit typically). That sustains contributions in the middle rounds. Toward the final rounds, contributions decline as forward-looking incentives to maintain future cooperation vanish; reward activity tapers too.

Overall predicted unfolding:
- Early rounds: a majority of the "pro-social" cluster contributes; a minority free-rides. Sparse, reciprocal rewards appear among cooperators.
- Middle rounds: contributions remain moderate-to-high as rewards sustain reciprocity; occasional defections reduce rewards for some.
- Endgame (last 3–4 rounds): contributions fall substantially and reward-giving declines as players stop investing in future reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, costly punishment and costly rewards, and full information about the structure but only aggregate/individual summary feedback (no identification of who punished or rewarded). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), each round is a clear cooperate/defect choice: cooperating leaves your 20 in the public pot (which is multiplied), defecting withdraws it for private use and still takes an equal share of the multiplied public pot.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: Many players have pro-cooperative priors (high patience, strong mental accounting) and will contribute to try to create a cooperative norm. However, a subset (players with high gain-seeking or very low patience) are likely to defect early to exploit the group.
- Punishment/reward: Because punishments are costly and anonymous to targets, we expect punishment to be used sparingly and targeted at repeat defectors; a few cooperators will pay small costs to punish defection to sustain cooperation. Rewards will be rarer still, used to reinforce cooperation occasionally.
- Learning and adaptation: Players update based on payoffs and the (anonymized) amounts they see in the summary. If defectors are punished enough to make defection unprofitable, they'll often switch to cooperate; if punishment is rare or too weak, defection can spread.
- Endgame: With a known finite horizon (26 rounds), cooperation tends to erode near the end as the incentive to enforce norms declines. Punishment also declines late since it costs money that cannot be recouped in the final rounds. Expect a relatively high cooperation level early-to-mid game with a gradual or stepwise decline toward defection in the late rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 20-coin endowment per round, binary contribution (0 or 20), and a multiplier (2.6) on total contributions that is equally split. Because the pot is multiplied and split among all players, a lone cooperator is directly exploited: contributing 20 returns less to the contributor than keeping it unless enough others contribute. Free-riding (contributing 0) gives a strictly higher immediate round payoff when many others contribute. The reward stage (cost 3 per reward unit to giver, +4 to receiver) provides a channel for cooperators to reward other cooperators and to encourage contribution — but it is costly so rewards are used sparingly.

Given the player personas, expect a core set of fairly consistent cooperators (players with high patience and high mental-accounting: SLOTH, DUCK, CHICKEN, MOOSE, OWL, CROCODILE) who start contributing and often reward fellow contributors. Some players are more opportunistic or risk-seeking (GORILLA, SNAKE, RABBIT, DOG) and will frequently free-ride, especially early and late (endgame). Others (PARROT, CHICK, COW) may be conditional: joining cooperation after seeing rewards or social returns.

Typical unfolding predicted here:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions (a substantial minority contributes). Cooperators give small positive rewards to other contributors; free-riders reap higher immediate payoffs and are rarely rewarded.
- Middle rounds (with repeated reward feedback): contributions rise as some conditional players join after receiving rewards or seeing a reasonably high pot.
- Late rounds (endgame): forward-looking players anticipate last-round collapse and reduce contributions; cooperation erodes approaching round 14. Rewarding likewise declines toward the end.

Rewards are expected to be sparse and small (mostly single-unit rewards), focused on reinforcing contribution, sometimes targeting new contributors to encourage them. Below is a round-by-round transcript that follows these patterns.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with 12 players and 26 rounds. Each player must either keep all 20 coins (contribute 0) or put all 20 into the group pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally — so a single-round selfish incentive favors keeping the 20 (you avoid the cost and still share the return). However, punishment (costly to the punisher, harsher on the target) and reward (cheaper, benefits target) allow social enforcement of cooperation. Because the horizon is finite and known (26 rounds), backward induction tends to push people toward defection in later rounds; but conditional cooperators, patient types, and those with strong mental-accounting are likely to contribute early on. Expect: moderate initial cooperation with sparse rewards/punishments to sustain it, then gradual decline as rounds progress, with some intermittent attempts (chat, targeted rewards/punishments) to stabilize contribution levels. Punishment will be used sparingly — usually when a defector persists — and rewards will be occasional recognitions of consistent contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a standard public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round, and a multiplier (1.9) that yields a per-contribution group return of 1.9*20/10 = 3.8 per player. Contributing 20 therefore costs the contributor 20 privately but returns only 3.8 to each player (including the contributor) — a net private loss of 16.2 for the contributor when others don't also contribute. The dominant (selfish) incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) every round. Heterogeneous social preferences and beliefs, plus the ability to send messages and the visibility of each player’s round payoff after each round, create the main dynamics:

- Early rounds: some players with stronger pro-social priors or high “mental accounting” may try to contribute to test cooperation. Because individual contributions are costly and the group return is small per contributor, contributors will often be rewarded by higher payoffs for free-riders, which tends to undermine cooperation quickly.
- Middle rounds: once contributors observe they were exploited (peers getting higher net payoffs), many stop contributing. A few persistent cooperators or those seeking to signal norms may occasionally contribute, but overall contribution rates are expected to be low.
- Endgame (final rounds): knowing the horizon typically leads to more defection; some players may still make a symbolic contribution in the last round(s). Messages can briefly coordinate small coalitions, but given payoff structure, sustained high cooperation is unlikely.

Uncertainties: exact timing of coordination attempts, who will stubbornly keep contributing, and whether any short-lived coalition forms after messages. Chat is typically sparse — a few promises, occasional expressions of frustration or offers to coordinate.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with 15 anonymous players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and privately decides how much to contribute (0–20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 3.7, and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns about 0.247 coins to its giver — a personal loss but a net group gain. Left unchecked, the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0); however, many people initially contribute some amount because of pro-social preferences, reciprocity expectations, or misunderstanding of the exact marginal return. Because players do not learn others' intentions before choosing and cannot identify who rewarded them, uncertainty about motives and limited feedback promote cautious, gradually declining cooperation.

The reward mechanism gives players a way to reward others at private cost (4 coins per reward unit) that benefits the target by 5 coins. That creates a small net group benefit from rewarding (+1 per unit) but a personal cost of -4, so rewards are used sparingly and mainly to incentivize or reciprocate cooperators. In practice, we expect:
- An initial moderate level of contributions from those with cooperative priors (high patience/mental-accounting), and low contributions from more opportunistic or risk-seeking types.
- Declining contributions over rounds as free-riding spreads and the marginal private incentive to reduce contributions dominates.
- Rewards concentrated on consistent cooperators and expressed by a few players each round (reciprocal pairs or a few "policers"), not broad or heavy reward use.
- Sparse rewarding overall, mostly 1-unit gifts and occasional reciprocal pairs, with continued anonymity creating uncertainty about the effectiveness of rewarding.

I will simulate nine rounds with these tendencies, reflecting each persona's relative inclination toward cooperation and sparing reward use.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives (keep private coins) against a group benefit: contributions to a shared pot are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. At the end of each round players can spend costly reward units (cost 4 each) to give small positive transfers (5 each) to specific peers. Rewarding is the only form of direct sanction/reinforcement available, and it is costly, so players tend to use it sparingly — typically to reinforce high contributors or reciprocate help rather than to “pay off” large numbers of people. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they treat each round as potentially important and are sensitive to recent signals about others’ cooperativeness.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: many players test the waters with modest-to-high contributions. High contributors (especially very cooperative-seeming avatars like PINGUIN, MONKEY, OWL, CROCODILE, DUCK) get noticed and occasionally rewarded. Rewards are sparse and targeted (single-unit rewards are common).
- Middle rounds: reinforcement of cooperative behavior keeps a cluster of consistently high contributors (and reciprocal rewarding among them). Low contributors (RABBIT, SNAKE in these priors) receive little or no reward; that lack of positive feedback tends to keep them low or marginal.
- Uncertainties: some avatars have mixed priors and may drift (SLOTH, GORILLA). Because rewards are private (receivers don’t know who sent them), reputation-building is noisier and rewards primarily sustain cooperation among a core rather than fully disciplining defectors.

I will simulate nine rounds. For each round I give a short explanation of the immediate incentives and why players choose as they do, then list contributions in the exact player order and the (sparse) reward actions as tuples (source, target, unit). Only non-zero reward edges are included.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game pits 15 players against the classic tension: each round everyone gets 20 coins and can contribute some (0–20) to a common pot that is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. The private incentive is to keep coins; the group incentive is to contribute. Because players see only the summary after each round (not who took which actions) and do not know the number of rounds, we expect the following broad dynamics:

- Early rounds: many players start at moderate contributions. Conditional cooperators raise or sustain contributions if the group return looks good; selfish players contribute little. Because punishment and reward are costly, these are used sparingly early on—small positive rewards to high contributors and occasional punishments of obvious low contributors.
- Middle rounds: contributions tend to drift downward as some players free-ride. Small, targeted punishment of persistently low contributors sometimes appears to enforce cooperation; reciprocated small rewards appear between consistently cooperative pairs.
- Later rounds: without strong incentives, average contributions typically fall further. Punishment remains rare because it costs the punisher as well, but some players who value fairness or have high loss-avoidance may continue to punish very low contributors. Small mutual rewards can appear as social reciprocity.
- Uncertainty and anonymity (players cannot identify who punished or rewarded them) make formal enforcement imperfect. Expect sparse, low-unit punishment and reward (1 unit is common), and overall a gradual decline in average contributions, with occasional localized enforcement actions.

The transcript below simulates rounds 1–9 under these tendencies: contributions evolve gradually, rewards and punishments are rare and small (1 unit), and summaries show per-player round payoffs and the small costs / effects of punishment and reward.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game (PGG) with optional costly punishment is likely to follow a common experimental pattern: some players initially try to establish cooperation, free-riders benefit from withholding contribution, and cooperators respond with occasional punishment to deter defection. Punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per unit) but removes a larger amount (8 coins per unit) from the target, so it can be an effective—but expensive—way to enforce norms. Because the marginal private return from contributing (0.19*20 = 3.8) is much smaller than the private cost (20), the incentive to defect is strong. Expect contributions to start mixed/moderate, a few targeted punishments early to sanction obvious free-riders, and then a gradual decline in contributions as the cost of enforcement and the temptation to free-ride take hold. Chat will be sparse and used mainly to warn, chastise, or coordinate punishment.

game_explanation:
This public goods game is a repeated, simultaneous-move setting with binary contributions (0 or 20) and a costly punishment stage. Early rounds typically serve as tests: some players try cooperation, others free-ride to exploit the multiplication factor. Because the pot returns less than full marginal per contribution (1.9/10 = 0.19 return per coin to each player), individual incentives favor withholding (0) while group welfare favors contributing. Punishment provides a way to deter free-riding: it is costly to the punisher (4 per unit) but doubly damaging to the target (8 per unit), so even a few punishments can shift incentives toward cooperation. Players with high patience and strong mental accounting (e.g., RABBIT, MOOSE, CHICKEN) are more likely to sustain contributions and to punish defectors; players with low patience or mixed tendencies (e.g., SLOTH, PARROT, GORILLA) are more likely to test defections. Over these 15 rounds I expect initial mixed cooperation, prompt targeted punishments against obvious defectors, a rise in cooperation as punishments establish norms, intermittent defections (especially near the end), and sparser punishment in the final round because there is less future to enforce.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone privately chooses either to contribute 20 coins to a shared pot or keep their 20. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns 0.19 to each player (a 20-coin contribution gives each player 3.8 coins). Individually, contributing reduces your personal payoff relative to keeping your coins (you pay 20 but only recover 3.8), so there's a strong individual incentive to free-ride; however if many players contribute the group's total payoff rises.

Given the personas, I expect some initial testing/cooperation from players with stronger norms or mental-accounting tendencies (GORILLA, DOG, DUCK, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK), while others will defect to exploit those contributions. After seeing summary payoffs, most players will notice defectors earn more within a round and cooperation will decline. A few players (especially DOG and GORILLA) are somewhat persistent and may try occasional returns to cooperation, but overall contributions will trend down to mostly zero. Chat will be sparse and used to make short promises, complaints, or explanations; players rarely coordinate reliably.

game_explanation: This public-goods game pits private incentives (keep 20) against collective benefit (contribute 20 to a pot that gets multiplied by 1.9 and split among all 10). With punishment available (cost 4 per unit to the punisher, -8 per unit to the punished), we should expect an initial mixture of cooperation and free-riding, followed by targeted punishment of persistent defectors. Players with pro-social or high mental-accounting tendencies (GORILLA, DUCK, RABBIT, CHICK, CHICK) are more likely to contribute and to spend scarce resources to punish free-riders early on. More self-interested or risk-seeking types (MOOSE, OWL, DOG) are likelier to defect initially. Punishment is typically sparse (1–2 units) and used to signal that defection will be costly rather than to exact maximal revenge. After a few rounds of targeted punishment, most players tend to switch to contributing to avoid losses from punishment; cooperation then stabilizes and punishment largely disappears, except for occasional one-off defections that are punished and corrected.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 anonymous players each round. Each round players choose an integer contribution from 0–20 out of 20 private coins. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 3.7, and split equally. After learning the contributions and redistribution, players can pay to punish or reward others (punish: source pays 4 per unit, target loses 7 per unit; reward: source pays 4 per unit, target gains 5 per unit). Players see peers' contributions and the aggregate punish/reward amounts in summaries, but do not learn who targeted them.

Likely dynamics:
- Many players have priors that favor cooperation (high patience, strong mental accounting), so initial average contributions should be moderate-to-high (roughly half to three-quarters of endowment). A few players (notably COW and some risk-seeking/more opportunistic avatars) will under-contribute or free-ride.
- Punishment and rewards will be used sparingly. Typical patterns: several players will modestly punish consistent free-riders (1 unit here and there) and occasionally reward consistent contributors to stabilize cooperation.
- Over rounds contributions may drift modestly down as some conditional cooperators reduce giving in response to persistent free-riding, but targeted sanctions and occasional rewards will limit collapse. No dramatic, frequent punishment edges — sanctions are sparse and mostly aimed at clear low contributors.
- Because players do not know the game length, endgame unwinding is unlikely; behavior will evolve gradually.

The transcript below shows rounds 1–9. Each round begins with a short explanation for the predicted behavior, then the concrete contributions and the sparse punishment/reward actions (only non-zero edges shown). After actions the game's round summary would be shown to players.
