predicted_rounds.explanation: First round: many players start moderately high from cooperative priors; a few opportunistic players give very little. Punishment/reward is exploratory and light — a few players reward high contributors and a couple lightly punish obvious free-riders to signal norms.

Players see contributions and small sanction/reward moves. Cooperators mostly persist; punished low contributors (DUCK, COW) may stay low or slightly adjust. A few additional light rewards appear to reinforce reliable contributors.

Repeated small punishments against clear free-riders continue. Some conditional cooperators reduce slightly after seeing persistent low contributions; reliable contributors hold steady and get occasional rewards.

Some players react to persistent low contributions by keeping contributions steady or increasing slightly; punishments concentrate on low contributors, while a few rewards continue to support cooperators.
After several rounds of light sanctions against obvious free-riders, a mild rebound in contributions occurs among cooperators. Punishment remains targeted and infrequent.

Behavior stabilizes around a moderate cooperative baseline. Low contributors remain visible and get occasional reprimands; occasional small rewards keep cooperation attractive for consistent contributors.

Players have formed expectations: most high contributors remain so; a small set (including COW and DUCK) persist with near-zero contributions and continue to be singly-targeted by a few punishers. Rewards appear occasionally.

Some fatigue among conditional cooperators leads to small declines in average contributions. Punishment continues to be used sparingly, mainly to signal social norms to persistent free-riders. A few players keep rewarding steady contributors.

Players continue stable, cautious strategies: many maintain mid-to-high contributions, a few persist in near-zero giving and are occasionally punished. Rewards remain infrequent but targeted at reliable cooperators. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, behavior stays gradual rather than collapsing sharply in a final round.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with voluntary rewarding. Each round players choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players can pay 4 coins to give 1 reward unit that gives the recipient +5 coins. Because reward units are individually costly to the giver but increase the recipient (and the group net by +1), rewards tend to be used sparingly to reinforce cooperative contributors rather than to punish free-riders directly. Players don't see who gave them rewards, and rounds are of unknown total length, so we expect an initial willingness to cooperate from prosocial types, persistent low contributions from habitual free-riders, and selective rewarding targeted at consistent high contributors. Over time, cooperation may drift down if low contributors persist and rewards remain small, but visible patterns of who gets rewarded (from the summary info) will sustain some high contributors who seek those small social incentives. Rewarding will remain rare — typically 0–3 small transfers per round — focused on a few reliable cooperators.

Cooperation hovers at a moderate level: core cooperators stay above average, while a persistent subset free-rides or contributes very little. Rewarding remains targeted and minimal, sustaining a small norm of rewarding top contributors.

Players lack knowledge of the end, so behavior mainly reflects ongoing reputational and reciprocity incentives. Those who repeatedly contributed highly (especially CHICK) continue to receive a few small rewards; many low contributors remain unchanged.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private incentives (each coin kept yields 1 to the player) against a social return (each coin contributed yields 3.7 to the pot, or ~0.247 back to each individual when split among 15 players). Rational self-interest predicts zero contribution, but real subjects display heterogeneity: some are conditional cooperators who match others' contributions, some are free-riders who contribute little, and a few are strong prosocials who contribute a lot regardless. Because players can reward others at a cost (4 coins -> gives 5 coins), small positive incentives exist to reinforce high contributors; however, rewards are typically used sparingly because they are costly.

Across rounds I predict an initial mix of moderate-to-high contributions from prosocial types and low contributions by more selfish types. Conditional cooperators will respond to observed peer behavior (via the summary). Sparse rewarding will target above-average contributors, helping sustain cooperation among the prosocial cluster. Over repeated rounds, contributions tend to drift down as free-riding is observed, but targeted rewards by committed cooperators and reciprocation can stabilize cooperation at an intermediate level for some players. Because players do not know the game length, endgame effects (sharp collapse) are unlikely until they suspect the game is ending.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game is a classic social dilemma: each player decides whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or keep it (defect). Any contributed coins are pooled, multiplied by 1.9, then split equally among all 10 players. That means each coin contributed returns only 0.19 to its giver; contributing is costly privately but can raise everyone’s payoff if many contribute. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding, there is uncertainty about how many others will contribute. Communication can help coordinate cooperation, but free-riding is tempting because defectors keep 20 coins plus still receive a share of the public pot.

Given the player personas, I expect some players (especially DOG, CHICK, OWL, MOOSE, RABBIT, CHICK) to favor contributing more often, while others (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT) will lean toward defecting. Early rounds often show moderate cooperation because of norms or pledges; however, when defectors reap larger round payoffs, cooperation usually declines. Occasional attempts to revive cooperation (messages, pledges) can cause short-lived recoveries. Endgame behavior (final rounds) often trends toward defection, since future retaliation or reciprocity is impossible.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated anonymous public-goods game with costly punishment and rewarding. Individually, each coin contributed returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 to its contributor, so selfish incentives favor contributing 0. However, full-group contributions produce much higher group payoffs, so cooperative preferences and reciprocity can support positive contributions. Punishments are costly to the sender (4 coins per unit) but subtract heavily from the target (7 coins per unit); rewards cost 4 to send and give 5 to the recipient. Because punish/reward are visible only as amounts (not who sent them), players use them as aggregate signals of social approval or disapproval. Typical dynamics: some players start reasonably cooperative, some free-ride; cooperators punish low contributors sparsely to discourage free-riding and may reward consistent high contributors. Over rounds, cooperation often drifts downward if punishments are sparse or too costly to sustain, but occasional targeted punishments and rewards can stabilize moderate cooperation. Uncertainty (no known horizon) encourages players to sustain cooperation early but can lead to erosion later if exploitation persists.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits private incentives (keep money: each coin you keep gives you 1) against a weak social return (each coin contributed returns 3.7 total, split across 15 players = ~0.247 per player). The dominant self-interested choice is to contribute 0 each round. Still, many people start by contributing some positive amount because of social preferences, uncertainty about others, or hope of building cooperation. Because players can punish (costly to the punisher) or reward (also costly), a few targeted sanctions/rewards can deter persistent free-riding and sustain moderate cooperation among a subset of players. However punishment costs (4 to sender per unit) and modest effectiveness (−7 to target per unit) mean sanctions are used sparingly; rewards (cost 4 to give +5 to target per unit) are also applied selectively to mark especially cooperative peers.

Uncertainties: players don't know how many rounds remain (no known end), so there is continued incentive to experiment with contributions; yet, with observed patterns of peers' contributions and anonymous sanction/ reward totals, contributions typically drift down if sanctions are rare. I therefore predict moderate initial contributions by many players, sparse targeted punishments of clear free-riders (especially any persistent 0s), occasional small rewards to high contributors, and a gradual decline in average contribution across rounds as some players reduce their giving.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, finite public goods game with 10 identifiable players, high visibility of punishers, and a cost to punishing (4 coins per punishment unit; target loses 8 per unit). Standard incentives: contributing (20) reduces your private holding but increases the shared pot; defecting (0) keeps your private holding while still reaping the shared benefit. Without enforcement, defection typically yields higher immediate payoff, so we expect some initial free-riding. Because punishers are identifiable and punishment can deter future defection, we expect punishments to appear when free-riders are visible and to push many punished players toward cooperation in subsequent rounds. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it will be used sparingly and mainly to enforce norms early- to mid-game. As the end approaches (final rounds), the threat of future retaliation weakens and some players may defect more, producing an end-game decline in contributions with fewer punishments.

Expect the group to settle into high cooperation for several middle rounds after early punishments, with a few intermittent defections that are punished lightly. In the final rounds, cooperation will likely drop as players exploit the inability to credibly threaten future punishment. Chat will be sparse: brief calls to cooperate, short reproaches after defections, occasional praise when cooperation stabilizes.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 players each round receiving 20 coins. Each coin contributed to the public pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally: the marginal per-player return on each coin contributed is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247, which is well below 1. That makes defection (contribute 0) the individually dominant action in a one-shot sense: every coin kept yields 1 to the individual, while contributing yields only ≈0.247 back to them personally. Group welfare, however, is maximized if everyone contributes more.

Because the game repeats (players do not know when it will end), we expect some conditional cooperation: many players with cooperative priors or high patience/mental-accounting will contribute moderately at first, testing others. Free riders (players with low patience or low inclination to cooperate) will contribute little or nothing; that creates tension. Since players can punish and reward (at a cost to themselves), punishments can deter free riding — but punishment is costly (4 coins per unit to the punisher) and inflicts −7 coins per unit on the target. Rewards cost 4 per unit and give +5 per unit to the target. Because punish/reward are expensive, players use them sparingly: a few targeted punishments of persistent free riders and occasional small rewards for consistent cooperators are the most likely enforcement behaviors.

Uncertainties include players' private preferences and risk attitudes, and the fact punishers/rewarders are anonymous. This encourages conservative, tit-for-tat-like adjustments: many players will start with modest-to-high contributions, drop contributions slowly if they see others free-riding, and occasionally pay to punish persistent defectors. Over rounds we expect an overall drift downward in contributions as some players tire of policing costs or conclude punishments are too sparse to change defectors’ behavior. The most likely visible enforcement pattern is a handful of low-intensity punishments directed at obvious free riders, plus occasional single-unit rewards to sustain high cooperators.
reflection:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 players each round receiving 20 coins. Each coin contributed to the public pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally: the marginal per-player return on each coin contributed is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247, which is well below 1. That makes defection (contribute 0) the individually dominant action in a one-shot sense: every coin kept yields 1 to the individual, while contributing yields only ≈0.247 back to them personally. Group welfare, however, is maximized if everyone contributes more.

game_explanation:
This 10-player public goods game gives each player a binary choice each round: keep their 20 coins or contribute them to a shared pot that returns 1.9× the total contributions split equally across all players. That makes contribution individually costly (you give up 20) but collectively beneficial because each contributor increases everyone's share by 3.8 coins. Since players see only aggregate outcomes after decisions, enforcement of cooperation comes through costly punishment: a punisher pays 4 coins per punishment unit to deduct 8 coins per unit from the target. Because punishment is costly and players can identify who punished them, expect the following overall dynamics:

- Early rounds: many players try contributing to establish cooperative norms. A minority will test free-riding because 0 contribution can yield higher immediate payoffs. Loss-sensitive players (high "loss-lottery" tendencies) are likely to punish visible defectors.
- Midgame: targeted, sparse punishment tends to discipline repeat free-riders. Once defectors get netted down by punishment, they often rejoin cooperation. Cooperative rounds with no punishment are common once norms stabilize.
- Late game (especially the final round): end-game temptation increases free-riding because future retaliation is valued less. Punishment frequency often drops in the last round, but some players with strong norm-enforcement preferences may still punish even in the final round.
- Uncertainty: who will punish, and how strongly, is the main source of strategic uncertainty. Punishers act sparingly because punishment is costly, so expect small numbers of 1–2 unit punishments targeted at repeat defectors rather than heavy multilateral punishment.

With the provided personas, I predict an initially cooperative group with a few opportunistic defections (notably by low-patience avatars like OWL and DUCK). Loss-sensitive players (CHICK, CHICKEN, MOOSE, RABBIT, DUCK/CHICK may be complex) will punish defectors occasionally to enforce cooperation. By midgame, cooperation becomes common; sporadic defections are punished. Final-round defections increase, with limited punishment.

game_explanation: This public goods game pits 15 players with varied social and economic priors against repeated anonymous contribution decisions and optional positive rewards. Because contributions are privately chosen each round and multiplied by 3.7 then shared equally, each player faces the usual tension: (1) the private incentive is to keep tokens (contributing zero) while (2) the group's payoff increases if many contribute. The reward stage allows only positive transfers (cost 4 to sender, +5 to receiver), so cooperation is supported indirectly: players can reward high contributors to encourage them, but cannot directly punish low contributors. Typical dynamics: an initial moderately high level of contributions from patient/other-regarding types, a gradual decline as free-riding attracts imitation, and sparse, targeted rewards concentrated on consistently high contributors. Conditional cooperators (high patience, high mental-accounting) will tend to sustain contributions and selectively reward peers; impulsive or low-patience types tend to contribute little and rarely give rewards. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, behavior tends to show gradual adjustment rather than abrupt end-game shifts; rewards remain sparse and used mainly to signal appreciation for above-average contributors.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game with costly punishment and binary contributions (0 or 20) typically shows an early willingness to cooperate, occasional free-riding by a few players, targeted punishment to discipline defectors, and then a gradual erosion of cooperation toward the final rounds (end‑game effect). Players with strong mental-accounting and patience (e.g., SLOTH, GORILLA, DOG, PARROT, RABBIT) are more likely to contribute early and to pay small punishment costs to discourage persistent free‑riders. Players with weaker mental accounting or higher gamble-seeking (e.g., MOOSE, OWL, CHICK) are more likely to test defecting, especially as the horizon shortens. Punishment is costly and therefore used sparingly; it appears most often when a defector repeatedly reduces group returns. Chat is sparse and used to call out or warn persistent defectors; retaliatory punishment is rare in this profile. Over 15 rounds I expect cooperation to rise after early enforcement, stay high for a midphase, then progressively decline as players anticipate the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game (15 rounds) pits individual incentives (keep 20 privately) against group returns (each 20 invested yields 3.8 to every player). The one-shot dominant action is to contribute 0, but with repetition and sparse communication we should see a mix: some players (high mental-accounting, patient types) are inclined to contribute as a fairness/collective strategy; others (low mental-accounting, high gain-seeking) will tend to free-ride or test cooperation. Early rounds typically start with some cooperative pledges, but defectors earn higher immediate payoffs and often cause cooperation to fall. Periodic messages can produce short-lived recoveries if enough conditional cooperators respond, but an end-game decline is likely as the horizon becomes certain. Chats will be sparse and strategic — promises, reminders, or short complaints — and contributions will move up and down as players respond to observed payoffs.

game_explanation: This public goods game pits individual incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a group incentive (contribute so the shared pot grows). The private return per contributed coin is 1 coin kept versus an expected return of 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 coins from the public pot — so the one-shot selfish best response is to contribute 0. Still, many players have social preferences or reputational concerns that lead to positive contributions early on. Because rounds are anonymous and players don't know how many rounds remain, initial rounds often show modest cooperation, sustained by social preferences and occasional rewards for contributors. Over time, free-riding tends to erode cooperation unless participants use costly punishment and reward to stabilize behavior. Punishments (cost 4 to sender, −7 to target) and rewards (cost 4 to sender, +5 to target) are used sparingly in real data; rewards are sometimes used to encourage high contributors, punishments to deter persistent low contributors. Anonymity of who punished/rewarded can lead to noisy enforcement, and players typically moderate their use of sanctions because they are costly.

Players react to the first round's visible summaries (net payoff and amounts used/received for sanctions, without identities). Those who were lightly punished raise contributions a bit; others maintain or slightly reduce. A couple of players continue to reward consistent high contributors.

With some early punishments having had effect, a few low contributors increase slightly. Others start trimming contributions modestly as they notice the public return is low. Rewards remain targeted at consistently high contributors; punishments still scarce and focused.

Late-game decay is visible: many reduce to small contributions or zero. A core of cooperative types (high patience/social preference) remains willing to contribute moderately. A few final punitive gestures target repeat zero contributors; isolated rewards persist as private encouragements.

Most players have settled on low contributions; a small set of steady cooperators still contributes materially. With sanctions costly and identification impossible, players reserve further punishments and mostly stop sanctioning; a couple of encouraging rewards remain.

By this late stage contributions are low on average; many players conserve private holdings. A few loyal cooperators continue to contribute at nontrivial levels; a couple final small rewards and a scarce punishment close the sequence.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 15 rounds, and a pot multiplier (1.9) that is smaller than the group size. Individually, defecting (keeping your 20) strictly dominates contributing (giving the 20) in every round: a unilateral defector gains 16.2 coins more than a unilateral contributor, given others' choices. That makes full defection the game-theoretic equilibrium.

Despite that, real groups often show initial cooperation followed by decline: some players are conditional cooperators or motivated by social preferences (they contribute if others do), while others are opportunistic free-riders who quickly exploit contributors. Messaging can encourage coordination, but promises are not contractible and the finite horizon creates an endgame effect — cooperation tends to unravel as the final rounds approach.

Given the player personas, I expect a modest level of early cooperation from those with stronger mental-accounting or pro-social tendencies (GORILLA, DOG, CHICKEN), plus a few sporadic contributions from adventurous or risk-seeking types (PARROT, CHICK). Once contributors observe lower net payoffs than free-riders, many will stop contributing. There may be occasional attempts later to re-start cooperation after players send brief messages, but these will be fragile and short-lived. By the final rounds the dominant pattern is likely universal defection.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with a high multiplication factor (3.7) and optional costly punishment and reward. Each round players choose how many of their 20 private coins to contribute to a common pot; contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish (cost 4 to sender, −7 to target per unit) or to reward (cost 4 to sender, +5 to target per unit). Players see peers' contributions and the summary of how much others spent on punish/reward and how much they lost/gained (but not who targeted whom). Players do not know when the game ends.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- The marginal per‑player return from contributing is (3.7 / 15) ≈ 0.247 — less than 1, so individually the selfish best response is to contribute 0. But the group benefit of contributing is large: if everyone contributes more, everyone gains.
- Early rounds usually show heterogeneous contributions: some cooperative types contribute substantially, some freeriders contribute little. Because punishment and reward are costly, they are used sparingly but strategically: rewards tend to be used to reinforce high contributors; punishments target persistent low contributors.
- Over repeated rounds with visible contributions and occasional sanctioning, contributions often stabilize at a moderate-to-high level (cooperators sustain each other, punishers discipline persistent free-riders). Because players do not know the number of rounds, endgame free-riding is less prominent than if the end were known.
- Given the player priors, expect a core of consistent contributors (SLOTH, DOG, CROCODILE, PINGUIN, MONKEY, etc.), a few cautious or low contributors (CHICKEN, CHICK, COW, PARROT), and sparse use of sanctions (a few single-unit punishments and rewards each round).

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with costly punishment. Each round, players choose either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so cooperation benefits the group but creates a temptation to free-ride (keep 20 and still get a share of others' multiplied contributions). Punishment is available after seeing contributions; it is costly to the punisher (4 coins per punishment unit) and costly to the target (8 coins per unit). Because the game lasts a fixed number of rounds, players balance immediate payoff incentives (free-ride) against social incentives (maintain contributions to raise group payoff) and the deterrent effect of punishment.

Likely unfolding and uncertainties:
- Early rounds often start with substantial cooperation: many players contribute to signal pro-social intent.
- Free-riding pays off in the short run if many others contribute; this often prompts some players to punish defectors. Punishment tends to be sparse (a few players, small units), because punishment is itself costly.
- Punishment can deter repeat defection — those who are punished often switch to contributing in subsequent rounds. But some players (or in late rounds, many players) will sometimes defect again to exploit cooperators.
- Over time, cooperation often drifts downward if punishment is insufficient or too costly relative to the gains from defection. In the final rounds (especially the last round), incentive to free-ride rises because there is no future benefit from building reputation.
- Chat messages are usually few and used for coordination, appeals, or short shaming. Players occasionally threaten or promise to punish.

These tendencies are shaped by the provided personas: several players are patient and likely to cooperate (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, RABBIT, CHICK, DUCK), others are more variable or inclined to defect occasionally (SLOTH, DOG, OWL, PARROT). Expect a mix of cooperation and occasional defection, with punishment used selectively to deter repeated free-riding.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits 15 anonymous players against the tension between private incentive (keep your 20 coins) and the social return from contributing (the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and shared). Because the multiplier per person (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247) is less than 1, each coin contributed lowers a player's private return but may raise group payoff — so unilateral incentives favor free-riding. However, repeated interaction, visible round summaries, and a post-contribution reward stage (costly-to-giver, beneficial-to-recipient) create room for conditional cooperation: some players start by contributing moderately to signal cooperativeness; others free-ride or contribute little. Sparse positive rewards are used to reinforce high contributors; these rewards are costly (4 coins per unit) so they are applied selectively and usually only a small number of units are given. Over time I expect an initial moderately high average contribution that drifts downward as free-riding appears, with a small core of consistently cooperative players (those with higher patience/mental-accounting cues) continuing to contribute and trading a few reciprocal rewards with each other. Because players don't know the total number of rounds, end-game collapse is muted but still emerges slowly; reward use remains sporadic throughout.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a group benefit: any coins contributed to the public pot are multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally among all 15 players. The social-optimal choice is to contribute everything, but the Nash incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because each contributed coin returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.246 coin to its contributor, less than the 1 coin private cost. The punishment and reward stage lets players affect others after contributions are revealed: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and reduces the target by 7 coins; each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the target +5 coins. Because punishments and rewards are costly, players typically use them sparingly — rewards to encourage cooperators, punishments to deter persistent low contributors. Uncertainty about how many rounds will be played tends to keep behaviour somewhat cautious: some players steadily cooperate because of pro-social priors (patience, risk-taking or strong mental accounting), while others experiment with low contributions. Expect initial moderate contributions, a few sparse rewards for high contributors, and occasional targeted punishments aimed at very low contributors. Over repeated rounds contributions often drift down unless rewards or targeted punishments sustain cooperation.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a social return from contributing to a shared pot (each coin you contribute is multiplied by 3.7 and then split equally among all 15 players). The private marginal return on one coin is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 — well below 1 — so the one-shot selfish incentive is to contribute 0. However, because many players have priors or tendencies toward patience, risk-taking, or mental-accounting (see personas), we expect a mix of conditional cooperation and free-riding. Early rounds often see moderate contributions from patient/other-regarding players and low contributions from more selfish or impatient players. Over repeated interactions people commonly condition on observed contributions: conditional cooperators reduce giving if others give less. Rewarding is available but costly (4 coins per reward unit) and yields a small prestige/gain to the target (5 coins per unit). Because rewards are costly and individual, they are used sparingly — typically to recognize above-average contributors rather than to punish. Uncertainty about the number of rounds encourages stable strategies (no last-round collapse), but the small per-coin social return and visible free-riders generally produce a slow downward drift in average contributions. Rewards will appear occasionally, targeted at consistent high contributors, and only a few players will pay the cost to encourage others.

game_explanation: This is a standard public-goods dilemma: each player can keep 20 coins privately or contribute that 20 to a group pot. Every contributed coin is multiplied by 1.9 and the total is split evenly among all 10 players. Individually, keeping your 20 always gives a higher personal payoff than contributing (because you keep 20 plus your share of the pot), but if many players contribute the group total is larger and everyone (including contributors) can be better off than if almost nobody contributes. Typical dynamics: some players signal or try to lead cooperation early, conditional cooperators respond to observed cooperation, and free riders exploit cooperators. Over repeated rounds (15 rounds) cooperation commonly declines as players learn who defects; strong “unconditional” cooperators (high mental-accounting / high patience) may keep contributing while others stop. Messaging can sustain cooperation if credible; otherwise people retaliate by withholding contributions. Because the game is finite and players know the horizon, some degree of endgame decline is likely; however empirical behavior often shows some cooperation even late in the game from a subset of players.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 anonymous players each round. Each player starts each round with 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. After payoffs are distributed, players may spend some of their round income to reward individuals: each reward unit costs 4 to the giver and gives 5 to the recipient. Players see others' contributions and reward totals (but not who gave which reward) at the end of the round, and they do not know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics:
- Conditional cooperation dominates early: many players with high patience and strong mental-accounting tendencies (PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, PINGUIN, CROCODILE, COW, CHICK) will tend to contribute relatively high amounts initially.
- Some high risk/gain-seeking or low-patience players (DUCK, DOG, SNAKE) will start lower or try to free-ride, especially if they expect others to contribute.
- Because rewards are costly, they will be used sparingly and mostly to acknowledge consistent cooperators (a few 1-unit gifts distributed across rounds). Rewards will typically favor high contributors (visible to all), but because givers are anonymous, rewards serve as a mild incentive for cooperation rather than precise enforcement.
- Over rounds, contributions often drift downward modestly due to temptation to free-ride and because the reward mechanism is costly and sparse. However, stable cooperators maintain relatively high contributions; a few conditional cooperators adjust up after seeing many others contribute.
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is less abrupt end-game collapse; changes are gradual.

game_explanation:
This 15-round public goods game pits 10 players against a simple tension: contributing 20 coins helps the group (total contributions are multiplied by 1.9 then split equally), but any individual who keeps their 20 privately benefits more if others contribute. Early rounds often show some cooperation (people willing to try contributing), then contributions usually drift downward as free-riding is rewarded. Messages can nudge coordination (promises, conditional offers, complaints), but they are cheap talk and not fully reliable. Players with high "mental accounting" or high patience (PARROT, CHICKEN, DOG, RABBIT, MOOSE) are more likely to contribute early and sometimes sustain cooperation; high risk-seeking or low-accounting players (GORILLA, OWL, SLOTH) are more likely to free-ride. Expect an initial moderate contribution level, gradual decay toward many zeros in the middle rounds, some local recoveries if a few pro-social players try to restart cooperation, and near-zero contributions in the final rounds as endgame incentives favor free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-player repeated public-goods game with an endowment of 20 each round. The public good multiplier (3.7) and equal split mean each coin contributed benefits the group more than zero, but each individual's private marginal return on their own coin (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247) is less than 1, so the selfish incentive is to under-contribute. In practice, we expect a mix: some players with cooperative priors (high patience, high mental accounting) will contribute relatively high amounts; risk-seeking or low-patience players will contribute less. Because punishments are costly to both giver and receiver and rewards are also costly but increase recipients' payoffs, punishments will be used sparingly (mostly targeted at repeated low contributors) and rewards will be used to encourage consistent cooperators. Over rounds contributions slowly move toward a stable moderate-to-high level as a few low contributors are signalled (by small punishments) and high contributors are selectively rewarded. Punishment and reward are anonymous (players cannot see who acted), so feedback is noisy and responses are gradual.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and must either contribute all 20 to the shared pot or keep them. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally across all 10 players. Contributing helps the group (it raises the per-player share) but is individually costly relative to defecting in the same round: a contributor receives the equal share only, whereas a non-contributor gets that share plus the 20 they kept. Because players decide simultaneously and only see round summaries (payoffs) afterward, we expect an initial optimistic phase with several contributions (players hoping others will contribute or following pledges), then learning from payoff summaries will tempt free-riding. When defecting yields consistently higher individual payoffs, many contributors will stop. A few patient or „principled“ players may keep contributing even as returns shrink; toward the middle and end rounds cooperation typically collapses, with occasional solitary contributions. Messages are allowed but tend to be sparse and used for promises, coordination attempts, or short complaints. Final-round behavior may include a small gesture or a final defection; because there's no future after round 15, many will defect, though a couple may contribute as a closing signal.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits short-term incentives to free-ride (keep 20 while still getting a share of the pot) against social enforcement via punishment (costly to the punisher but expensive for the punished). Expect an initial mix of contributions and a few free-riders. Cooperative-minded players (high patience / mental accounting) will tend to contribute and occasionally use small punishments to enforce norms. Risk- or short-term–oriented players may try defecting sporadically for immediate gain; if punished, many will switch to contributing to avoid repeated losses. Because punishment is costly, players punish sparingly (one or two units) and usually only to signal displeasure or deter repeat defections. Towards the end, a few players may test the norm (final-round defections are common), prompting last-round punishments or resigned acceptance. Chat will be sparse and targeted (requests for cooperation, reproaches, brief promises to change).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 players, 20-coin endowments each round, a group multiplier of 3.7, and a stage after redistribution where players can spend costly reward units (cost 4 to giver, +5 to recipient). The private marginal return to a 1-coin contribution is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 — so contributing is costly for an individual unless players have social preferences or expect reciprocal rewards. Because players do not know the total number of rounds and cannot see others' choices before deciding each round, behavior will be driven by (a) initial social preferences and priors about others, (b) conditional cooperation (matching others’ observed contributions in later rounds), and (c) the incentive effect of rewards: rewards are costly but can sustain cooperation if targeted at contributors.

Uncertainties that shape dynamics here:
- Who will contribute initially (some players are more cooperative by prior) and whether others reciprocate.
- How frequently players will use the costly reward stage to reinforce cooperation — in real games rewards are used sparingly, so expect only a few targeted gifts per round.
- Whether free-riding participants reduce others’ contributions over time (a common decay), or whether targeted rewards stabilize cooperation among a core of contributors.

Using the provided personas as priors: several players (PARROT, CROCODILE, PINGUIN, RABBIT, DOG, MONKEY) have cues consistent with higher or more stable contributions; others (GORILLA, OWL, MOOSE) are likely to contribute very little. We expect an initial moderate average contribution (not full cooperation) and a gradual decline across rounds as conditional cooperators respond to observed free-riding, with occasional targeted rewards aimed at top contributors to sustain a cooperative core. Rewards will be sparse (1–2 units) and usually directed at those who contributed noticeably above the group mean.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private incentives (each contributed coin returns less than its cost to the contributor) against collective incentives (the group benefit rises with total contributions). With 15 players and a multiplication factor of 3.7, each contributed coin returns only about 0.247 to each person, so the individual payoff-maximizing action is to contribute 0. Still, many players have priors or social preferences that favor cooperation; others are more self-interested. Because players see only round summaries (contributions and aggregate punishment/reward flows) after each round and cannot identify who acted, behavior will likely show conditional cooperation: higher contributors tend to sustain or slightly reduce contributions, low contributors may be nudged upward if punished or rewarded, and overall contributions typically drift downward over time (a common empirical pattern). Punishment and reward are costly, so they occur sparsely: rewards typically praise high contributors, punishments target persistent low contributors. Expect early rounds to establish norms (some generous contributors rewarded), mid-game to show stabilization with occasional sanctions, and later rounds to show mild decline.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with voluntary contributions (0–20 each round) and a costly positive reward instrument (cost 4 to giver, gives 5 to receiver per unit). The dominant tension each round is between private short-term gain from free-riding (keep your 20) and collective gain from contributing (raise the shared pot, multiplied by 3.7 and split equally). Because players do not see others' choices before deciding and do not know how many rounds remain, early rounds typically show moderate cooperation as players probe others; conditional cooperators will reduce contributions if they see low group contributions later, while stable cooperators continue contributing. The reward stage lets players selectively reinforce cooperation (or reciprocate), but it is costly so rewards tend to be sparse and usually directed to clearly cooperative peers.

Uncertainties that shape behavior here:
- Social expectations: without prior communication, some players will hedge (mid-level contributions), while those with pro-social priors (high patience, strong mental accounting) tend to contribute more.
- Reward effectiveness: because rewarding is costly, players reward infrequently; reward decisions are therefore informative signals about who is seen as reliably cooperative.
- End-game incentives: if players suspect the game might end soon, contributions tend to decline toward later rounds; because players do not know the horizon, that decline is gradual and noisy rather than abrupt.

Given the provided personas, I predict a mixed group: several reliably cooperative players (DOG, CROCODILE, OWL, RABBIT, MONKEY, COW, PARROT) will contribute at higher levels most rounds; a small subset (GORILLA, CHICKEN, SNAKE, DUCK) will contribute less and drift toward free-riding; rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce high contributors and occasionally reciprocate.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with optional costly punishment and reward after contributions are revealed. Each round every player has 20 coins and decides how many to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. Contributing helps the group but is individually costly; withholding (free-riding) increases private payoff if others contribute. Punishment (costly to send, more costly to target) can deter free-riding; reward (also costly to send) can reinforce cooperation. Players do not see others' choices before they decide, and they cannot identify who targeted them when punishments or rewards arrive, but they do see how much each peer used to punish or reward and how much they themselves were affected.

Likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds: heterogeneous behavior. Some participants with stronger mental-accounting and patience (e.g., RABBIT, PINGUIN, MOOSE, CHICKEN) tend to contribute more; impulsive or risk-taking types (PARROT, DOG, CROCODILE, MONKEY, DOG especially risk-takers) are more likely to contribute little. Because players do not know the number of rounds, there's an incentive to cooperate moderately to build reciprocal norms, but uncertainty about others' tendencies creates fluctuation.
- Punishment and reward will be used sparingly. Real players typically use 0–2 units on a small number of peers to signal disapproval of low contributions or to reward high contributions. Punishment will often target clear low contributors; rewards will go to consistent high contributors. Because targets are anonymous, punishers risk imposing cost without clear personal reciprocation.
- Over rounds: limited, targeted sanctions combined with visible evidence of peers' sanctioning activity tend to raise average contributions moderately. Some low contributors adapt upward; some persistently low contributors attract occasional sanctions but are not heavily targeted every round. Rewards reinforce top cooperators, but are rarer than punishments in many datasets. Overall we expect an upward trend in average contributions across rounds with sparse sanctioning.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-player repeated public-goods game with a low marginal per-capita return (MPCR = 1.9/10 = 0.19). Contributing 20 to the pot returns only 3.8 to each player when a single person contributes; when many contribute each contributor still receives less than 20 back. That creates a strong individual incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) because any contributor loses compared to a non-contributor given the other players' contributions. However, some players have priors that make them more likely to try cooperating early (high mental-accounting, patience, or prosocial tendencies), and short messages can be used to signal intentions. In practice, we should expect:

- A few players will try contributing early to signal cooperation or test others.
- Free-riding will be rewarded in the immediate payoff summaries, so contributors will typically be punished by lower round payoffs.
- After observing those payoffs, most players will move toward free-riding quickly; attempts at re-coordination may appear but rarely sustain full cooperation.
- Chat will be sparse and mostly used to signal intentions or complain after seeing payoffs.
- By late rounds, contributions will mostly vanish (endgame free-riding).

This transcript predicts a plausible unfolding consistent with those incentives and the given player personas: some early contributions, then declining cooperation, occasional single-player trials or short coordination attempts, and mostly zeros by the end.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public-goods game with 16 players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses a contribution (0–20) to a shared pot that is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players may spend costly reward units (cost 4 to the giver per unit, 2 added to the receiver per unit). Players also can chat at any time. The main dynamics to expect:

- Early rounds: many players start somewhat optimistic and contribute moderately to highly (hoping for mutual cooperation). A handful of more self-oriented or risk-seeking players will contribute little or nothing.
- Rewarding is costly, so it will be used sparingly. When used, it typically goes from cooperators to other cooperators (to reinforce contribution) rather than to defectors. A few “strong cooperators” will pay to reward others early on to signal conditional cooperation.
- Mid game: free-riding tends to spread — those who contribute a lot in the first rounds may be exploited if rewards are too rare or too costly, and average contributions decline. If a small core of players keep rewarding cooperators, their behavior can sustain higher contributions among friends but overall contributions will likely fall.
- Late rounds: toward the end (backward induction), some players reduce contributions further. However, some high-patience or high-norm players (e.g., CHICKEN, PARROT, OWL) often keep contributing more reliably, and peers may reward them occasionally. Conversations tend to be sparse, targeted (requests, thanks), and reward actions are selective and infrequent.

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game has a clear tension: each coin contributed loses the contributor more than it returns to them personally (private return ≈ 0.281 per coin), so the short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). The group optimum is everyone contributing their full endowment. Two second-stage institutions change incentives: punishment (cheap to deliver from a social-impact standpoint: it costs the sender 4 coins and removes 15 from the target) and reward (costs 4, gives 2 to the target). Because punishment is severe to the target, a small number of punishments can strongly deter persistent free-riding. Rewards are less powerful but signal appreciation and foster cooperation.

Given the player priors, I expect initially many players with high patience and strong mental-accounting cues (OWL, DUCK, DOG, MOOSE, PARROT, etc.) to contribute moderately-high amounts. Some risk-seeking or gain-seeking players (SNAKE, RABBIT, COW, RABBIT) are more likely to test or free-ride early. Early, sparse punishment will be used by a few high-contributing, enforcement-oriented players (e.g., OWL, DUCK, DOG) to discipline clear free-riders (especially low contributors like SNAKE). Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce high contributors.

Over rounds, contributions will settle into a moderate cooperative norm for a while (sustained by occasional punishment and reinforcement), then drift down as end-game effects and retaliatory dynamics set in: once many players reduce contributions, punishers receive diminishing marginal benefit from continuing to punish and will cut back their enforcement. In the final rounds (last 2–3), we should see a pronounced decline (typical end-game unraveling) toward zero contributions; punishment and reward activity will also wane.

Below is a full round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 17. Contributions are shown in the exact avatar order given; punishment/reward uses tuples (source_avatar, target_avatar, unit) with negative unit = punishment unit and positive unit = reward unit. Only non-zero edges are listed. Chat is sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players who each get 20 coins per round and may contribute some portion (0–20) to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so there is a strong social return to contributions but a private incentive to free-ride. Because players see only past outcomes (not choices before deciding) and the game runs for 17 rounds, we expect conditional cooperation: many players who value fairness or have high patience/mental accounting will start by contributing moderately-to-high, while risk-seeking or low-mental-accounting players will contribute less. Over rounds contributions tend to drift downward as free-riding appears, unless positive rewards are used to sustain cooperation. Rewards cost 4 coins to the sender and give 2 coins to the recipient, so they are used sparingly — typically targeted to reward above-average contributors to encourage continued cooperation. Chat messages will be relatively rare and purposeful (e.g., promises, thanks, appeals). Because players can identify who rewarded them, targeted rewards early on will try to bolster cooperators; later, rewards may dwindle as the benefits of sustaining cooperation decline toward the end of the finite horizon.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds, and a high public multiplier (4.5). Individually, each coin contributed returns only about 0.281 to the contributor, so the dominant short-term incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, because the game is repeated and players can punish (costly to punisher, very costly to target) and reward (costly to source, small benefit to target), social enforcement and conditional cooperation can sustain higher contributions early and mid-game. Expect:

- Early rounds: a mix of cooperative and selfish behavior. Some players (high patience and high mental-accounting players like PARROT, RABBIT, MONKEY, PINGUIN, FROG, COW) will contribute strongly as conditional cooperators or inherently pro-social. Others (SLOTH, CHICK, OWL, DOG) will likely contribute less.
- Punishment and rewards will be used sparingly. Punishment is an effective deterrent because it heavily penalizes targets; expect a few targeted punishments aimed at repeat low contributors, especially early-to-mid game, to enforce cooperation.
- Rewards will be rare and symbolic (few units), used to recognize high contributors.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators will respond to others' contributions; if low contributions spread, punishments may increase briefly to re-establish norms.
- Endgame: as the horizon nears (last few rounds), forward-looking incentives to maintain cooperation decline; contributions are likely to fall and punishments be used less often (punishing in the very last round is less common because it cannot change future behavior).

I will simulate a plausible unfolding: initial moderate-to-high contributions with sparse punishment of clear free-riders (notably SLOTH), some small reward gestures, a few enforcement episodes mid-game, and a gradual decline in contributions toward the end.

This is a 17-round public goods game with an opportunity to reward other players after payoff redistribution. The social incentives are clear: any coin contributed is multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally among all 16 players, so the private marginal return to a contributor is well below 1 (about 0.28125 per coin), creating a temptation to free-ride each round. At the same time, the group payoff is higher when many contribute, and the reward stage lets players transfer costly signals of appreciation (each reward unit costs 4 to the sender and grants 2 to the recipient). Because players can see who rewarded them, reciprocity and targeted reward strategies can support cooperation, but sending rewards is itself expensive and therefore used sparingly.

Given the player personas (many with high patience and strong mental-accounting tendencies), expect moderate-to-high contributions early on from a core of cooperators (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, OWL, PINGUIN, FROG). A minority (e.g., CROCODILE, CHICKEN, MONKEY) are more likely to free-ride or experiment with low contributions. Early small rewards will be directed to recognized cooperators and will sometimes be reciprocated, sustaining cooperation for several rounds. However, because the game has a finite horizon and players know it ends at round 17, cooperation will tend to wane in the later rounds (end-game effect), with rewards tapering off and contributions decaying toward the final rounds. Chat is likely to be sparse but used occasionally to praise, request cooperation, or call out free-riding.

game_explanation:
This public goods game combines the standard social dilemma (individual incentive to free-ride versus group benefit from contributing) with costly punishment and modest rewards. Because the marginal per-capita return (4.5/16 ≈ 0.281) is below 1, each coin contributed is privately costly but collectively valuable if many contribute. Expect these broad dynamics:

- Early rounds: many players test the waters. Some reliably pro-social types (e.g., GORILLA, OWL, RABBIT, MOOSE) will contribute relatively high amounts; more selfish or risk-seeking types (e.g., CROCODILE, DUCK, COW) will contribute little. Sparse use of punishment by a few enforcement-minded players will target clear low contributors; rewards will be used even more sparingly to encourage cooperators.
- Middle rounds: if punishment/reward successfully raises average contributions, cooperation can rise or stabilize. If punishers tire or costs of enforcement mount, contributions tend to drift downward.
- Late rounds: a classic end-game decline (backward induction, or 'why bother') is likely — overall contributions fall. Punishment becomes less frequent because it is costly and less effective late in the game; rewards may be used by a few to signal approval.
- Uncertainty and identity effects: players know punishers/rewarders are identified, so targeted players may temporarily increase contributions after being punished or may hold grudges; but retaliatory punishment is unlikely to be frequent because punishment is expensive to the punisher and punishing punishers often yields little group benefit.

I will simulate a plausible 17-round trajectory keeping chat sparse, punishments/rewards rare, and following the given avatar order.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 16 players against a standard social dilemma: each round everyone gets 20 coins and privately decides how many to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally, so each coin given returns only 0.28125 to its giver — contributing is socially beneficial but individually costly. Because the game lasts 17 rounds and contributions are revealed after choices, we should expect classic dynamics: an initial phase with moderate cooperation as players test one another, a middle phase where free-riding chips away at contributions unless social incentives (messages, rewards) sustain cooperation, and a late-stage endgame decline as forward-looking players reduce giving when future enforcement loses power.

Rewards here are costly: each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives the receiver 2 coins (net -2 coins to group per unit). Rewards therefore serve as social signaling (thank-you to cooperators or scarce investments to try to sustain cooperation) rather than efficient growth of group payoff. Because players see who rewarded them, rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperative contributors or to signal disapproval indirectly by selectively rewarding cooperators while ignoring free-riders.

Given the player personas, expect a mixed population: several reliably cooperative, patient types (GORILLA, PARROT, RABBIT, CHICK, maybe FROG/PINGUIN) who start relatively high; some variable contributors (DUCK, OWL, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW); and a few likely low contributors (DOG, SLOTH, MONKEY) who free-ride early and throughout. Early rounds will feature a few short messages proposing cooperation and a handful of small positive rewards to high contributors. Over time, contributions will trend downward with occasional upticks when small clusters use rewards or messages to prop cooperation. In the final rounds contributions collapse as the shadow of future punishment fades; rewards become very sparse.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits 16 identifiable players against the classic tension: each coin kept privately yields 1 to the individual, but every coin contributed increases the group pot by 4.5, which is split equally across all 16 players (so each contributed coin returns only 0.28125 to the contributor). That creates a strong individual incentive to free-ride while the group optimum is to contribute the whole endowment. Because contributions are revealed each round and a small, costly reward mechanism is available (cost 4 to sender, gives 2 to recipient), players can signal approval for cooperators — but rewards are net-lossful for the group and expensive for senders, so they are used sparingly and mainly as social incentives.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players who each get 20 coins per round. The social multiplier (4.5) yields a private marginal return of 4.5/16 ≈ 0.281 per coin contributed: by pure self-interest players should contribute 0 each round. Social preferences, patience, and the availability of costly punishment/reward can sustain cooperation: a few consistent high contributors plus a small number of punishers or rewarders can raise average contributions. Key uncertainties: players don't observe others' choices before acting in the round, so early rounds reveal signals about types; punishments are harsh on targets (−15 per punishment unit) but costly to the punisher (−4 each), so punishers tend to use very small numbers of units and only when they care about enforcement or fairness. Rewards are also costly (cost 4 to giver, +2 to receiver) and thus are used sparingly to signal approval. Messages can help coordinate norms or signal intent (e.g., "I'll punish persistent free-riders"), which can raise contributions later. Expect moderate-to-high contributions from cooperative personas (GORILLA, DUCK, OWL, MONKEY, FROG, DOG, CROCODILE), some persistent low contributions from a few (notably RABBIT and sometimes PARROT), sparse but targeted punishments early-to-mid game aimed at repeat low contributors, and occasional small rewards to sustain high contributors. Towards the very end (round 17) contributions often drift downward because future enforcement is limited, though players with strong intrinsic fairness may keep contributing.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 16 players, 17 known rounds, and a costly reward stage. Each round each player chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a public pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split evenly among all players. Because the multiplier per person is (4.5/16) ≈ 0.28125 per coin contributed, contributors sacrifice some private return to benefit the group. With a finite, known horizon (17 rounds), two key forces shape behavior:

- Conditional cooperation and reciprocity: many players will be inclined to contribute if they expect others to do so and to reward those who contribute. Rewards are costly (4 coins per reward unit) but signal appreciation and can sustain cooperation early and mid-game.
- Free-riding and endgame logic: selfish incentives favor contributing 0. Over time, especially as the end approaches, contributions typically decline as the incentive to build reputations vanishes. Rewarding tends to be sparse because of its direct cost; it's used selectively to reinforce cooperation or reciprocate kindness.

Uncertainties and heterogeneity:
- Players differ in patience, risk preferences, and mental-accounting tendencies. Some (high patience/reciprocal types) will contribute fairly consistently; others (low patience or high loss aversion) will contribute less.
- Because nobody sees contributions before choosing each round, expectations are formed from past rounds and sparse chat. Rewards reveal who valued whom, and that social signal can sustain cooperation for many rounds but usually not all the way to the last round.

Predicted unfolding:
- Early rounds: moderate-to-high average contributions as reciprocal players test cooperation and reward selectively.
- Middle rounds: contributions hover or slowly decline; rewards focus on consistently high contributors.
- Late rounds (final 3–4 rounds): clear decline in contributions toward free-riding; reward activity becomes very sparse and strategic reciprocity fades. By round 17 contributions fall sharply.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game where individual incentives (keep money) conflict with group efficiency (full contribution). Each coin invested returns 0.28125 to each player, so private return < 1 and social return > 1: players who value group payoffs, patience, or reputation will tend to contribute more; short-term or gain-seeking players will try to free-ride. Because there is a punishment option that is costly to the punisher (4 coins per punishment unit) but very costly to the punished (15 coins per unit), punishment will be used sparingly but can strongly discipline persistent low contributors. Rewards are expensive to the giver and modest to the receiver (cost 4, benefit 2) and will be used occasionally to encourage high contributors.

I expect:
- Early rounds: many players test cooperation; a few low contributions trigger selective punishments from those who value cooperation (high patience / high mental-accounting players).
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators sustain moderate-to-high contributions; punishment frequency falls as most raise contributions; occasional rewards reinforce cooperative behavior.
- Late rounds: an end-game tendency to reduce contributions appears (final two rounds especially), with some last-minute punishments but overall fewer enforcement actions because future deterrence is limited.

Gameplay will therefore show moderate initial contributions, targeted punishment early to enforce norms, a somewhat stable cooperative middle, and a decline at the end.

This is a 17-round public goods game with a costly punishment and reward stage. Individually, contributing is costly because each coin returns only ~0.28 to the contributor (4.5 is split across 16 players), so without social enforcement many people will under-contribute. The presence of punishment (very costly to punished players: -15 per punishment unit) and reward (cheap to benefit target: +2 per unit) creates strong incentives for norm enforcement: some players will invest small amounts to punish persistent low contributors and reward high contributors, which tends to raise average contributions after early rounds. Players with strong mental-accounting or high patience cues (e.g., DUCK, RABBIT, OWL, PARROT, CHICK, COW, MONKEY) are more likely to contribute high amounts early and consistently; risk/taste-for-lottery cues make some players (e.g., GORILLA, PINGUIN, CROCODILE) likely to free-ride or contribute little. Punishment/reward tends to be sparse and targeted — a few 1-unit punishments or rewards per round from players who kept some private coins after contributing. Over rounds we expect cooperation to strengthen after early punishment of clear free-riders, stabilize mid-game, and then erode toward the last few rounds as the endgame approaches.

game_explanation: This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 identifiable players. Players begin each round with 20 coins, decide a contribution (0–20) without knowing others' choices, then the shared pot is multiplied and redistributed, and finally players may spend private coins to reward others (each reward unit costs 4 to sender, gives 2 to target). Players know the game length, so there is an incentive for early cooperation but an end-game tendency to free-ride as the horizon approaches. Rewarding is costly, so it will be used sparingly — mainly to recognize consistently high contributors and to try to sustain cooperation early on. Expect initial moderate-to-high contributions by many players who have high patience and strong mental accounting (e.g., SLOTH, RABBIT, COW, OWL, PARROT), some conditional cooperators who follow others (GORILLA, DUCK, SNAKE), and a few low contributors (CROCODILE, some risk-preferring players) from the start. Over rounds, if some players reduce contributions, others will mirror that decline. Because rewards only add positively, they function as praise/encouragement rather than direct punishment — so they can slow but not always stop collapse. Near the final rounds contributions typically fall close to zero as the shadow of the future shrinks.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 16 players, small private endowments each round (20 coins) and a fairly large multiplier on the public pot (4.5) split equally. The main incentives:
- Contributing increases the shared pot and benefits everyone, so cooperative contributions (mid-to-high values) raise everyone's payoffs.
- Free-riding (contributing little or nothing) benefits the individual directly that round while still allowing them to share the public return, so there is a strong short-run incentive to defect.
- Punishment is highly asymmetric: each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins but removes 15 coins from the target. Rewards cost 4 to the sender and give only +2 to the target. Because punishment is powerful but costly to the punisher, it is used sparingly and typically directed at persistent free-riders.
- Players know the game has 17 rounds. That creates end-game incentives: cooperation tends to decay toward the final rounds unless enforcement (punishment) stabilizes contributions.
- Uncertainty: players don’t see others’ current-round choices before deciding, only past patterns and any messages. That encourages using prior beliefs and early signals (messages, prior actions, and visible punishments) to shape behavior.

Likely overall dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players start fairly cooperative (mid to high contributions), with a few low contributors testing the waters.
- A small number of punishments directed at persistent low contributors appears early after one or two low contributions; punishers try to deter free-riding.
- Rewards are rare and modest (1 unit) — mostly used to recognize consistent high contributors.
- Mid-game: contributions rise somewhat toward mutual cooperation as punishment and social norms discourage free-riding.
- Late-game (last 2–4 rounds): contributions drift down as the finite horizon becomes salient; punishments drop off because punishment costs private coins and yields little future deterrence late in the game.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with contribution, then costly punishment and reward. Expect initial cooperation from players with high patience and strong mental-accounting cues (they value the group pot and fairness), and low contributions from players with low mental-accounting or high short-term preference. Punishment will be used sparingly to discipline persistent free-riders early/mid game — usually by contributors who keep a small buffer to pay for 1 punishment/reward unit per round. Rewards will be rarer than punishment but used occasionally to reinforce cooperation. Over time, because the game has a known finite horizon, contributions typically drift downward: cooperation is maintained midgame by selective punishment/reward, but toward the last few rounds many players reduce contributions (backward-induction/endgame effect), and costly sanctions are used less in the very final rounds.

Expect these uncertainties and incentives to shape behavior:
- Incentive to contribute: multiplied public pot (×4.5) split equally makes contributing profitable for the group but individually costly, so conditional cooperators will contribute more if others do.
- Incentive to punish: punishing low contributors can be costly but deters free-riding; we should see a handful of 1-unit punishments rather than mass reprisals.
- Rewarding: small, targeted rewards (1 unit) for high contributors will appear intermittently.
- Endgame: contributions fall in later rounds and sanctions decline because they are less profitable (or spiteful punishments appear sporadically).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After payoffs are redistributed, players may spend coins to give positive rewards to specific others (each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives the recipient +2 coins). Players see only their own round summary (their net payoff, what they spent on rewards, and what they received) and can send chat messages.

Typical incentives and likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players who are inclined toward cooperation (high patience, strong mental accounting) will contribute moderately-high amounts expecting reciprocation. A few low-cooperation types (low patience / low pro-social cues) may free-ride early.
- Mid game: conditional cooperators will reduce contributions in response to persistent free-riding; contributions will trend downward unless rewards successfully sustain cooperation. Rewards are costly to give, so expect only targeted, small positive rewards (1–3 units) to encourage high contributors or thank cooperators.
- Endgame: because the horizon is known (17 rounds), standard game-theoretic and empirical patterns predict a decline in contributions as the game approaches the final rounds. Even so, a subset of players with strong cooperative priors may keep contributing somewhat. Rewards may be used sparingly near the end either to sustain cooperation or as last-round "gratitude" tokens.
- Uncertainties: exact magnitudes and timing of contribution declines depend on how many people notice free riders and whether a few players sustain cooperation with rewards. Rewards will be sparse and targeted rather than broad.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round everyone privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally among the 16 players, so each contributed coin returns only 0.28125 to the contributor — individually costly but socially beneficial if many contribute. After payoffs are redistributed, players can spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Players also may send chat messages at any time.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Initial rounds often show some optimistic or conditional cooperation (some high contributors testing others).
- Because the marginal private return per contributed coin is low (<1), purely self-interested players will free-ride, causing contributions to drift down over time unless social enforcement (here, rewards) sustains cooperation.
- Rewards will be used sparingly: to reinforce consistent high contributors (positive reciprocity) rather than to punish. Because rewards are costly to the sender, they will usually be small (1–2 units) and targeted to a few cooperators.
- Messages are sparse but can be used to appeal to norms, thank rewarders, or signal commitments; these can briefly stabilize contributions when effective.
- Expect partial collapse of contributions late in the game, with a small subset of consistently cooperative players continuing to contribute at higher levels and occasionally exchanging mutual rewards.

game_explanation: This 17-round public goods game will start with relatively high cooperation from patient, pro-social players and some low contributions from a few selfish or impulsive players. Early rounds typically show a mix of high contributions from those who value the group multiplier (SLOTH, CHICKEN, PARROT, COW, PINGUIN, CHICK) and low, opportunistic contributions from a few (CROCODILE, MONKEY, SNAKE, RABBIT). Because punishment and reward are available, cooperative players will sometimes reward steady contributors and occasionally punish persistent low contributors; punishment is costly to the sender and harsh for the target, so it is used sparingly. Over the middle rounds cooperation tends to stabilize as punishments discipline free-riding and small rewards reinforce contributors. As the known end of the game approaches (final rounds), forward-looking players reduce contributions (end-game decline), punishment becomes less frequent (less future to enforce), and overall contributions fall. Chat messages are sparse and mostly used to coordinate or scold a visible pattern of free-riding.

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game pits 16 identifiable players against the short-run temptation to free-ride and the longer-run incentives to sustain cooperation. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the pot is multiplied and shared, so group payoffs are maximized by high contributions but individuals can do better by contributing less while others contribute. Because players can punish (costly to the punisher, strongly costly to the target) and reward (costly to the giver, modest benefit to the recipient), we expect the following broad dynamics:

- Early rounds: uncertainty about others’ behavior leads to varied contributions (some conditional cooperators will start moderately high; some opportunistic players will experiment with low contributions).
- Detection and norm-setting: a few players with a preference for fairness or “mental accounting” (and those who plan to enforce norms) will punish clear low contributors. Sparse rewards will be used to encourage consistent cooperators.
- Stabilization: punishment (used sparingly) plus targeted rewards will raise average contributions toward a cooperative norm for many players, though occasional defections (mistakes or strategic free-riding) will be met with selective punishment.
- Endgame: because the game has a known finite horizon (17 rounds), some players with shorter time horizons or low patience may try one last defection near the end; others may sustain cooperation if social enforcement is perceived as effective.

Uncertainties: who becomes an early enforcer (some high mental-accounting players are more likely), and whether any players retaliate or engage in costly punishment cycles. Chat messages will be sparse and typically used to announce intentions, call out low contributors, or apologize after being punished.

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game combines simultaneous contribution decisions with a costly, positive-only reward stage and optional chat. Typical dynamics: many players start by contributing moderately to highly (hoping for mutual benefit), while a few free-ride early. Because the group return is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, high contributions benefit everyone, creating an incentive to contribute but also to free-ride. Reward opportunities allow players to privately reinforce cooperative behavior (or reward allies), which can sustain contributions early on. Over a long finite horizon (17 rounds), expect some decline in contributions as players anticipate endgame defection, with occasional pockets of maintained cooperation where rewards and social messages create reciprocity. Chat will be sparse and strategic (praise, requests, mild reproach). Rewards will be used sparingly because each reward unit is costly (4 coins) and players have limited leftover private coins after contributing.

Players with high “patience” and pro-social cues (e.g., DUCK, MONKEY, SNAKE) are likely to contribute more and receive rewards; players with low patience or low gain-seeking (e.g., RABBIT, CHICK) are likely to contribute little and receive few rewards. Some players with medium tendencies may try to sustain cooperation through occasional rewards. As rounds progress, contributions will trend downward; reward use and chat will decline as players conserve private coins or retaliate/stop rewarding perceived free-riders.
