game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a one-shot-style repeated public goods game with 19 players per round. Each round each player either leaves the 20-coin pot intact (contribute 20) or withdraws the 20 for private use (contribute 0). The pot of all contributions is multiplied by 11.5 and divided equally among all 19 players. Individually, withdrawing (defecting) strictly dominates contributing: a single player's contribution of 20 increases each player's share by 20*11.5/19 ≈ 12.11, so a contributor loses about 7.89 relative to withdrawing, holding others fixed. Thus individual incentives favor taking the private 20 every round.

However, group payoff is highest when many contribute. Players do not see contemporaneous choices, but they do see each round's summary showing everyone's net payoff, so they can infer others' behavior over time. They also don't know how many rounds remain, which prevents simple end-game defection coordination but supports gradual learning: if contributors see they earn less than withdrawers they are likely to switch to withdrawing in later rounds, producing a predictable drift toward full defection. A few players with strong “mental accounting” or pro-social priors may persist in contributing despite personal loss, producing occasional small cooperative pockets. Expect relatively high initial cooperation among some pro-social/stable types, followed by steady decline as the advantage of withdrawing is visible in payoffs; occasional isolated attempts at cooperation may reappear but typically yield lower personal payoffs, so full defection becomes the absorbing pattern.

predicted_rounds.explanation: At the start players have no round history, so initial choices reflect their priors. Those with higher mental-accounting and patience (e.g., GORILLA, OWL, CHICK, COW, WHALE, MONKEY, DUCK, DOG) are the likeliest initial contributors. Others (low patience or low mental-accounting) are likely to withdraw 20. Because contributions are costly to the individual, expect contributors to earn noticeably less than withdrawers in the summary. This contrast will prompt many to switch to withdrawing in subsequent rounds.

Players observe round 1 payoffs: contributors earned ~96.84 while withdrawers earned ~116.84. Many conditional cooperators will switch to withdrawing. A core of more persistent cooperators (high mental-accounting or strong pro-social priors) may continue contributing (e.g., GORILLA, OWL, CHICK, COW, WHALE). Expect a smaller group of contributors this round.

Seeing a clear per-round penalty for contributing, more players defect. Only the most persistent cooperators are likely to remain (Gorilla, Owl, COW). Expect contributions to shrink further to a small minority.

With contributors earning substantially less again, some persistent cooperators may quit. Now only a couple (OWL and COW) remain willing to bear the cost for the group's benefit. Expect further shrinkage.

One very persistent player (COW) continues to contribute despite repeated personal losses; the rest withdraw. Isolated contribution yields a small group return but is worse for the contributor personally. Expect 1 contributor this round.

Seeing that the lone contributor earned the least, most players (including the previous lone contributor) switch to withdrawing. Full defection is an expected absorbing outcome, so expect zero contributors this round.

Even after full defection, a few stubborn players may occasionally re-attempt cooperation. COW has shown persistent pro-social behavior before; expect a lone attempt to contribute again, though it will again be personally costly. Most players will continue to withdraw.
After another costly experiment, most players revert to withdrawing; the visibility of payoffs reinforces full defection. Expect zero contributors.

Occasional coordination between a couple of previously persistent cooperators (OWL and COW) could occur if they try to rebuild trust. Two contributors produce a slightly larger public return than a lone contributor, but contributors still earn less than withdrawers. Expect a small two-person contribution attempt.

By now the visible pattern strongly favors withdrawing; even those who occasionally tried to re-establish cooperation see that contributors consistently earn less. Expect return to full defection for the final observed round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public-goods game with 19 players and a very large multiplier (11.5) on the shared pot. Each round every player faces a clean binary choice: contribute the full 20 coins (leave nothing for private use) or withdraw them (contribute 0). Because the pot is multiplied and the return is split equally, everyone receives the same per-player share regardless of their own action; a defector who withdraws 20 always earns exactly 20 coins more than a cooperator that round. That creates a strong short-run incentive to withdraw (free-ride), but also a high group-level return if many contribute.

Given the player personas (many high-patience and many with strong mental-accounting/community priors), I expect an initially substantial but not unanimous level of contribution: some players will try cooperation to capture the high group return, while others will defect to exploit it. Because players see only each round’s net payoffs (their own and peers') and not explicit contribution labels, they can infer how many people likely contributed and update expectations. The defection advantage ( +20 to defectors) tends to produce a gradual erosion of contributions over repeated rounds — especially when players observe that noncontributors earn more than contributors. At the same time, a subset of “pro-social” or more risk-tolerant players (perceived from the personas) are likely to keep contributing across rounds, producing a slow decline to a low-but-nonzero cooperation level, with occasional small rebounds when inferred group returns look attractive again.

Uncertainties: without communication and without knowing the horizon, some players will experiment (switch) to see what peers do; some will stick to a cooperative identity despite monetary disadvantage; others will opportunistically defect whenever the inferred number of cooperators is high. I therefore predict a moderate initial contribution rate, followed by gradual decline and a small late rebound as a few players re-invest in cooperation after observing the payoff patterns.

predicted_rounds.explanation: In the first round many players start optimistic or normative about public goods (high patience, high loss-avoidance, and many with high mental-accounting scores). A cluster of players with strong communal priors (e.g., PARROT, OWL, CROCODILE, ELEPHANT, etc.) contribute; some others test the waters by withdrawing privately to exploit any contributors. Because no one has prior feedback yet, the pattern reflects initial dispositions: sizable cooperation but not unanimity.

Players see that noncontributors earned 20 more than contributors. Some conditional cooperators (who hoped others would contribute) switch to withdrawing to capture the private +20. A core of morally/strategically committed players remains contributing because they value the large group return, or because their priors favor continued cooperation. Expect a modest drop in contributors relative to Round 1.

Seeing that withdrawing gives a consistent +20 advantage and that the share declined when some left, more conditional contributors pull back. Those with the strongest communal priors or reputational consistency (internal to their own strategy) keep contributing. Expect a sharper drop as some early cooperators switch.

With a clearer pattern — defectors consistently out-earn cooperators by 20 — a few more contributors defect. Yet a stable minority still contributes (those with high mental-accounting and patience). Expect a small further decline in the contributor count.

Momentum toward defection continues as observations reinforce the private advantage of withdrawing. A small core of steadfast cooperators remains (often those with both high patience and strong communal priors), producing a lower-but-stable contribution baseline.

After several rounds of observing the payoff gap, many players settle on the private-withdrawal strategy; the small cooperative core holds its line (either out of principle or because those players anticipate occasional mutual gains). Expect the contributor count to stabilize at the same low level as Round 5.

Some contributors, seeing persistent exploitation and perhaps depleting any hope of broad cooperation, move to withdraw as well. The cooperative minority shrinks further but does not disappear entirely — a handful of players keep contributing, possibly motivated by intrinsic preferences or experiments to re-establish cooperation.
The pattern from Round 7 is reinforced; defecting dominates short-run payoffs and most players continue to withdraw. The small cooperative cluster persists at its minimal size.

Some players experiment when the inferred return looks attractive enough (or because they want to seed cooperation). Observing previous rounds, a couple of previously-withdrawing players (e.g., COW) try contributing again to see if a small coalition can lift group payoffs, producing a modest rebound in contributors.

Given the mixed signals through repeated summaries — persistent advantage to withdrawing but occasional small rebounds — a few more players return to contributing (either to test a higher-cooperation equilibrium or from intrinsic pro-social preferences). Expect a moderate uptick in contributors compared to the immediate low point, but still far from full cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a classic collective‑action problem with a strong individual incentive to withdraw the 20 coins for private use (defect) and a much larger group payoff if many people leave their 20 coins in the shared pot (cooperate). Because the pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally, everyone benefits far more when many contribute — but each player gains more by defecting given whatever others do. In a repeated, anonymous setting where players do not know the total number of rounds, two opposing forces shape behavior:

- Strategic incentive: withholding (defecting) strictly dominates contributing for an individual in a single round; everyone gets a larger private payoff by taking the 20 and still receiving the shared return from others’ contributions.
- Social/behavioral tendencies: some players (based on the personas) have stronger mental‑accounting or patience cues and are more likely to contribute initially (they treat the “shared pot” as meaningful or are more prosocial/forward‑looking). Others with low mental accounting or high preference for immediate private gains are likely to defect from the start.

Given these forces, I predict initial rounds will show a mix — a sizable minority of contributors (people with stronger mental‑accounting/tendency to cooperate), while others free‑ride. Seeing many free‑riders, conditional cooperators will gradually reduce contributions; cooperation will decay over rounds, with only a few committed contributors remaining after mid‑game and near‑universal defection by the end. Because players don’t know when the game ends, there’s less of a final‑round cooperative spur; slow attrition of contributors is most likely.

game_explanation:
This is a standard one-shot-style public goods game repeated over unknown rounds with 19 players. Each player chooses whether to withdraw the full 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave the full 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). Each contributed coin is multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally across all 19 players, so individual incentives push toward withdrawing (free-riding) while the group outcome is best when everyone contributes. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding and cannot see who punished them afterwards, uncertainty about others' strategies and the source of punishment matters. Punishment is costly to send (2 coins per unit) but can deter free-riding by imposing a 3-coin penalty per unit on targets.

Typical dynamics: initial rounds often show a mix of cooperation and withdrawal as players test each other. Cooperators tend to punish visible free-riders, but punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher. Over time, punishment can stabilize higher cooperation if enough cooperators are willing to pay the enforcement cost; otherwise cooperation drifts downward as some players exploit the public good. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, pure end-game collapse is less likely than in known-finite games, but gradual erosion can occur. I therefore predict moderate-to-high cooperation overall, occasional defectors, and a small number of targeted punishments each round directed at persistent free-riders.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated binary public-goods game with 19 players each round: each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot (contribution = 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribution = 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split evenly, so each round whoever contributes benefits the group but is exploited by defectors who keep an extra 20. Because everyone's summary payoff is shown to all players at the end of each round, players can infer who contributed (contributor payoffs will be 20 less than defectors', given the same group outcome). That transparency makes conditional strategies possible: many players will try cooperating early (uncertainty and some prosocial priors), but seeing defectors earn 20 more will tend to drive cooperation down over time. Some persistent cooperators (more patient or more “mental-accounting” / prosocial types) may continue to contribute despite exploitation, producing occasional small rebounds; however, without communication and without a known fixed horizon, the typical pattern is an early-to-mid-game decline in contributions that stabilizes at low or zero contribution by the later rounds. I therefore predict an initial moderate number of contributors, then a cascade of defections as players respond to higher private payoffs for defecting, ending in near-universal withholding by the final rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This public goods game gives each player two stark choices each round: keep all 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally, so cooperating increases everyone’s return but is individually costly. The punishment stage allows costly peer enforcement: every punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target. That makes punishment an effective deterrent but itself costly, so we expect punishments to be used sparingly and mainly by players trying to enforce cooperation.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically feature a mix of cooperators and a minority of free-riders. Conditional cooperators will be willing to contribute if enough peers do; opportunistic players may test free-riding.
- Because players cannot see choices before deciding and cannot identify who punished them, punishments signal social disapproval but are noisy. Punishment tends to be targeted and modest (1–2 units) and concentrated on repeat defectors.
- Over an unknown horizon, cooperation can stabilize if punishments are credible but not too costly. Expect a short adaptation period: several defectors punished early will often switch to contributing in later rounds; occasional experimental defections may still appear and be punished to maintain norms.
- Uncertainties: players don't know the total number of rounds (so end-game behavior is less predictable), punishers are anonymous (reducing reputational dynamics), and the 0/20 binary choice simplifies strategies (no partial contributions). Given these factors and the provided personas, I predict a majority of cooperative contributions most rounds, with a few rounds showing isolated defections followed by targeted punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with binary contributions: each player either leaves 20 coins in the public pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all active players. Because each extra contributor adds 230 total to the multiplied pot and that is split across the group, the marginal per-person return from one additional contribution is 230 / (number of players). With 19 players here, each contributed coin adds about 12.11 to every player's round payoff, while a single player who contributes forgoes 20 private coins. That makes contribution individually costly (private cost 20 > private return ≈12.11), so the one-shot incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). However, some players have priors (high mental-accounting endorsements, high patience) that make them more likely to keep contributing even when it lowers their own immediate payoff; others are more responsive to payoff feedback and will quickly stop contributing once they see cooperating yields lower net payoffs.

Because players cannot communicate and only see round payoff summaries (their own payoff and corresponding peers' payoffs), contributions are inferred from the pattern of payoffs (contributors will have the smaller payoff in a mixed round). Early rounds often start with a mix: some intrinsically cooperative types experiment or stick with contributing, while many defect. Over rounds we expect contributions to fall as players learn that contributors earn less in a given round, though a small set of players with strong mental-accounting or intrinsic pro-social preferences may persist. With an unknown total number of rounds, endgame effects are not anticipated within these ten rounds; rather we predict a gradual decline toward universal withdrawal, with a few persistent contributors.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game is a clear social dilemma. Each player privately chooses whether to withdraw all 20 coins (keep 20, contribute 0) or withdraw none (contribute 20, keep 0). The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players. So every coin a single player contributes yields 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 to each player's payoff — less than the 1 coin they give up. Rational self-interest therefore favors withdrawing all 20 every round. However, some players (by persona priors) have higher patience or mental-accounting tendencies and may be more inclined to contribute initially. Because decisions are private during each round but payoffs for all players are shown after the round, players quickly learn who contributed (contributors always end up 20 lower than non-contributors in the same round) and can update expectations. Without communication and without knowing when the game ends, we expect an initial minority of contributions from more pro-social or mentally-accounting players, followed by a gradual decline in cooperation as contributors see that free-riders earn 20 coins more and as conditional cooperators switch to withdrawing. A small set of steadfast cooperators may persist for several rounds, but by later rounds most or all players will be withdrawing all 20.

- Conditional cooperation and learning: players see a full summary at the end of each round (everyone’s net payoffs), so they can infer who contributed and adapt. That tends to produce some initial cooperation (from patient/mentally-accounting types) and then decline as other players learn defecting yields higher payoffs.
- Uncertain horizon: players do not know the total number of rounds. Without a known end, classic endgame unraveling is weakened, so some players may sustain cooperation longer than in a known finite game. Expect an early cluster of cooperators, a drift toward defection, and occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation by those predisposed to contribute.
- Heterogeneity by persona: some avatars have high patience and mental-accounting (more likely to contribute conditionally), others show high gain-seeking or low mental-accounting (more likely to defect). Given full-information summaries, contributions will trend down but may spike occasionally as small groups try to coordinate implicitly.

I will simulate a plausible unfolding over 10 rounds. Each round I state why I expect that pattern and then give the contributions and the round summary (each player’s net payoff as seen by all).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly peer punishment. Each round players simultaneously choose whether to withdraw the full 20 coins (contribute 0 to the public pot) or leave the full 20 in the pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and shared equally, so cooperation benefits the group but invites free-riding incentives for individuals. Punishment is possible after payoff realization: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target. Punishment is anonymous (players see aggregate punishment they received and used but not who punished them), so it can enforce cooperation via reputational signaling of resistance to freeloading, but it is costly and therefore used sparingly.

Given the players' priors (mental-accounting and other cues), I predict a mix initially — many players with high mental-accounting and loss sensitivity will start by contributing, while some with low mental-accounting or strong temptation to "gain" will defect early. Early rounds will see a handful of defectors; cooperators will respond with sparse, targeted punishments. Over repeated interaction (unknown horizon), persistent defectors get targeted a few times and many will switch to cooperation; punishment incidence should decline as cooperation becomes established. Because punishment is costly, it will typically be in small units (1–2 units) and not overly frequent.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits a strong material incentive to withdraw the full 20 coins (free-ride) against social enforcement via costly punishment. Because the shared pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split among all players, everyone gains from others' contributions, so a single-minded material optimizer prefers withdrawing 20 every round. That creates a classic tension: cooperators (who keep their coins in the pot) lower their private return but help raise the group return; defectors keep private coins and still receive the group share.

Likely dynamics: at first, we expect a mix of choices — some players follow cooperative norms or conditional strategies and contribute, others defect to capture the private 20. When defecting is visible in round summaries, a few cooperative players will use punishment sparingly to discourage persistent free-riding. Punishment is costly to impose, so it will only be applied selectively (targets who repeatedly defect or who are blatant outliers). Over rounds, punishments should shift behavior toward more cooperation for many players, but a few may remain stubborn or lapse occasionally. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, those who value long-term reciprocity or reputation will be inclined to sustain cooperation once it becomes established.

Most players maintain cooperation. One player (HORSE) lapses again, perhaps testing enforcement or reacting to private incentives; a couple of cooperators apply light punishment to deter further lapses. With cooperation stable, punishments are rare and small.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public-goods setting where each player either keeps the full 20 coins (contributes 0) or leaves the 20 in the shared pot (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split evenly among all 19 players, so each contributor increases everyone's share by the same fixed amount (230/19 ≈ 12.11) but by contributing they forgo the private 20. That creates a classic temptation: defecting yields an immediate private +20 while still enjoying the public return from others' contributions.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: some players with stronger pro-social priors or high mental-accounting endorsement (e.g., CHICKEN, CROCODILE, ELEPHANT, CHICK) are more likely to contribute initially. More loss-averse or low-mental-accounting players tend to withhold (defect) to secure the private 20.
- Social learning and payoff feedback: because contributors are immediately disadvantaged in any round with defectors (defectors get share+20 while cooperators get only the share), contributors who see themselves “exploited” are likely to reduce contributions in later rounds. Without communication and without knowing the total number of rounds, we expect cooperation to decline over time (a downward trend).
- Heterogeneity: a small core of persistent cooperators (those with high mental-accounting or strong patience) may continue contributing even as most abandon cooperation, producing a gradual decay rather than an immediate collapse.
- Uncertainty: players do not know the total number of rounds, so end-game rush is not predictable; some players may keep contributing hoping to sustain cooperation, while others opportunistically defect as they observe payoffs.

Predicted outcome: moderate cooperation at first, then steady decline as defectors benefit and contributors respond by withholding. By the final rounds a very small minority (one or two persistent cooperators) remain contributing.

A single player's contribution of 20 raises each player's share by 20*11.5/19 ≈ 12.1, so contributing is socially efficient but personally costly (you forgo 20 to get ≈12.1 back). Because choices are simultaneous and rounds are of unknown total length, we expect a mix of conditional cooperation and opportunistic free-riding. Punishment is available: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target, a deterrent but itself costly — so punishment is typically used sparingly by a few players who value norm enforcement.

Given the player personas, I predict:
- Many patient, risk-averse or high mental-accounting players initially cooperate; some low-patience or opportunistic players defect.
- Early punishments target obvious defectors and are small (1 unit) because punishment is costly and participants cannot single out punishers later.
- Punishment raises cooperation in subsequent rounds; cooperation spikes after punished defections, then occasional opportunistic lapses occur.
- Overall pattern: moderate-to-high cooperation most rounds, sparse, low-intensity punishment events concentrated on repeat or obvious defectors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with 19 players each round. Each round starts with 20 coins in the shared pot for each player; each player must choose whether to withdraw all 20 for private use (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the pot (contribute 20). The pot of total contributions is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players.

Key strategic features and likely dynamics:
- Individual incentive to withdraw (defect): holding out 20 coins privately always gives the withdrawing player an extra 20 relative to a cooperator, while lowering total public return by 20. Because the multiplier is split across many players, the private gain from defecting exceeds the private benefit from contributing in almost all simple comparisons; this creates a strong one-shot incentive to withdraw.
- Social optimum vs. Nash equilibrium: everyone contributing yields much higher group payoff, but unilateral deviation is profitable, so the unique dominant-strategy equilibrium is that every player withdraws every round.
- Repetition and feedback: players do not know how many rounds remain, but they do see a round-by-round summary of payoffs (their own and peers’). This feedback allows learning: early cooperation can appear if some players are pro-social or optimistic, but observing higher payoffs for withdrawers typically triggers defection in subsequent rounds.
- Heterogeneous priors (personas): some players (high patience, high mental-accounting, high loss-aversion) may be more likely to attempt contribution initially; others (high gain-seeking, low patience) are more likely to withdraw immediately. With no communication or punishment, any early cooperation is fragile and likely to decay across rounds as free-riding is observed.
- Expectation across 10 rounds: likely a moderate number of initial contributors (some experimenters test cooperation), followed by learning and a steady decline in contributors, converging to all-withdrawal by middle-to-late rounds.

Below I simulate a plausible transcript from round 1 through round 10, using the provided player personas as priors on who starts by contributing and who switches to withdrawing as they observe payoffs.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game creates a strong collective incentive (the shared pot is multiplied by 11.5) but a weaker private incentive to contribute: each coin you leave in the pot returns only 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 coins to you, so contributing is socially efficient but individually dominated. With 19 players and binary choices (contribute all 20 or withdraw all 20), the static best response is to withdraw (defect). However, some players have stable prosocial or “mental-accounting” tendencies and will sometimes contribute despite the individual loss; others respond quickly to the observed payoff advantage of defectors and switch.

Uncertainties that shape play here:
- Players do not know the total number of rounds, so endgame defection is less certain; that can sustain cooperation early on but also encourages cautious, conditional strategies.
- Choices are simultaneous and private within each round, but after each round everyone sees the realized payoffs for every player. That permits conditional behavior across rounds: many players will mimic what seemed to work in recent rounds (i.e., defect if defectors got higher payoffs).
- Personas provide priors about who is likely to be a persistent cooperator (players with high mental-accounting or consistently high loss-aversion/patience) versus who will favor selfish play. Expect initial mixed cooperation (some contributing) followed by a drift toward defection as the payoff advantage of withdrawing becomes visible and contagion spreads; a handful of “stubborn” cooperators will often keep contributing.

- Players do not know the total number of rounds, so endgame defection is less certain; that can sustain cooperation early on but also encourages cautious, conditional strategies.
- Choices are simultaneous and private within each round, but after each round everyone sees the realized payoffs for every player. That permits conditional behavior across rounds: many players will mimic what seemed to work in recent rounds (i.e., defect if defectors got higher payoffs).
- Personas provide priors about who is likely to be a persistent cooperator (players with high mental-accounting or consistently high loss-aversion/patience) versus who will favor selfish play. Expect initial mixed cooperation (some contributing) followed by a drift toward defection as the payoff advantage of withdrawing becomes visible and contagion spreads; a handful of “stubborn” cooperators will often keep contributing.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Round 1 is the first simultaneous decision with no history. Some players with high mental-accounting or strong social/avoidance tendencies will lean to contribute; others will withdraw. Because contributions are private and defecting yields a clear round payoff advantage when others contribute, we expect a moderate number of contributions but also several withdrawals. Players who anticipate reciprocity or simply prefer to keep money labeled for the group will be the initial contributors.

Players saw in Round 1 that defectors earned 20 coins more than contributors (141.05 vs. 121.05). Many conditional cooperators will switch to withdrawing to capture that advantage; a core of more strongly prosocial or high-mental-accounting players likely remains contributing. Expect fewer contributors than Round 1.

Round 2 reinforced that defecting yields a steady premium (≈ +20) over contributing. Conditional cooperators who switched in Round 2 will likely stay withdrawn; only a smaller set of committed cooperators persists. Expect contributions to fall again.

The payoff gap remains: defectors keep a comfortable advantage. Many of the remaining occasional cooperators will stop contributing; a small core of players with stronger norms or accounting habits may still contribute this round.

By now the signal is clear: contributing is individually costly given how many withdraw. Only very committed cooperators (by preference or habit) are likely to continue. Expect only a couple of contributors.

Contributors are now likely down to very few stubborn players. Observing the persistent advantage of withdrawal, many players will permanently defect; a lone cooperator may remain trying to sustain the public good.

Seeing that a lone cooperator barely helps (and still loses relative to withdrawing), remaining conditional cooperators will almost surely stop contributing. At this point, the dominant pattern is universal withdrawal: stable, individually rational, and reinforced by the private-payoff signals.

After one full round of universal withdrawal, players have strong evidence that contributing is exploited in this population under anonymity and without credible punishment. Expect continued universal withdrawal.
No new incentives have appeared to revive cooperation: anonymity, lack of communication, and the visible advantage of withdrawal keep everyone defecting. Expect continued all-withdrawal.

Absent coordination, punishment, or changing payoffs, the dominant-all-defect outcome remains stable through Round 10. Some players might nostalgically try contributing, but observed history makes that unlikely.

game_explanation:
This is a standard voluntary-contribution public goods game with a strong individual incentive to withdraw (free-ride) and a clear group-level benefit if many leave the 20 coins in the shared pot. Each player chooses either to withdraw the whole 20 (contribute 0) or leave it (contribute 20). Any contributor reduces their private balance by 20 relative to a withdrawer but increases the public pot for everyone: the pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among the 19 active players, so each contributor increases everyone’s share by about 12.11. Thus (a) defection (withdraw) strictly dominates contribution for the individual in that round (withdrawers always get 20 more than contributors), and (b) the group's total payoff is higher the more players contribute (social optimum is everyone contributes).

Given these incentives and the provided player priors, I predict an initial mix of contributions: several players with strong "mental accounting" or loss-averse/consistent pro-social tendencies will contribute early (they care about group outcomes or treat money as earmarked), while many others will withdraw. Because players see each other's round net payoffs at the summary stage, they can deduce who contributed and who withdrew; this visibility enables conditional strategies (conditional cooperators may maintain contributions if many others did, but will cut back if exploited). Over repeated rounds (unknown horizon), I expect an overall downward drift in contributions as defectors get systematically higher payoffs and some conditional cooperators switch to withdrawing — leaving a small persistent core of contributors with high mental-accounting or strong cooperation priors.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start with no history. Some avatars have strong priors toward treating money as earmarked or being cooperative (e.g., PARROT, OWL, CHICK, COW, PINGUIN, ELEPHANT, HORSE, MOOSE, MONKEY), so a large initial cluster of those players are likely to try contributing. Risk/greed-prone and low mental-accounting players are likelier to withdraw. Because no one has yet been exploited, contribution rates are relatively high in Round 1.

Round 1 showed contributors earned the same public return but were 20 coins worse off than withdrawers, an obvious exploitation signal. Some conditional cooperators (especially those who saw many contributors) may still try contributing, but a number of players who were exploited switch to withdrawing. The core pro-social types (PARROT, OWL, CHICK, COW, PINGUIN, ELEPHANT, HORSE, MOOSE, MONKEY) largely stay contributing; a few others revert to withdraw.

Observing that withdrawers continue to enjoy a clear +20 advantage, a few conditional cooperators reduce contributions further. Persistent cooperators with high mental-accounting or strong loss-averse signals remain, while some marginal contributors step away.

After a few rounds of seeing withdrawers reap higher payoffs, cooperation continues to decline. Only those with stronger priors toward cooperation (mental-accounting, loss aversion, or identity-based motives) keep contributing. A smaller committed core remains.

The downward trend continues. Some earlier cooperators have switched out after repeated exploitation. A very small group of consistently pro-social players (those with the strongest mental-accounting or cooperative priors) still contribute, but overall group-level returns fall as contributions shrink.

Some players briefly return to contributing (possible experimenter effects, mistaken beliefs, or attempts to re-establish cooperation), but the incentive to withdraw remains strong. Expect a small uptick or stabilization from the committed core rather than a full recovery.

By now many players have observed repeatedly that withdrawers keep a stable advantage, so cooperation compresses to a very small, persistent set of contributors. The core contributors tend to be those with the strongest cooperative priors (high mental-accounting, consistent preferences). Expect further decline compared to early rounds.

Contributions continue to be sparse. A tiny handful stay contributing out of principled consistency; most players withdraw every round because it is the individually dominant option and the repeated visibility of payoffs confirms the pattern.

No new incentives to resume wide cooperation have appeared; the pattern from Round 8 persists. A very small stable core (those with the strongest cooperative priors) continues contributing by the end of the game.

By the final round here (players still don’t know the total number of rounds while playing), most conditional cooperators have long since defected; only the smallest core of principled contributors remains. Expect very low contribution rates compared to Round 1.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public-goods game with 19 players: each round each player either leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them privately (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally, so a single cooperator raises everyone’s redistribution share by a modest amount (~12.105 per cooperator). Defection gives an immediate private gain (you keep 20) but reduces the public return. After seeing who contributed, players can punish others at a private cost (2 per punishment unit) that inflicts a larger loss on the target (3 per unit).

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds usually show substantial cooperation because many players have priors or norms that favor contributing (high mental-accounting or patience). However, the temptation to free-ride is strong: a defector gets the private 20 plus the redistributed benefit from others’ contributions.
- Punishment is the only enforcement mechanism. Because punishment is costly to the punisher, it tends to be used sparingly and targeted at persistent defectors rather than occasional free-riders. We'd expect a few players to punish repeat defectors to enforce contributions.
- Uncertainty: players do not know the number of rounds, so end-game defection is possible. Also punishment is anonymous (players don’t see who punished them), which reduces direct retaliation but still allows conditional strategies (punish defectors broadly).
- Over ten rounds we expect an initial mix of cooperation and defection, with cooperators using modest, targeted punishment to drive down defection. Persistent defectors may get punished more heavily; as rounds progress, overall cooperation typically rises (or defectors remain isolated and punished).

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round often has substantial cooperation but several players with low mental-accounting or strong risk/gain preferences may free-ride. Punishment will be light and primarily symbolic — a few cooperators penalize obvious defectors to signal norm enforcement.
Some defectors respond to punishment and switch to cooperating; a couple of earlier defectors remain stubborn. Punishment remains sparse and targeted at the persistent noncontributors.
A few players revert to defecting (some are driven by high gain-seeking or low patience). Cooperators respond by punishing the now-noticeable defectors; punishments are small but more distributed.
Punishment of persistent defectors makes continued defection more costly; some give up free-riding. Punishment continues but remains limited to a few enforcers.
As punishments accumulate, one or two players remain stubborn. Some cooperators apply slightly stronger penalties to signal that persistent defection will be costly.
Sustained pressure results in most defectors switching back to contribution; one player remains a lone free-rider. Punishment is minimal and focused.
If a single player continues to defect despite costs, a few cooperators may coordinate to impose slightly stronger penalties. Still, punishments remain limited because they’re personally costly.
Persistent punishment and the visibility of consequences push the last defector to switch back to contributing. With near-universal cooperation, punishment drops to zero.
High cooperation is now the norm. There is little reason to punish, and punishments cease entirely.
With sustained cooperation and no recent defections, the group continues cooperating and no punishments are issued. Players finish the unknown-length game in a high-cooperation state.

game_explanation:
This is a classic public-goods game with a strong private incentive to withdraw the full 20 coins (i.e., contribute 0) because each coin you keep yields a higher private return than the per-person share you would get back from contributing. The shared pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split across 19 players, so a lone contributor loses on net relative to defecting. Because players do not know the total number of rounds and cannot see others' choices before deciding, early rounds are noisy: some players follow cooperative priors (high patience / mental-accounting) and contribute, while others defect.

Punishment gives a mechanism to enforce cooperation: players can spend to reduce others' payoffs (2 cost per unit to sender, 3 deduction to target). Punishment is costly, so it is used sparingly in real experiments—usually focused on persistent or obvious free-riders. Over repeated rounds we typically see:
- Early mixed cooperation as people test each other's behavior.
- Targeted, low-level punishment of clear defectors.
- Some defectors switch to contributing after being punished (conditional cooperation).
- Cooperation tends to rise if punishment deters free-riding; but punishers pay costs, and some players may occasionally "slip" back to defecting, prompting occasional further punishment.
Uncertainty remains: players cannot trace who punished them, and only see round payoff summaries (so they infer contributions indirectly). Given the provided personas (some consistently patient/cooperative, some impatient/risk-seeking, some loss-sensitive), I predict an initial mix of cooperators and defectors, moderate punishment early, and a trend toward higher cooperation by mid-game, with sporadic backsliding and sparse punishment throughout.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start without history. Those with higher patience and pro-social priors are likely to contribute 20; more self-interested or impatient players will withdraw 20 (contribute 0). Some contributors will try punishment against obvious defectors to signal that free-riding will be costly, but punishments will be small and targeted because punishment itself is costly.

Players see payoffs and deductions from Round 1 and infer who likely defected. Some previously punished defectors may experiment with cooperation; many original cooperators remain cooperative. Punishment remains targeted at serial or obvious defectors.

Repeated (though small) punishment of some defectors makes contributing more attractive for a subset; a few previously non-contributing players decide to contribute. Remaining defectors are increasingly singled out for low-cost punishment.
With cooperation now common, defectors are few. Those remaining may be targeted with occasional punishment to encourage switching; punishers remain cautious because of the cost.

The group is close to full cooperation; persistent non-cooperators (few remain) receive stronger signals in the form of slightly larger or multiple small punishments. Most players keep cooperating to sustain the high group return.

After concentrated punishment, the previously non-cooperating player is likely to switch. However, occasional temptation causes others to test free-riding (small backsliding); these slips are met with punishment but remain sparse.
Punished players usually revert to cooperation; occasional new defectors may appear as some players test the stability of cooperation. Peers punish these sporadic defectors lightly to restore norms.
Punishment has largely stabilized behavior; by now most players expect cooperation to be reciprocated, so full cooperation is plausible. With no obvious defectors, there is little reason to punish.

Even in generally cooperative groups, temporary temptation can cause an individual to defect one round (a test). Other players typically respond with a small punitive signal to deter future defections.

Seeing targeted punishments for slips and the overall cooperative environment, the occasional defector usually returns to contributing. In many experiments the later rounds show a re-convergence toward cooperation.
